59603.explanation: {tex}({e^x} + {e^{ - x}})dy = ({e^x} - {e^{ - x}})dx{/tex} {tex}\int {dy} = \int {\frac{{({e^x} - {e^{ - x}})}}{{({e^x} + {e^{ - x}})}}} dx {/tex} Since {tex}\int {\frac{{f'(x)}}{{f(x)}}} dx = \ ln f(x) +c{/tex} {tex}y = \log \left| {({e^x} + {e^{ - x}})} \right| + C{/tex}

59605.explanation: {tex}y\log y \ dx = x \ dy{/tex} {tex}\int {\frac{1}{x}} dx = \int {\frac{1}{{y\log y}}} dy{/tex} {tex} \log \left| x \right| = \log \left| {\log y} \right| + \log C {/tex} Since {tex}\int \frac {{f'(x)dx}}{f(x)} = log |f(x)|+c{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{1}}{C} =c \ a \ new \ constant{/tex} {tex}\\ \log x = \log (C\log y) {/tex} {tex}x = C\log y {/tex} {tex} \log y = \frac{1}{C}x {/tex} {tex}\log y = cx {/tex} {tex}y = {e^{cx}}{/tex}

59606.explanation: {tex}\frac{dx}{dy}+\frac{x}{y}=y^{2} {/tex} Divide by ydy {tex}xe^{\int \frac{dy}{y}}=\int y^{2} \ e^{\int \frac{dy}{y}}dy+c {/tex} Since it is in linear differential equation in y, {tex}x e^{\int P dy } = \int Q(y)e^{\int P dy }dy+c {/tex} is a solution for {tex} \frac{dx}{dy}+Py=Q {/tex} {tex}xy=\int y^{3} dy + c \because e^{{logy}}=y{/tex} {tex}xy=\frac{y^{4}}{4}+c {/tex}

59607.explanation: {tex}dy=sin^{-1}x dx{/tex} {tex}\\\int dy=\int sin^{-1}x dx{/tex} {tex}y=sin^{-1}x\int dx-\int \int dx . \frac{\mathrm{d} }{\mathrm{d} x}sin^{-1}xdx+c{/tex} {tex}y=xsin^{-1}x-\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}dx{/tex} {tex}y=xsin^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\int \frac{2x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}dx {/tex} {tex}y=xsin^{-1}x+\frac{1}{2} 2{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}+c{/tex} {tex}y=xsin^{-1}x+{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}+c {/tex}

59608.explanation: {tex}{e^{x} }{}tanydx=(e^{x}-1 )sec^{2}y dy{/tex} {tex} \int\frac{e^{x}dx }{e^{x}-1}= \int \frac{sec^{2}y dy}{tany}{/tex} {tex}\log|e^{x}-1|+logc =log|tany|{/tex} {tex}\\log|c(e^{x}-1)| =log|tany|{/tex} {tex}\\c(e^{x}-1)=tany{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

59609.explanation: {tex}x(x^{2}-1)dx=dy{/tex} {tex}\int (x^{3}-x) dx=\int dy{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^{4}}{4}-\frac{x^{2}}{2}=y+c{/tex} Here y = 0 when x = 2 {tex}\frac{2^{4}}{4}-\frac{2^{2}}{2}=0+c{/tex} {tex}4-2=c{/tex} {tex}\therefore c = 2{/tex} Therefore,the required solution is {tex}\frac{x^{4}}{4}-\frac{x^{2}}{2}=y+2\\{/tex}

59610.explanation: {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=cos^{-1}a {/tex} {tex} \int dy=cos^{-1}a\int dx {/tex} {tex} y=xcos^{-1}a+c{/tex} When y = 1 , x = 0 , then 1=0 {tex}cos^{-1}a+c{/tex} {tex}c=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=xcos^{-1}a+1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{y-1}{x}=cos^{-1}a{/tex}

59611.explanation: {tex}\frac{dy}{y}=tanx dx{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{dy}{y}=\int tanx dx{/tex} {tex}\\log|y|=log|secx|+logc{/tex} {tex}log|y|=log|csecx|{/tex} {tex}y=csecx{/tex} here y=1 and x=0 gives {tex}1=c sec 0{/tex} hence c = 1 {tex}\therefore y=secx{/tex}

59612.explanation: {tex}\frac{ydy}{y+2}=\frac{(x+2)dx}{x} {/tex} {tex}\int \frac{ydy}{y+2}=\int \frac{(x+2)dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{y+2-2dy}{y+2}=\int \frac{(x+2)dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\int dy-\int \frac{2}{y+2}=\int dx+\int \frac{2}{x}{/tex} {tex}y-2log|y+2|=x+2log|x|+c{/tex} Here x=1 and y=-1 implies {tex}-1-2log|-1+2|=1+2log|1|+c\implies-1-2log|1|=1+c \because log|1|=0\implies\therefore c=-2{/tex} Hence, {tex}y-2log|y+2|=x+2log|x|-2{/tex} {tex}y-x+2=2log|x|+2log|y+2|{/tex} {tex}y-x+2=2log|x(y+2)|{/tex} {tex}\\y-x+2=log|x^{2}(y+2)^{2}|{/tex}

59613.explanation: {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=e^{3x}e^{4y}{/tex} {tex}\int {e^{-4y}}dy=\int e^{3x}dx{/tex} {tex}\frac{-e^{-4y}}{4}=\frac{e^{3x}}{3}+c{/tex} here x = y =0 gives {tex}\\\frac{-1}{4}=\frac{1}{3}+c{/tex} {tex}\therefore c=\frac{-7}{12}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{-e^{-4y}}{4}=\frac{e^{3x}}{3}-\frac{7}{12}{/tex} {tex}4e^{3x}+3e^{-4y}-7=0{/tex}

59614.explanation: Let P be the principal at any time t. then, {tex}\frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{{rP}}{{100}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{P}{{100}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int {\frac{1}{P}dP = \int {\frac{r}{{100}}dt} } {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log P = \frac{r}{{100}}t + \log c{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \frac{P}{c} = \frac{r}{{100}}t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P = c{e^{\frac{r}{{100}}}}{/tex} When P = 100 and t = 0., then, c = 100, Thus, we have: {tex}\Rightarrow P=100 \ {{e}^{{}^{r}\!\!\diagup\!\!{}_{100}\;}}{/tex} Now, suppose t = T, when P = 100., then; {tex}\Rightarrow 200 = 100{e^{\frac{T}{{100}}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {e^{\frac{T}{{100}}}} = 2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow T = 100\log 2{/tex} = 100(0.6931) = 6.93%. Which is the required solution

59615.explanation: Here P is the principal at time t {tex}\frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{{5P}}{{100}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{P}{{20}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int_{}^{} {\frac{1}{P}dP = \int_{}^{} {\frac{1}{{20}}dt} } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log P = \frac{1}{{20}}t + \log c{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \frac{P}{c} = \frac{1}{{20}}t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P =c {e^{\frac{1}{{100}}}}{/tex} When P = 1000 and t = 0 ., then , c = 1000, therefore, we have : {tex} \Rightarrow P = 1000{e^{\frac{T}{{100}}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A = 1000{e^{\frac{5}{{10}}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {e^{\frac{5}{{10}}}} = A{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow A = 1000\log 0.5{/tex} = 1000(1.648) = 1648

59617.explanation: {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{y-x}{x+y}{/tex} it is a homogenous equation Hence we put y=vx {tex}x = {-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac} \over 2a}let \ y=vx{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{\mathrm{dv} }{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} {tex}v+x\frac{\mathrm{dv} }{\mathrm{d} x}=\frac{vx-x}{vx+x}{/tex} {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{v-1}{v+1}-v{/tex} {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{v-1-v^{2}-v}{v+1}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{(v+1) dv}{1+v^{2}}=-\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{vdv}{1+v^{2}}+\int \frac{dv}{1+v^{2}}=-\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}log|1+v^{2}|+tan^{-1}v=-logx+c{/tex} {tex}log|1+v^{2}|+2tan^{-1}v+2logx=c {/tex} Resubstituting v=y/x we get {tex}log|\frac{x^2+y^2}{x^2}|+2tan^{-1}\frac{y}{x}+2logx=c{/tex} When x=y=1 we get, {tex}log|\frac{1^2+1^2}{1^2}|+2tan^{-1}\frac{1}{1}+log1=c{/tex} {tex}\\c=log2+\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}log|\frac{x^2+y^2}{x^2}|+2tan^{-1}\frac{y}{x}+2logx=log2+\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\\log|{x^2+y^2}|+2tan^{-1}\frac{y}{x}=log2+\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

59618.explanation: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + (\frac{1}{x} + \cot x)y = 1 \Rightarrow P = \left( {\frac{1}{x} + \cot x} \right),Q = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I.F. = {e^{\int {\left( {\frac{1}{x} + \cot x} \right)} dx}} = {e^{\log x + \log \sin x}} = {e^{\log (x\sin x)}} = xsinx{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y(x\sin x) = \int_{}^{} {1.x\sin x \Rightarrow } xy\sin x = - x\cos x + \sin x + c{/tex} {tex}xysinx=-xcosx+sinx+c {/tex} Dividing by xsinx, we get {tex} y=-cotx+\frac{1}{x}+\frac{c}{xsinx}{/tex} It is a linear differential equation in y in the form of {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}+Py=Q{/tex} hence solution is {tex}y.IF=\int IF\ \ Q(x)dx+c{/tex}

59628.explanation: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {e^x}\sin x {/tex} {tex}\int {dy = \int {{e^x}} } \sin xdx {/tex} {tex} y = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\sin x - \cos x} \right){e^x} + C{/tex} When x = y = 0 , we get {tex}0 = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\sin 0 - \cos 0} \right){e^0} + C{/tex} {tex}\\C=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}y = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\sin x - \cos x} \right){e^x} + \frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex}2y-1=\left( {\sin x - \cos x} \right){e^x} {/tex}

59629.explanation: Given that {tex} y\frac{dy}{dx}=x {/tex} {tex}ydy=xdx{/tex} {tex}\int ydy=\int xdx{/tex} {tex}\\\frac{y^{2}}{2}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+c{/tex} When x = 0 and y = 2, we get {tex}\frac{-2^{2}}{2}=\frac{0^{2}}{2}+c{/tex} c = 2 {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{2}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+2{/tex} {tex}y^{2}-x^{2}=4{/tex}

59630.explanation: Given that {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=2\frac{y+3}{x+4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{y+3}=\frac{2dx}{x+4}{/tex} {tex}\\\int \frac{dy}{y+3}=\int \frac{2dx}{x+4}{/tex} {tex}\\log|y+3|=2log|x+4|+logc{/tex} Here, x= -2 and y = 1 {tex}log|1+3|=2log|-2+4|+logc{/tex} {tex}log4=log4+logc{/tex} {tex}logc=0{/tex} {tex}\log|y+3|=2log|x+4|{/tex} {tex}y+3=(x+4)^{2}{/tex}

59631.explanation: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} + y}}{x}{/tex} Put y=vx, we have ; {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \sqrt {1 + {v^2}} + v \Rightarrow \int_{}^{} {\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 + {v^2}} }}dv} = \int {\frac{1}{x}dx} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log |\sqrt {1 + {v^2}} + v| = \log |x| + \log C \Rightarrow |\sqrt {1 + {v^2}} + v| = Cx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {(y + \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} )^2} = {C^2}{x^4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {(y + \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} )} = {C}{x^2}{/tex}

59632.explanation: y’ = h(x)g(y) since we can segregate functions of y with dy and x with dx. {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=h(x)g(y){/tex} and {tex}\frac{dy}{g(y)}=h(x)dx{/tex}

59633.explanation: {tex}\left\{ {x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + y\sin \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)} \right\}ydx = \left\{ {y\sin \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) - x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)} \right\}xdy{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\left\{ {x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + y\sin \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)} \right\}}}{{x\left\{ {y\sin \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) - x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)} \right\}}}{/tex} Putting y = vx, {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=v\frac{xcosv+vsinx}{vxsinv-xcosv}{/tex} {tex}\\x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{vcosv+v^{2}sinx}{vsinv-cosv}-v{/tex} {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{vcosv+v^{2}sinx-v^{2}sinv+vcosv}{vsinv-cosv}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{(vsinv-cosv)dv}{vcosv}=\int \frac{2dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}-log|vcosv|=2logx+logc{/tex} {tex}log|(vcosv)x^{2}c|=0{/tex} {tex}(vcosv)x^{2}c=1{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}cos(\frac{y}{x})x^{2}=c'{/tex} {tex}xycos(\frac{y}{x})=c \because c'=c{/tex}

59634.explanation: A differential equation of the form y' = F(x,y) is homogeneous if F(x,y) is a homogeneous function of degree zero , so that we can convert it into variable separable form by y=vx.

59635.explanation: {tex}({x^2} - {y^2})dx + 2xydy = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{y^3} - {x^3}}}{{2xy}}{/tex} Put y = vx, we have; {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{y^2} - {x^2}}}{{2xy}} \Rightarrow v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{v^2}{x^2} - {x^2}}}{{2xvx}} \Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = - \left( {\frac{{{v^2} + 1}}{{2v}}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int_{}^{} {\frac{{2v}}{{{v^2} + 1}}dv} = - \int_{}^{} {\frac{1}{x}dx \Rightarrow \log |{v^2} + 1| = - \log |x| + \log C} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log |{v^2} + 1| + \log |x| = \log C \Rightarrow ({v^2} + 1)|x| = C \Rightarrow ({x^2} + {y^2}) = Cx{/tex}

59636.explanation: {tex}{{x}^{2}}dy+(xy+{{y}^{2}})dx=0\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}=-\left( \frac{xy+{{y}^{2}}}{{{x}^{2}}} \right){/tex} Put {tex}y = vx \ and \ \frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = - (v + {v^2}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x\frac{dv}{dx}=-2v-v^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow xdv=-(v^2+2v)dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int{\frac{1}{v^2+2v}}dv=-\int{\frac{1}{x}}dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int{\frac{1}{(v+1)^2-1^2}}dv=-\int{\frac{1}{x}}dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\log \left| {\frac{v}{{v + 2}}} \right| = - \log x + \log c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\log \left| {\frac{v}{{v + 2}}} \right| +logx^2= - \log k {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\log \left| {\frac{vx^2}{{v + 2}}} \right| = \log k {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left| {\frac{{v{x^2}}}{{v + 2}}} \right| = k \Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2}y}}{{y + 2x}} = k{/tex} When x = 1, and y = 1, k = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{x}^{2}}y}{y+2x}=\frac{1}{3}\Rightarrow 3{{x}^{2}}y=y+2x{/tex}

59637.explanation: Let y = vx {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} Question becomes {tex} \\ v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=v-cosecv{/tex} {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=-cosecv{/tex} {tex}- \int sinv dv=\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\\cosv=logx+logc{/tex} {tex}cos(\frac{y}{x})=logx+logc{/tex} when x = 1 and y = 0 {tex}cos(\frac{0}{1})=log1+logc{/tex} { log c =1 } {tex}\\c=e{/tex} {tex}\\cos(\frac{y}{x})=logx+loge{/tex} {tex}cos(\frac{y}{x})=log|ex|{/tex}

59638.explanation: Let y = vx {tex} \frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} Question becomes {tex}v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{2v+v^2}{2}{/tex} {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{2v+v^2}{2}-v{/tex} {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{2v+v^2-2v}{2}{/tex} {tex}2\int \frac{dv}{v^2}=\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{-2}{v}=logx+c{/tex} When x=1 y=2 we get {tex}\frac{-2x}{y}=logx+c{/tex} {tex}\frac{-2}{2}=log1+c\implies c=-1{/tex} {tex}\frac{-2x}{y}=logx-1{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{2x}{1-log|x|}{/tex}

59639.explanation: A homogeneous equation of the form {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = h\left( {\frac{x}{y}} \right){/tex} can be solved by making the substitution x= vy.so that it becomes variable separable form and integration is then possible

59640.explanation: it is a homogeneous differential equation ,because the degree of each individual term is same i.e. 2.

59641.explanation: Here the degree and order of the equation is 1 and also is of the form {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q\;{/tex} hence it is linear differential equation in first order

59643.explanation: Converting the above equation in Linear differential equation we get {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=1+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}+\frac{-1}{x}y=1{/tex} {tex}ye^{\int\frac{-dx}{x} }=\int e^{\int\frac{-dx}{x}}dx+c{/tex} {tex}y\frac{1}{x}=\int\frac{1}{x}dx+c{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}=log|x|+c{/tex} {tex}y=xlog|x|+cx{/tex}

59646.explanation: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{x + y}}{{x - y}}{/tex} Put y = vx and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{x + y}}{{x - y}} \Rightarrow v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{1 + v}}{{1 - v}} \Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{1 + {v^2}}}{{1 - v}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int_{}^{} {\frac{{1 - v}}{{1 + {v^2}}}dv = \int_{}^{} {\frac{1}{x}dx} \Rightarrow \int_{}^{} {\frac{1}{{1 + {v^2}}}dv - \frac{1}{2}\int_{}^{} {\frac{{2v}}{{1 + {v^2}}}dv = \int_{}^{} {\frac{1}{x}dx} } } } {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}v - \frac{1}{2}\log (1 + {v^2}) = \log x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\log ({x^2} + {y^2}) + C{/tex}

59647.explanation: {tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 2y = \sin x \Rightarrow P = 2,Q = \sin x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I.F. = {e^{\int {2dx} }} = {e^{2x}} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y{e^{2x}} = \int {\sin x.{e^{2x}}dx \Rightarrow } y{e^{2x}} {/tex} {tex}= {e^{2x}}\frac{1}{5}\left( {2\sin x - \cos x} \right) + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{1}{5}\left( {2\sin x - \cos x} \right) + C{e^{ - 2x}} {/tex}

59648.explanation: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 3y = {e^{ - 2x}} \Rightarrow P = 3,Q = {e^{ - 2x}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I.F. = {e^{\int_{}^{} {3.dx} }} = {e^{3x}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y.{e^{3x}} = \int_{}^{} {{e^{ - 2x}}{e^{3x}}dx} \Rightarrow y.{e^{3x}} = \int_{}^{} {{e^x}dx \Rightarrow y.{e^{3x}} = {e^x} + C} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = {e^{ - 2x}} + C{e^{ - 3x}}{/tex}

59649.explanation: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{y}{x} = {x^2} \\ \ Here \ P = \frac{1}{x},Q = {x^2} \\ \Rightarrow I.F. = {e^{\int {P.dx} }} = {e^{\int {\frac{1}{x}.dx} }} \\ = {e^{\log \left| x \right|}} = x \\ \Rightarrow y.x = \int {x.{x^2}} dx \Rightarrow xy \\ = \frac{{{x^4}}}{4} + C \\ {/tex}

59650.explanation: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \sec x.y = \tan x \Rightarrow P = \sec x,Q = \tan x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I.F = {e^{\int_{}^{} {\sec xdx} }} = {e^{\log |\sec x + \tan x|}} = \log |\sec x + \tan x|{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y.\log |\sec x + \tan x| = \int_{}^{} {\tan x.\log |\sec x + \tan x|dx} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y.\log |\sec x + \tan x| = \sec x + \tan x - x + C{/tex}

59651.explanation: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + {\sec ^2}x.y = \tan x.{\sec ^2}x \Rightarrow P = {\sec ^2}x,Q = \tan x.{\sec ^2}x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I.F. = {e^{\int_{}^{} {{{\sec }^2}xdx} }} = {e^{\tan x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y.{e^{\tan x}} = \int_{}^{} {\tan x{{\sec }^2}x{e^{\tan x}}dx \Rightarrow y.{e^{\tan x}} = (\tan x - 1){e^{\tan x}} + C} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = (\tan x - 1) + C{e^{ - \tan x}}{/tex}

59653.explanation: {tex}xlogx\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + y = \frac{2}{x}logx\;{/tex} Dividing by xlogx we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{1}{{xlogx}}y = \frac{2}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} it is a linear diff equation we get, {tex}y{e^{\int {\frac{{dx}}{{xlogx}}} }} = \int {\frac{2}{{{x^2}}}} {e^{\int {\frac{{dx}}{{xlogx}}} }}dx + c{/tex} {tex}ylogx = \int {\frac{2}{{{x^2}}}} logxdx + c{/tex} {tex}ylogx = 2[\frac{{ - logx}}{x} - \frac{1}{x}] + c{/tex} {tex}ylogx = - \frac{2}{x}(1 + logx) + c{/tex} Since {tex}\int {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}}} logxdx = logx\int {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}}} dx - \int {\int {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}}} } dx\frac{{\text{d}}}{{{\text{d}}x}}logxdx{/tex} {tex}\int {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}}} logxdx = \frac{{ - 1}}{x}logx - \int {\frac{{ - 1}}{x}} \frac{1}{x}dx + c{/tex} {tex}\int {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}}} logxdx = \frac{{ - 1}}{x}logx - \frac{1}{x} + c{/tex}

59654.explanation: {tex}(1+x^2)dy=(cotx-2xy)dx{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{cotx-2xy}{1+x^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}+\frac{2x}{1+x^2}y=\frac{cotx}{1+x^2}{/tex} It is a linear differential equation in y. Therefore, Solution is {tex}\\ye^{\int \frac{2xdx}{1+x^2}}=\int \frac{cotx}{1+x^2}e^{\int \frac{2xdx}{1+x^2}}dx+c{/tex} {tex}y(1+x^2)=\int \frac{cotx}{1+x^2}(1+x^2)dx+c{/tex} {tex}y(1+x^2)=\int cotxdx+c{/tex} {tex}y(1+x^2)=log|sinx|+c{/tex}

59655.explanation: A vector has both magnitude as well as direction.

59656.explanation: Position vectors of any point in space are usually calculated from the origin, so we write {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} ,\;\overrightarrow {OB} ,\overrightarrow {OC} {/tex} for three points A ,B,C in space to represent the origin as the initial point.

59657.explanation: the angles {tex}\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma {/tex} are called direction angles, which the position vector {tex}\overrightarrow r {/tex} makes with the positive x-axis ,y-axis and z-axis respectively.

59658.explanation: Cosines of the angles {tex}\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma {/tex} are called direction cosines.

59659.explanation: If l, m, and n are the direction cosines of vector {tex}\overrightarrow r {/tex} denoted by {tex}\overrightarrow {OP} {/tex} , then, the coordinates of point P are given by: lr, mr and nr respectively.

59660.explanation: A vector whose initial and terminal points coincides is called Zero Vector. The magnitude of Zero Vector is also Zero.

59661.explanation: The vector whose magnitude is always 1 or unity is called a Unit Vector.

59662.explanation: {tex}\widehat a = \frac{{\overrightarrow a }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|}}{/tex} , is called the unit vector of a given vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} in the direction of {tex}\vec a{/tex}

59663.explanation: Two vectors whose initial point is same are called co- initial vectors.

59664.explanation: Two vectors A and B are said to be collinear , if they are parallel to the same line irrespective of their magnitudes and directions.

59665.explanation: Two vectors having same magnitude as well as same direction are always equal.

59666.explanation: Negative of {tex}\vec a{/tex} is equal to - {tex}\vec a{/tex} , i.e. A vector whose magnitude is the same as that of {tex}\vec a{/tex} ,but direction is opposite to that of {tex}\vec a{/tex} .

59667.explanation: If a vector is multiplied by any scalar then , the result is always a vector.

59681.explanation: If the direction cosines of two vectors are the same, then the vectors are said to be parallel.

59684.explanation: We have: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat {i} - 7\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} , then, {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \sqrt {{2^2} + {{( - 7)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2}} = \sqrt {4 + 49 + 9} = \sqrt {62}{/tex} .

59685.explanation: We have : {tex}\vec a = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k{/tex} , then, {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3}} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{3}} = 1.{/tex}

59686.explanation: The scalar and vector components of the vector with initial point (2, 1) and terminal point (– 5, 7) is given by : (- 5 – 2 ) i.e. – 7 and (7 – 1 ) i.e. 6. Therefore, the scalar components are – 7 and 6 .,and vector components are - {tex}7\hat i\;and\;6\hat j.{/tex}

59687.explanation: {tex}\Rightarrow 2\hat i + 3\hat j\; = \;x\hat i + y\hat j \Rightarrow x = 2,y = 3 \\\\\\ {/tex}

59688.explanation: We have: vectors {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-2\hat{j}+\hat{k},~\vec{b}=-2\hat{i}+4\hat{j}+5\hat{k}~and~\vec{c}=\hat{i}-6\hat{j}-7\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec a +\vec b +\vec c=\hat i -2\hat j+\hat k+(-2\hat i +4 \hat j+5\hat k)+\hat i-6\hat j -7\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec a +\vec b+\vec c= 0\hat i -4\hat j -\hat k{/tex}

59689.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow r = x\widehat i + y\widehat j {/tex} be a unit vector in XY plane,making angle 30 0 with positive X axis ,so we have the vector as {tex}\overrightarrow r = \cos {30^0}\hat i + \sin {30^0}\hat j{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2};y = \frac{1}{2},\left( {\because \left| {\overrightarrow r } \right| = 1} \right).{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow r = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}\widehat i + \frac{1}{2}\widehat j{/tex} is the required vector.

59690.explanation: As {tex}x{/tex} {tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} is a unit vector, therefore, {tex} \left| {x\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right)} \right| = 1 \Rightarrow x\sqrt {1 + 1 + 1} = 1 \Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} \\ {/tex}

59691.explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = 4\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 6\widehat k = 2\left( {2\widehat i + \widehat j + 3\widehat k} \right){/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow {BC} = 6\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 9\widehat k = 3(2\widehat i + \widehat j + 3\widehat k){/tex} {tex} \therefore \overrightarrow {AB} = 2 \times \frac{{\overrightarrow {BC} }}{3} {/tex} Therefore , AB and BC are parallel, but point B is common , so points , A,B,C are collinear. As {tex}\frac{{\overrightarrow {AB} }}{{\overrightarrow {BC} }} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} , thus , point B divides AC in the ratio 2 : 3.

59692.explanation: Let position vector of point R be {tex}\overrightarrow r {/tex} . As point R divides externally the line segment PQ in the ratio 1:2 .therefore , {tex} \overrightarrow r = \frac{{1\left( {\overrightarrow a - 3\overrightarrow b } \right) - 2\left( {2\overrightarrow a + \overrightarrow b } \right)}}{{1 - 2}} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\left( { - 3\overrightarrow a - 5\overrightarrow b } \right)}}{{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow r = 3\overrightarrow a + 5\overrightarrow b \\ {/tex} Also , mid point of the line segment RQ is : {tex}= \frac{{3\overrightarrow a + 5\overrightarrow b + \overrightarrow a - 3\overrightarrow b }}{2} = \frac{{4\overrightarrow a + 2\overrightarrow b }}{2} = 2\overrightarrow a + \overrightarrow b{/tex} ,w hich is the position vector of point P.Therefore , P is the mid point of line segment RQ.

59693.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{d}=\lambda (\overrightarrow{a}\times \overrightarrow{b}),{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec d = \lambda \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&4&2 \\ 3&{ - 2}&7 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}=\lambda(32\hat i -\hat j-14 \hat k){/tex} Also, {tex}\vec c.\vec d{/tex} = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow (2\hat i -\hat j+ 4\hat k).\lambda(32 \hat i -\hat j-14 \hat k)=15{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 9\lambda=15\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{5}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec d=\frac{5}{3}(32 \hat i -\hat j-14 \hat k){/tex} {tex}=\frac{160}{3}\hat i-\frac{5}{3}\hat j -\frac{70}{3}\hat k{/tex}

59694.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a}=\widehat{i}+\widehat{j}+\widehat{k},\overrightarrow{b}=2\widehat{i}+4\widehat{j}-5\widehat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{c}=\lambda \widehat{i}+2\widehat{j}+3\widehat{k},{/tex} {tex}\vec b +\vec c =(\lambda+2)\hat i +6\hat j-2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec b+\vec c|=\sqrt{(\lambda+2)^2+40}{/tex} Therefore, a unit vector along {tex}\overrightarrow{b}+\overrightarrow{c}{/tex} is given by: {tex}\frac{\vec b +\vec c}{|\vec b+\vec c|}=\frac{(\lambda+2)\hat i +6 \hat j-2\hat k}{\sqrt{(\lambda+2)^2+40}}=1{/tex} Also, scalar product of {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} with above unit vector is 1. {tex}\therefore(\hat i+ \hat j+\hat k).\frac{(\lambda+2)\hat i +6 \hat j-2\hat k}{\sqrt{(\lambda+2)^2+40}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (\lambda+6)^2=(\lambda+2)^2+40\Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex}

59695.explanation: The scalar product of two nonzero vectors {tex}\vec a\;{/tex} and {tex}\;\vec b{/tex} is denoted by {tex}\overrightarrow a \overrightarrow {.b} {/tex} .and is defined by {tex}\overrightarrow a \overrightarrow {.b} {/tex} = {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|\cos \theta {/tex}

59696.explanation: The scalar product of two nonzero vectors {tex}\vec a\;{/tex} and {tex}\;\vec b{/tex} is defined as: {tex}\vec a.\vec b = \left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|\cos \theta {/tex} .

59697.explanation: If {tex}\vec a = {a_1}\hat i + {a_2}\hat j + {a_3}\hat k\;and\;\vec b = {b_1}\hat i + {b_2}\hat j + {b_3}\hat k{/tex} then {tex}\vec a.\vec b = {/tex} {tex}{a_1}{b_1} + {a_2}{b_2} + {a_3}{b_3}{/tex} {tex}( {\because \widehat i.\widehat i = \widehat j.\widehat j = \widehat k.\widehat k = 1\,and\,\widehat i.\widehat j = \widehat j.\widehat k = \widehat k.\widehat i = 0}){/tex}

59698.explanation: If {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between vectors {tex}\vec a\;{/tex} and {tex}\;\vec b{/tex} then, the cross product: {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = {/tex} {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|\sin \theta \hat n{/tex} .

59699.explanation: We have: vector {tex}\overrightarrow a = \hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k,{/tex} {tex}\widehat a = \frac{{\overrightarrow a }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|}} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} }} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt 6 }} = \frac{{\hat i}}{{\sqrt 6 }} + \frac{{\hat j}}{{\sqrt 6 }} + \frac{{2\hat k}}{{\sqrt 6 }}{/tex}

59700.explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow{PQ}=3\widehat{i}+3\widehat{j}+3\widehat{k}{/tex} , then {tex}\hat{PQ}=\frac{\vec{PQ}}{|\vec {PQ}|}=\frac{3\hat i +3 \hat j +3 \hat k}{\sqrt{3^2+3^2+3^2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3\hat i +3 \hat j +3 \hat k}{\sqrt{27}}=\frac{3(\hat i +\hat j +\hat j)}{3\sqrt3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt3}\hat i+\frac{1}{\sqrt3}\hat j +\frac{1}{\sqrt 3}\hat k{/tex}

59701.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a}=5\widehat{i}-\widehat{j}+2\widehat{k}{/tex} then, {tex}8\hat a = 8\frac{\vec a}{|\vec a|}{/tex} = {tex}8.\frac{5 \hat i-\hat j +2 \hat k}{\sqrt{5^2+(-1)^2+2^2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8(5 \hat i-\hat j +2 \hat k)}{\sqrt{30}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{40}{\sqrt{30}}\hat i -\frac{8}{\sqrt{30}}\hat j +\frac{16}{\sqrt{30}}\hat k{/tex}

59702.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow a =\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k {/tex} , Then , {tex}\widehat a = \frac{{\overrightarrow a }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|}} = \frac{{\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {2^2} + {3^2}} }} = \frac{{\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k}}{{\sqrt {14} }}{/tex} Therefore , the D.C.’s of vector a are : {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt {14} }},\;\frac{2}{{\sqrt {14} }},\;\frac{3}{{\sqrt {14} }}.{/tex}

59703.explanation: We have: {tex}\overrightarrow{AB}=-2\widehat{i}-4\widehat{j}+4\widehat{k}{/tex} , then, {tex}\hat {AB}=\frac{\vec {AB}}{|\vec {AB}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-2\hat i -4\hat j +4 \hat k}{\sqrt{(-2)^2+(-4)^2+4^2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-2\hat i -4\hat j +4 \hat k}{\sqrt{36}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-2\hat i }{6}-\frac{4\hat j}{6}+\frac{4\hat k}{6}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1\hat i }{3}-\frac{2\hat j}{3}+\frac{2\hat k}{3}{/tex} Therefore, the D.C.’s of vector AB are given by: {tex}-\frac{1}{3},~-\frac{2}{3},\frac{2}{3}{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

59704.explanation: {tex} \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \sqrt 3 ,\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right| = 2,\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b = \sqrt 6 ,{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b = \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|.\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|\cos \theta \Rightarrow \sqrt 6{/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt 3 \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{4} {/tex}

59705.explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow a = \widehat i - 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k,\overrightarrow b = 3\widehat i - 2\widehat j + \widehat k \Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \sqrt {14} ,\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right| = \sqrt {14} ,\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b = 10, {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|}} = \cos \theta \Rightarrow \frac{{10}}{{14}} = \cos \theta {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{5}{7} \Rightarrow \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{5}{7} \\\\\\ {/tex}

59706.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow a = \widehat i - \widehat j,\overrightarrow b = \widehat i + \widehat j{/tex} , then,projection of vector a on vector b is given by : {tex}\frac{{\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|}} = \frac{{(\widehat i - \widehat j).(\widehat i + \widehat j)}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{0}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 0{/tex}

59707.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow a = \widehat i + 3\widehat j + 7\widehat k,\overrightarrow b = 7\widehat i - \widehat j + 8\widehat k{/tex} , then projection of vector a on vector b is given by : {tex}\frac{{\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|}} = \frac{{(\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 7\widehat k).(7\widehat i - \widehat j + 8\widehat k)}}{{\sqrt {49 + 1 + 64} }} {/tex} {tex}\\ = \frac{{7 - 3 + 56}}{{\sqrt {114} }} = \frac{{60}}{{\sqrt {114} }}{/tex}

59708.explanation: {tex}(\overrightarrow a + \overrightarrow b ).(\overrightarrow a - \overrightarrow b ) = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\overrightarrow a|^2- |\overrightarrow b|^2=8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 64|\overrightarrow b|^2- |\overrightarrow b|^2=8\Rightarrow 63|\overrightarrow b|^2=8 \Rightarrow |\overrightarrow b| =\sqrt{\frac{8}{63}}=\frac{2\sqrt2}{3\sqrt7}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\overrightarrow a|=8|\overrightarrow b|\Rightarrow |\overrightarrow a|=\frac{16\sqrt2}{3\sqrt7}{/tex}

59709.explanation: {tex}\left( {3\vec a - 5\vec b} \right).\left( {2\vec a + 7\vec b} \right){/tex} = {tex}6 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } + 21 \overline { a } \cdot \overline { b } - 10 \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } - 35 \vec { b } \cdot \vec { b } = 6 | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 11 \vec { a } \vec { b } - 35 | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex}

59710.explanation: It is given that: {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = \left| {\vec b} \right|,\theta = {30^ \circ },\overrightarrow a. \overrightarrow b = 2\sqrt 3 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow | \vec { a } | \cdot | \vec { b } | \cos 30 ^ { \circ } = 2 \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 }.\frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } = 2 \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } = 4 \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | = 2 = | \vec { b } |{/tex}

59711.explanation: It is given that : {tex} \left( {2\overrightarrow x - 3\overrightarrow a } \right).\left( {2\overrightarrow x + 3\overrightarrow a } \right) = 91 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4{\left| {\overrightarrow x } \right|^2} - 9{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|^2} {/tex} {tex} = 91 \Rightarrow 4{\left| {\overrightarrow x } \right|^2} - 9.1 = 91 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 4{\left| {\overrightarrow x } \right|^2} = 100 \Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow x } \right| = 5 \\{/tex}

59712.explanation: If {tex}\vec{a}=2\hat{i}+2\hat{j}+3\hat{k},~\vec{b}=-\hat{i}+2\hat{j}+\hat{k}~and~\vec{c}=3\hat{i}+\hat{j}~{/tex} are such that {tex}\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b} {/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec{c}, then (\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b} ).\vec{c}=0{/tex} . {tex}[(2\hat i +2\hat j +3 \hat k)+\lambda(-\hat i +2 \hat j+\hat k)].(3\hat i +\hat j)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow [(2-\lambda)\hat i +(2+2\lambda)\hat j+(3+\lambda)\hat k].(3\hat i+\hat j)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6-3\lambda+2+2\lambda=0\Rightarrow \lambda=8{/tex}

59713.explanation: It is given that: If {tex}\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}{/tex} are unit vectors such that {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0,{/tex} then: {tex}(\vec a +\vec b+\vec c).(\vec a +\vec b+\vec c)=\vec 0 .\vec 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec a|^2+|\vec b|^2+|\vec c|^2+2(\vec a.\vec b+\vec b.\vec c+\vec c.\vec a)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1+1+1+2(\vec a.\vec b+\vec b.\vec c+\vec c.\vec a)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (\vec a.\vec b+\vec b.\vec c+\vec c.\vec a)=-\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

59714.explanation: Position vectors of the points A , B and C are {tex}\widehat{i}+2\widehat{j}+3\widehat{k},-\widehat{i},and \ \widehat{j}+2\widehat{k}{/tex} respectively. Then; {tex}cos\theta=\frac{\vec{BA}\vec{BC}}{|\vec {BA}||\vec{BC}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(2\hat i+2\hat j+3\hat k).(\hat i+\hat j+2\hat k)}{\sqrt{17}\sqrt6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow cos\theta=\frac{10}{\sqrt{102}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \angle ABC=cos^{-1}(\frac{10}{\sqrt{102}}){/tex}

59715.explanation: {tex}\lambda \vec a{/tex} is a unit vector if and only if {tex}\vec a{/tex} is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{{\left| \lambda \right|}}{/tex} .

59716.explanation: {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 3&1&2 \\ 2&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right| = 8\hat i - 8\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;|\vec a \times \vec b| = \sqrt {{8^2} + {{( - 8)}^2} + {{( - 8)}^2}} = 8\sqrt 3 {/tex}

59717.explanation: It is given that: {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i+2 \hat j+2 \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b=\hat i +2 \hat j-2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec a +\vec b=4\hat i+4\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\vec a -\vec b=2\hat i +4 \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore (\vec a + \vec b) \times (\vec a - \vec b) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 4&4&0 \\ 2&0&4 \end{array}} \right| = 16\hat i - 16\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore| (\vec a + \vec b) \times (\vec a - \vec b) |= \sqrt{576}=24{/tex} Therefore, the unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}(\overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b}){/tex} and {tex}(\overrightarrow{a}-\overrightarrow{b}){/tex} is given by: {tex}=\pm \frac{(16\widehat{i}-16\widehat{j}-8\widehat{k})}{24}=\pm \frac{1}{3}(2\widehat{i}-2\widehat{j}-\widehat{k}) .{/tex}

59718.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a}={{a}_{1}}\widehat{i}+{{a}_{2}}\widehat{j}+{{a}_{3}}\widehat{k},{/tex} It is given that {tex}\left| \overrightarrow{a} \right|=1{/tex} , then, {tex}\Rightarrow a^2_1+a^2_2+a^2_3=1{/tex} ....(1) {tex}\therefore \vec a.\hat i =(a_1\hat i+a_2\hat j+a_3\hat k).\hat i{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec a||\hat i|cos\frac{\pi}{3}=a_1 \Rightarrow a_1=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec a.\hat j =(a_1\hat i+a_2\hat j+a_3\hat k).\hat j{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec a||\hat j|cos\frac{\pi}{4}=a_2 \Rightarrow a_2=\frac{1}{\sqrt2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec a.\hat k =(a_1\hat i+a_2\hat j+a_3\hat k).\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec a||\hat k|cos{\theta}=a_3 \Rightarrow a_3=\cos\theta{/tex} Putting these values in (1), we get: {tex}\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{2} + {\cos ^2}\theta = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3}{4} = 1 - {\cos ^2}\theta \Rightarrow {\sin ^2}\theta = \frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \sin \theta = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = {60^ \circ }{/tex}

59719.explanation: Let, {tex}\overrightarrow a = {a_1}\hat i + {a_2}\hat j + {a_3}\hat k{/tex} , then, {tex}\Rightarrow a^2_1+a^2_2+a_3^2=1{/tex} .....(1) {tex}\therefore \vec a.\hat i = (a_1\hat i +a_2 \hat j+a_3\hat k).\hat i\Rightarrow |\vec a||\hat i|cos\frac{\pi}{3}=a_1\Rightarrow a_1=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec a.\hat j= (a_1\hat i +a_2 \hat j+a_3\hat k).\hat j\Rightarrow |\vec a||\hat j|cos\frac{\pi}{4}=a_2\Rightarrow a_2=\frac{1}{\sqrt2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec a.\hat k= (a_1\hat i +a_2 \hat j+a_3\hat k).\hat k\Rightarrow |\vec a||\hat k|cos\frac{\pi}{4}=a_3\Rightarrow a_3=\cos\theta{/tex} Putting these values in (1) , we get : {tex}\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2}+cos^2\theta=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3}{4}=1-cos^2\theta \Rightarrow sin^2\theta=\frac{3}{4}\Rightarrow sin\theta =\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\Rightarrow \theta =60^o{/tex} {tex}\therefore a_3=cos60^o=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec a=\frac{1}{2}\hat i+\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\hat j+\frac{1}{2}\hat k{/tex}

59720.explanation: It is given that: {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right)X\left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + \mu \hat k} \right) = \overrightarrow 0 {/tex} {tex}\left| {\matrix{ {\hat i} & {\hat j} & {\hat k} \cr 2 & 6 & {27} \cr 1 & \lambda & \mu \cr } } \right| = \hat i\left( {6\mu - 27\lambda } \right) - \hat j\left( {2\mu - 27} \right) + \hat k\left( {2\lambda - 6} \right) = \overrightarrow 0 {/tex} , equating the coefficients of {tex}\hat i,\hat j,\hat k{/tex} on both sides,we get {tex}\left( {6\mu - 27\lambda } \right) = 0,\left( {2\mu - 27} \right) = 0,\left( {2\lambda - 6} \right) = 0.{/tex} solving,we get {tex}{\lambda = 3,\mu = {{27} \over 2}}{/tex}

59721.explanation: It is given that {tex}\overrightarrow{a}\times \overrightarrow{b}{/tex} is a unit vector, then: {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec a\times \vec b|=1\Rightarrow |\vec a||\vec b|sin\theta =1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3.\frac{\sqrt2}{3}sin\theta=1\Rightarrow sin\theta =\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\Rightarrow \theta =\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

59722.explanation: {tex}\vec a.\vec b=|\vec a||\vec b|cos\theta{/tex} , Also, {tex}\vec a .\vec b \geq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec a||\vec b|cos\theta \Rightarrow cos\theta\leq 0\Rightarrow 0\leq\theta\leq\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

59723.explanation: It is given that {tex}|\vec a|=|\vec b|=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore |\vec a+\vec b|^2=(\vec a+\vec b).(\vec a+\vec b)=|\vec a|^2+|\vec b|^2+2\vec a.\vec b{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec a+\vec b|^2=1+1+2|\vec a||\vec b|cos\theta=2+2cos\theta{/tex} It is given that {tex}(\vec a+\vec b){/tex} is a unit vector. Therefore, 2 + 2 {tex}cos\theta{/tex} = 1 Therefore, {tex}\theta=\frac{2\pi}{3}{/tex}

59724.explanation: We have: {tex}|\vec a.\vec b|=|\vec a \times \vec b|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec a||\vec b|cos\theta =|\vec a||\vec b|sin\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow cos\theta=\sin\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow tan\theta =1\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

59725.explanation: Given position vector of A , {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} position vector of B, {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} and that of C, {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = \hat i + 5\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} therefore, {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} = \left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k} \right) - \left( {\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right) = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} (by triangle law of vector addition) thus we may write {tex}\vec{AB}=\hat i +2\hat j+3\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\vec{AC}=4\hat j+3\hat k,{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{AB}\times \overrightarrow{AC}=\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&3 \\ 0&4&3 \end{array}} \right|=-6\hat i-3\hat j+4\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\overrightarrow{AB}\times\vec{AC}|=\sqrt{61}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{1}{2} |\overrightarrow{AB}\times\vec{AC}|=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{61}{/tex} Therefore, the area of triangle ABC is = {tex}{1 \over 2}\sqrt {61} {/tex}

59726.explanation: It is given that: {tex}\vec a =\hat i -\hat j+3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b=2\hat i -7\hat j+\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \tilde { k } } \\ { 1 } & { - 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { - 7 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| = 20 \hat { i } + \hat { 5 } \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}|\vec a \times \vec b| =\sqrt{450}=15\sqrt2{/tex}

59727.explanation: We have: {tex}​​\overrightarrow {OA} = - \hat i + {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} (position vector of A) similarly , {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = \hat i + {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = \hat i - {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow {OD} = - \hat i - {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} : ,where {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} = \left( {\hat i + {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) - \left( { - \hat i + {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) = 2\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} (by triangle law of vector addition),similarly {tex}\overrightarrow {AD} = 0\hat i - \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} , Therefore , area of rectangle ABCD is given by {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} X} \right.\left. {\overrightarrow {AD} } \right|{/tex} ,where {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} X\overrightarrow {AD} = {/tex} {tex}\left| {\matrix{ {\hat i} & {\hat j} & {\hat k} \cr 2 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & { - 1} & 0 \cr } } \right| = \hat i\left( {0 - 0} \right) - \hat j\left( {0 - 0} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 2 - 0} \right){/tex} = {tex} - 2\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} X} \right.\left. {\overrightarrow {AD} } \right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {{0^2} + {0^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2}} = {/tex} 2 sq. units.

59728.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow r = - x\widehat i + y\widehat j{/tex} , {tex}x = \frac{5}{2},y = \frac{{3\sqrt 3 }}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow r = \frac{{ - 5}}{2}\widehat i + \frac{{3\sqrt 3 }}{2}\widehat j{/tex}

59729.explanation: We have, Diagonal AC as, {tex}\vec{AC}=(\hat i - 2\hat j -3\hat k )+(2\hat i-4\hat j +5\hat k)=3\hat i-6\hat j+2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{AC}|=\sqrt{(3)^2+(-6)^2+(2)^2}=\sqrt{49}=7{/tex} A unit vector parallel to {tex}\vec AC{/tex} is given by: {tex}\frac{\vec{AC}}{|\vec {AC}|}=\frac{3\hat i -6\hat j+2\hat k}{7}= \frac{3}{7}\hat i-\frac{6}{7}\hat j+\frac{2}{7}\hat k{/tex} Also, area of parallelogram {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{AB}\times\vec{AD}|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&{ - 4}&5 \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} = {tex}|22\hat i +11 \hat j|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{22^2+11^2}=\sqrt{605}=11\sqrt5{/tex}

59730.explanation: A line when intersect another line or axis two kind of angle form ,as an assumption we take positive side of axis, to define the direction of line we take angle made from all three axis.and we take cos not other trigonometric function like sin,tan because we can define Direction cosines of a line are coefficient of i, j ,k of a unit vector along that line.

59732.explanation: Since we know Direction cosines of a line are coefficient of i, j, k of a unit vector along that line, first find a vector {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ}{/tex} = (x 2 - x 1 ) {tex}\widehat i {/tex} + (y 2 - y 1 ) {tex}\widehat j{/tex} + (z 2 - z 1 ) {tex}\widehat k{/tex} then to convert it unit vector divide by its magnitude | {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} | the coefficient of this unit vector will be {tex}\frac{{{x_2} - {x_1}}}{d},\;\frac{{{y_2} - {y_1}}}{d},\;\frac{{{z_2} - {z_1}}}{d}{/tex}

59733.explanation: If l, m and n are the direction cosines of a line, Direction ratios of the line are the numbers which are Proportional to the direction cosines of the line.

59735.explanation: Two lines are said to be skew lines if they are neither parallel nor intersecting, this implies two lines are skew if they are not coplanar.

59736.explanation: Angle between skew lines is the angle between two lines which are parallel to the given skew lines and passing through a common point in space.

59737.explanation: If {tex}{l_1},{\text{ }}{m_1},{\text{ }}{n_1}{/tex} and {tex}{l_2},{\text{ }}{m_2},{\text{ }}{n_2}{/tex} are the direction cosines of two lines; and {tex}\theta {/tex} is the acute angle between the two lines; then the cosine of the angle between these two lines is given by : {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\left| {{l_1}{l_2} + {m_1}{m_2} + {n_1}{n_2}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {(l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2)(l_2^2 + m_2^2 + n_2^2)} }}{/tex} But {tex}{l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2 = 1}{/tex} and {tex}{l_2^2 + m_2^2 + n_2^2 = 1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \cos \theta = \left| {{l_1}{l_2} + {m_1}{m_2} + {n_1}{n_2}} \right|{/tex}

59738.explanation: If a 1 ,b 1 ,c 1, and a 2 ,b 2 ,c 2 are the direction ratios of two lines and {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between the two lines; then, the cosine of the angle between these two lines is given by: {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{{a_1}{a_2} + {b_1}{b_2} + {c_1}{c_2}}}{{\sqrt {a_1^2 + b_1^2 + c_1^2} \sqrt {a_2^2 + b_2^2 + c_2^2} }}} \right|{/tex} .

59739.explanation: The vector equation of a line that passes through the given point whose position vector is {tex}\vec a{/tex} and parallel to a given vector {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by: {tex}\vec r = \vec a + \lambda \vec b{/tex} ., {tex}\lambda \in R{/tex} Where, {tex}\overrightarrow r = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow a = {a_1}\hat i + {b_1}\hat j + {c_1}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow b = {a_1}\hat i + {b_1}\hat j + {c_1}\hat k{/tex}

59740.explanation: The equation of a line passing through a point (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) and having direction cosines (l, m, n )is given by : {tex}\frac{x-x_1}l=\frac{y-y_1}m=\frac{z-z_1}n=\lambda{/tex} . where l,m,n are non zero real numbers.

59743.explanation: If {tex}\theta {/tex} is the acute angle between {tex}\vec r = \overrightarrow {{a_1}} + \lambda \overrightarrow {{b_1}} \;and\;\vec r = \overrightarrow {{a_2}} + \lambda \overrightarrow {{b_2}} \;{/tex} , then cosine of the angle between these two lines is given by : {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{\overrightarrow {{b_1}} .\overrightarrow {{b_2}} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex} Because angle between two lines is the angle between their parallels

59744.explanation: If {tex}\frac{{x - {x_1}}}{{{l_1}}} = \frac{{y - {y_1}}}{{{m_1}}} = \frac{{z - {z_1}}}{{{n_1}}}\ {/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x - {x_1}}}{{{l_2}}} = \frac{{y - {y_1}}}{{{m_2}}} = \frac{{z - {z_1}}}{{{n_2}}}{/tex} are the equations of the two lines, then the acute angle between the two lines is given by: {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {{l_1}{l_2} + {m_1}{m_2} + {n_1}{n_2}} \right|{/tex} . and {tex} l_1^2+m_1^2+n_1^2=1 ; l_2^2+m_2^2 +n_2^2=1{/tex}

59745.explanation: Shortest distance between two skew lines is the line segment in space perpendicular to both the lines.

59751.explanation: The equation of the line which passes through the point (1, 2, 3) and is parallel to the vector {tex}3\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} , let vector {tex}\overrightarrow a = \widehat i + \widehat j + \widehat k{/tex} and vector {tex}\overrightarrow b = 3\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} , the equation of line is : {tex}\overrightarrow a + \lambda \overrightarrow b \\ {/tex} {tex}= (\widehat i + \widehat j + \widehat k) + \lambda (3\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k){/tex}

59757.explanation: If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the acute angle between {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{{{a}_{1}}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{{{a}_{2}}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}}~{/tex} , then cosine of the angle between these two lines is given by : {tex}\cos \theta =\left| \frac{\overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}}.\overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}}}{\left| \overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}} \right|\left| \overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}} \right|} \right|{/tex} Here, {tex}\overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}}=3\hat{i}+2\hat{j}+6\hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}}=\hat{i}+2\hat{j}+2\hat{k}{/tex} Then, {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{(3\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k).(\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k)}}{{\left| {(3\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k)} \right|\left| {(\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k)} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{19}}{{\sqrt {49} \sqrt 9 }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{19}}{{21}}} \right| \Rightarrow \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\left| {\frac{{19}}{{21}}} \right|} \right){/tex}

59758.explanation: Given lines are: {tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{2p/7}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 3p/7}} = \frac{{y - 5}}{1} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 5}}{/tex} The D.R's of the lines are -3, 2p/7, 2 and -3p/7, 1, -5. {tex}\therefore \cos {90^o} = \frac{{( - 3) \times \left( {\frac{{ - 3p}}{7}} \right) + \left( {\frac{{2p}}{7}} \right) \times 1 + 2 \times ( - 5)}}{{\sqrt {9 + \frac{{4{p^2}}}{{49}} + 4} \sqrt {\frac{{9{p^2}}}{{49}} + 1 + 25} }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{9p}}{7} + \frac{{2p}}{7} - 10 = 0 \Rightarrow p = \frac{{70}}{{11}}{/tex}

59759.explanation: On comparing the given equations with: {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_1}} + \lambda \overrightarrow {{b_1}} ,{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_2}} + \mu \overrightarrow {{b_2}} ,{/tex} we get: {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = \hat i - \hat j + \hat k,{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_2}} = 2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = 2\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore S.D. = \left| {\frac{{(\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} ).(\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} )}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{( - 3\hat i + 3\hat k).(\hat i - 3\hat j - 2\hat k)}}{{3\sqrt 2 }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{ - 3 + - 6}}{{3\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{ - 9}}{{3\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{3\sqrt 2 }}{2}{/tex}

59761.explanation: On comparing the given equations with: {tex}\overrightarrow{r}=\overrightarrow{{{a}_{1}}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}},{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{r}=\overrightarrow{{{a}_{2}}}+\mu \overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}}{/tex} , we get: {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = \hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k,{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_2}} = 4\hat i + 5\hat j + 6\hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore S.D = \left| {\frac{{(\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} ).(\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_2}} )}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{( - 9\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k).(3\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k)}}{{\sqrt {171} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{ - 27 + 9 + 27}}{{3\sqrt {19} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{9}{{3\sqrt {19} }}} \right| = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {19} }}{/tex}

59762.explanation: The given equations can be reduced as: {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}-2\hat{j}+3\hat{k}+t\left( -\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2\hat{k}. \right){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}+s\left( \hat{i}+2\hat{j}-2\hat{k}. \right){/tex} On comparing them with: {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_1}} + t\overrightarrow {{b_1}} ,{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_2}} + t\overrightarrow {{b_2}} ,{/tex} We get: {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_2}} = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = - \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore S.D = \left| {\frac{{(\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} ).(\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_2}} )}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{(2\hat i - 4\hat j - 3\hat k).(\hat j - 4\hat k)}}{{\sqrt {29} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{ - 4 + 12}}{{\sqrt {29} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{8}{{\sqrt {29} }}} \right|{/tex}

59763.explanation: If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the acute angle between {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_1}} + \lambda \overrightarrow {{b_1}} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_2}} + \lambda \overrightarrow {{b_2}}{/tex} then cosine of the angle between these two lines is given by: {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{\overrightarrow {{b_1}} .\overrightarrow {{b_2}} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex} Here, {tex}\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = \hat i - \hat j - 2\hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = 3\hat i - 5\hat j - 4\hat k{/tex} Then, {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{(\hat i - \hat j - 2\hat k).(3\hat i - 5\hat j - 4\hat k)}}{{\left| {(\hat i - \hat j - 2\hat k)} \right|\left| {(3\hat i - 5\hat j - 4\hat k)} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{16}}{{\sqrt 6 \sqrt {50} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{16}}{{\sqrt {300} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\left| {\frac{{16}}{{10\sqrt 3 }}} \right|} \right){/tex} {tex}= {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\left| {\frac{8}{{5\sqrt 3 }}} \right|} \right){/tex}

59805.explanation: A linear programming problem is one that is concerned with finding the optimal value (maximum or minimum) of a linear function of several variables .

59806.explanation: Traffic signal control types of problems cannot be solved by linear programming methods, because there is no need for optimization in such problems.

59807.explanation: In linear programming feasible region (or solution region) for the problem is given by the common region determined by all the constraints including the non – negative constraints x {tex} \geqslant {/tex} 0, y {tex} \geqslant {/tex} 0

59808.explanation: In linear programming infeasible solutions fall outside the feasible region. In other words, it the region other than the feasible region is called the infeasible region.

59809.explanation: In linear programming, any point in the feasible region which gives that gives the optimal value (maximum or minimum) of the objective function is called optimal solution. In other words, it satisfies all the constraints as well as the objective function.

59810.explanation: Let R be the feasible region (convex polygon) for a linear programming problem and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. When Z has an optimal value (maximum or minimum), where the variables x and y are subject to constraints described by linear inequalities then , optimal value must occur at a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region.

59811.explanation: Let R be the feasible region for a linear programming problem, and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. If R is bounded, then the objective function Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R and each of these occur at the corner point (vertex) of R.

59812.explanation: Let R be the feasible region for a linear programming problem,and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. If R is bounded, then the objective function Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R and each of these occurs at a corner point (vertex) of R.

59813.explanation: A maximum or a minimum may or may not exist for a linear programming problem if the feasible region is unbounded. However if it exits it must occur at the corner points of R.

59814.explanation: In Corner point method for solving a linear programming problem the first step is : To find the feasible region of the linear programming problem and determine its corner points (vertices) either by inspection or by solving the two equations of the lines intersecting at that point.

59815.explanation: In Corner point method for solving a linear programming problem the second step after finding the feasible region of the linear programming problem and determining its corner points is : To evaluate the objective function Z = ax + by at each corner point.

59816.explanation: In Corner point method for solving a linear programming problem one finds the feasible region of the linear programming problem ,determines its corner points and evaluates the objective function Z = ax + by at each corner point. If Mand m respectively be the largest and smallest values at corner points then If the feasible region is bounded, M and m respectively are the maximum and minimum values of the objective function .

59817.explanation: In Corner point method for solving a linear programming problem one finds the feasible region of the linear programming problem ,determines its corner points and evaluates the objective function Z = ax + by at each corner point. Let M and m respectively be the largest and smallest values at corner points. In case feasible region is unbounded, M is the maximum value of the objective function if the open half plane determined by ax + by > M has no point in common with the feasible region . Otherwise, Z has no maximum value.

59818.explanation: In Corner point method for solving a linear programming problem one finds the feasible region of the linear programming problem ,determines its corner points and evaluates the objective function Z = ax + by at each corner point. Let M and m respectively be the largest and smallest values at corner points. In case feasible region is unbounded, m is the minimum value of the objective function if the open half plane determined by ax + by < m has no point in common with the feasible region . Otherwise, Z has no minimum point.

59819.explanation: If two corner points of the feasible region are both optimal solutions of the same type, i.e., both produce the same maximum or minimum , then any point on the line segment joining these two points is also an optimal solution of the same type .

59922.explanation: Let {tex}{E_1}{/tex} and {tex}{E_2}{/tex} are events of selection of the first and second bag respectively {tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = P({E_2}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Let A = event of getting a red ball. {tex}\therefore P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { 4 } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} , {tex}P \left( \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { E } _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P \left( E _ { 1 } / \mathrm { A } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 1 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) P \left( \mathrm { A } / E _ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex}

Let A = event of getting grade A. {tex}\\P(A/{{E}_{1}}) =\frac{30}{100}{/tex} = 0.3, {tex}P(A/{{E}_{2}}) =\frac{20}{100}{/tex} = 0.2 {tex}P({{E}_{1}}) =\frac{60}{100}{/tex} = 0.6, {tex}P({{E}_{2}}) =\frac{40}{100}{/tex} = 0.4 {tex}P({{E}_{1}}/A){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P({{A/E}_{1}}).P({{E}_{1}})}{P(A/{{E}_{1}}).P({{E}_{1}})+P({A/{E}_{2}})P({{E}_{2}})}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(0.6)(0.3)}{(0.6)(0.3)+(0.4)(0.2)} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{9}{13} {/tex}

59925.explanation: Let E 1 and E 2 are events that a person has a disease and the person has not diseased. {tex}\therefore P({{E}_{1}})=\frac{0.1}{100}{/tex} , {tex}P({{E}_{2}})=\frac{99.9}{100}{/tex} . 59954.explanation:
For R = {(a, b): a+b is even,{tex}\forall {/tex} a,b {tex} \in {/tex} N},
Reflexive property: (a, b) {tex} \in {/tex} R {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} a + b is even. Now put b = a, a + a is also even {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}(a, a) {tex} \in {/tex} R, R is reflexive.
Symmetric property: (a,b) {tex} \in {/tex} R  {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} a + b is even {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} b + a is also even {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (b, a) {tex} \in {/tex} R  {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} R is Symmetric.
Transitive property: (a, b) {tex} \in {/tex} R,(b,c) {tex} \in {/tex} R {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} a + b is even and b + c is even {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} a + c is also even {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (a, c) {tex} \in {/tex} R, so R is transitive. Hence R is an equivalence relation.

230692.explanation:
Given matrix A is a symmetric and matrix B is a skew-symmetric.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AT = A and BT = -B
Since, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {3} \\ {5} & {-1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} (given) ... (i)
On taking transpose both sides, we get
(A + B)T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {3} \\ {5} & {-1}\end{array}\right]^{T}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A^{T}+B^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {5} \\ {3} & {-1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(ii)
Given, AT = A and BT = -B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {5} \\ {3} & {-1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {4} \\ {4} & {-1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {-1} \\ {1} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, {tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {4} \\ {4} & {-1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {-1} \\ {1} & {0}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4} & {-2} \\ {-1} & {-4}\end{array}\right]{/tex}

for which
AT A = 3I3
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{0} & {2 x} & {2 x} \\ {2 y} & {y} & {-y} \\ {1} & {-1} & {1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{0} & {2 y} & {1} \\ {2 x} & {y} & {-1} \\ {2 x} & {-y} & {1}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{3} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {3} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{8 x^{2}} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {6 y^{2}} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {3}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{3} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {3} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Here, two matrices are equal, therefore equating the corresponding elements, we get
8x2 = 3 and 6y2 = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\pm \sqrt{\frac{3}{8}}{/tex}
and {tex}y=\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex}  There are 2 different values of x and y each. So, 4 matrices are possible such that ATA = 3I3

230708.explanation:
Given, matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore A^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos ^{2} \alpha-\sin ^{2} \alpha} & {-\cos \alpha \sin \alpha-\sin \alpha \cos \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha \cos \alpha+\cos \alpha \sin \alpha} & {-\sin ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \alpha} & {-\sin 2 \alpha} \\ {\sin 2 \alpha} & {\cos 2 \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Similarly,
{tex}A^{n}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos (n \alpha)} & {-\sin (n \alpha)} \\ {\sin (n \alpha)} & {\cos (n \alpha)}\end{array}\right], n \in N{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A^{32}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos (32 \alpha)} & {-\sin (32 \alpha)} \\ {\sin (32 \alpha)} & {\cos (32 \alpha)}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {-1} \\ {1} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, cos (32 {tex}\alpha{/tex}) = 0 and sin (32 {tex}\alpha{/tex}) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 32 \alpha=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{64}{/tex}

230719.explanation:
Given matrix
{tex}P=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {3} & {1} & {0} \\ {9} & {3} & {1}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {3} & {0} & {0} \\ {9} & {3} & {0}\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P = X + I (let)
Now, P5 = (I + X)5
= I + 5C1 (X) + 5C2 (X2) + 5C3 (X3) + ...
[{tex}\because{/tex} In = I, I.A = A and (a + x)n = nC0an + nC1an-1x + ... + nCnxn]
Here, {tex}X^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {3} & {0} & {0} \\ {9} & {3} & {0}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {3} & {0} & {0} \\ {9} & {3} & {0}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {9} & {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
and {tex}X^{3}=X^{2} \cdot X=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {9} & {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {3} & {0} & {0} \\ {9} & {3} & {0}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow X^{4}=X^{5}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, {tex}P^{5}=I+5\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {3} & {0} & {0} \\ {9} & {3} & {0}\end{array}\right]+10\left[\begin{array}{lll}{0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {9} & {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {15} & {1} & {0} \\ {135} & {15} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
and {tex}Q=I+P^{5}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{2} & {0} & {0} \\ {15} & {2} & {0} \\ {135} & {15} & {2}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}{q_{i j}}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q21 = 15, q31 = 135 and q32 = 15
Hence, {tex}\frac{q_{21}+q_{31}}{q_{32}}=\frac{15+135}{15}=\frac{150}{15}=10{/tex}

230723.explanation:
Given, AAT = I
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{0} & {2 q} & {r} \\ {p} & {q} & {-r} \\ {p} & {-q} & {r}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{0} & {p} & {p} \\ {2 q} & {q} & {-q} \\ {r} & {-r} & {r}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{0+4 q^{2}+r^{2}} & {0+2 q^{2}-r^{2}} & {0-2 q^{2}+r^{2}} \\ {0+2 q^{2}-r^{2}} & {p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}} & {p^{2}-q^{2}-r^{2}} \\ {0-2 q^{2}+r^{2}} & {p^{2}-q^{2}-r^{2}} & {p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}}\end{array}\right|=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
We know that, if two matrices are equal, then corresponding elements are also equal, so
4q2 + r2 = 1 = p2 + q2 + r2, ...(i)
2q2 - r2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 2q2 ...(ii)
and p2 - q2 - r2 = 0
Using Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get
p2 = 3q2 ...(iv)
Using Eqs. (ii) and (iv) in Eq. (i), we get
4q2 + 2q2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6q2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2p2 = 1 [using Eq. (iv)]
{tex}p^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow|p|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

230733.explanation:
Here, {tex}P=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {4} & {1} & {0} \\ {16} & {4} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad P^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {4} & {1} & {0} \\ {16} & {4} & {1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {4} & {1} & {0} \\ {16} & {4} & {1}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {4+4} & {1} & {0} \\ {16+32} & {4+4} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {4 \times 2} & {1} & {0} \\ {16(1+2)} & {4 \times 2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}P^{3}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {4 \times 2} & {1} & {0} \\ {16(1+2)} & {4 \times 2} & {1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {4} & {1} & {0} \\ {16} & {4} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {4 \times 3} & {1} & {0} \\ {16(1+2+3)} & {4 \times 3} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}...(ii)
From symmetry,
{tex}P^{50}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {4 \times 50} & {1} & {0} \\ {16(1+2+3+\ldots+50)} & {4 \times 50} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} P50 - Q = I [given]
{tex}\therefore\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1-q_{11}} & {-q_{12}} & {-q_{13}} \\ {200-q_{21}} & {1-q_{22}} & {-q_{23}} \\ {16 \times \frac{50}{2}(51)-q_{31}} & {200-q_{32}} & {1-q_{33}}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 200-q_{21}=0, \frac{16 \times 50 \times 51}{2}-q_{31}=0{/tex}
200 - q32 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} q21 = 200, q32 = 200, q31 = 20400
Thus, {tex}\frac{q_{31}+q_{32}}{q_{21}}=\frac{20400+200}{200}=\frac{20600}{200}=103{/tex}

230859.explanation:
Given, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {2} & {2} \\ {2} & {1} & {-2} \\ {a} & {2} & {b}\end{array}\right], A^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {2} & {a} \\ {2} & {1} & {2} \\ {2} & {-2} & {b}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and
{tex}A A^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {2} & {2} \\ {2} & {1} & {-2} \\ {a} & {2} & {b}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {2} & {a} \\ {2} & {1} & {2} \\ {2} & {-2} & {b}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{9} & {0} & {a+4+2 b} \\ {0} & {9} & {2 a+2-2 b} \\ {a+4+2 b} & {2 a+2-2 b} & {a^{2}+4+b^{2}}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
It is given that, AAT = 9I
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{9} & {0} & {a+4+2 b} \\ {0} & {9} & {2 a+2-2 b} \\ {a+4+2 b} & {2 a+2-2 b} & {a^{2}+4+b^{2}}\end{array}\right]=9\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{9} & {0} & {a+4+2 b} \\ {0} & {9} & {2 a+2-2 b} \\ {a+4+2 b} & {2 a+2-2 b} & {a^{2}+4+b^{2}}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{9} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {9} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {9}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
On comparing, we get
a + 4 + 2b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + 2b = -4 ...(i)
2a + 2 - 2b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - b = -1 ...(ii)
and a2 + 4 + b2 = 9 ...(iii)
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
a = -2, b = -1
This satisfies Eq. (iii)
Hence, (a, b) {tex}\equiv{/tex} (-2, -1)

230884.explanation:
Now, {tex}P^{T} P=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\sqrt{3} / 2} & {-1 / 2} \\ {1 / 2} & {\sqrt{3} / 2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\sqrt{3} / 2} & {1 / 2} \\ {-1 / 2} & {\sqrt{3} / 2}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad P^{T} P=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PTP = I
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PT = P-1
Since, Q = PAPT
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PTQ2005 P = PT [(PAPT) (PAPT) ...2005 times] P
{tex}=\frac{\left(P^{T} P\right) A\left(P^{T} P\right) A\left(P^{T} P\right) \ldots\left(P^{T} P\right) A\left(P^{T} P\right)}{2005 \text { times }}{/tex}
{tex}=I A^{2005}=A^{2005}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {1} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}A^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {1} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {1} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}A^{3}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {1} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {3} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
and so on.....

230893.explanation:
Given, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{\alpha} & {0} \\ {1} & {1}\end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {5} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{\alpha} & {0} \\ {1} & {1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}{\alpha} & {0} \\ {1} & {1}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{\alpha^{2}} & {0} \\ {\alpha+1} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Also, given, A2 = B
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll}{\alpha^{2}} & {0} \\ {\alpha+1} & {1}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {5} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha^{2}=1{/tex} and {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1 = 5
Which is not possible at the same time
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No real values of {tex}\alpha{/tex} exists.
