269313.explanation:
{tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{array}\right) B=\left(\begin{array}{cc} a & 1 \\ b & -1 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}A+B=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 1+a & 0 \\ 2+b & -2 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}(A+B)^{2}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 1+a & 0 \\ 2+b & -2 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc} 1+a & 0 \\ 2+b & -2 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} (1+a)^{2} & 0 \\ (2+b)(1+a)-4-2 b & -4 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} (1+a)^{2} & 0 \\ 2+2 a+b+a b-4-2 b & 4 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} (1+a)^{2} & 0 \\ 2 a+a b-b-2 & 4 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}A^{2}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{B}^{2}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \mathbf{a} & \mathbf{1} \\ \mathbf{b} & -\mathbf{1} \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc} \mathbf{a} & \mathbf{1} \\ \mathbf{b} & -\mathbf{1} \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} a^{2}+b & a-1 \\ a b-b & b+1 \end{array}\right){/tex}
Given that; (A + B)2 = (A2 + B2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \left(\begin{array}{cc} (1+a)^{2} & 0 \\ 2 a+a b-b-2 & 4 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right)+\left(\begin{array}{cc} a^{2}+b & a-1 \\ a b-b & b+1 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} -1+a^{2}+b & a-1 \\ a b-b & b \end{array}\right){/tex}
By comparison
a - 1 = 0
a = 1
b = 4

269410.explanation:
Given = {tex}\int(2 x+3)^{5}{/tex}
Let, 2x + 3 = z
⇒ 2dx = dz
So,
{tex}\int(2 x+3)^{5} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{z^{5}}{2} d z{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{z^{6}}{6}+c{/tex} where c is the integrating constant.
{tex}=\frac{z^{6}}{12}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(2 x+3)^{6}}{12}+c{/tex}

269420.explanation:
Given = {tex}\int{/tex}(3-5 x)7
Let, 3 – 5x = z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -5dx = dz
So,
{tex}\int{/tex}(3-5 x)7 dx
{tex}=-\int \frac{z^{7}}{5} d z{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{5} \frac{z^{8}}{8}+c{/tex} where c is the integrating constant.
{tex}=-\frac{z^{8}}{40}+c{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{(3-5 x)^{8}}{40}+c{/tex}

269424.explanation:
Given integral is {tex}\int \frac{1}{(2-3 x)^{4}}{/tex}
Let, 2 – 3x = z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3dx = dz
So, {tex}\int \frac{1}{(2-3 x)^{4}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{1}{z^{4}}\left(\frac{d z}{-3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d z}{z^{4}}{/tex}    where, c is the integrating constant.
{tex}=-\frac{1}{3} \int z^{-4} \mathrm{d} z{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{3} \frac{z^{-3}}{-3}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{9(2-3 x)^{3}}+c{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\int \frac{1}{(2-3 x)^{4}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{9(2-3 x)^{3}}+c{/tex}

269430.explanation:
Given integral is {tex}\int \sqrt{a x+b}{/tex}
Let, ax + b = z2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} adx = 2zdz
So,
{tex}\int \sqrt{a x+b} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int z \frac{2 z d z}{a}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{a} \int z^{2} d z{/tex}    where, c is the integrating constant.
{tex}=\frac{2}{a} \frac{z^{3}}{3}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3 a} z^{3}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2(a x+b)^{3 / 2}}{3 a}+c{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\int \sqrt{a x+b}{/tex} = {tex}=\frac{2(a x+b)^{3 / 2}}{3 a}+c{/tex}.

Which is the required solution.

dx = dt

269542.explanation:
{tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x}{/tex}{tex}=\int \frac{1}{\cos ^{4} x\left(\tan ^{4} x+1\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{\sec ^{4} x}{\tan ^{4} x+1} d x{/tex}

{tex}=\int \frac{1+t^{-2}}{t^{2}+t^{-2}} d t{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{1+t^{-2}}{t^{2}+t^{-2}+2-2} d t{/tex}

Let t - t-1 = u
1 + x-2dt = dt
{tex}=\int \frac{d u}{(u)^{2}+\sqrt{2}^{2}}{/tex}
We know, {tex}\int \frac{1}{x^{2}+a^{2}}=\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{a}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{u}{\sqrt{2}}+c{/tex}
put u = t - t-1
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{t-t^{-1}}{\sqrt{2}}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{t^{2}-1}{\sqrt{2} t}+c{/tex}
put t = tan x
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{\tan ^{2} x-1}{\sqrt{2} \tan x}+c{/tex}

269546.explanation:
Given integal is: I = {tex}\int {/tex} cosec 3(2 x+1) cot (2 x+1) d x=?
So,
 I ={tex}\int {/tex} cosec 3(2 x+1) cot (2 x+1) dx
={tex}\int {/tex}cosec 2(2 x+1) cosec (2 x+1) cot (2 x+1) dx
Let, cosec(2x + 1) = z
⇒ -2cosec(2x + 1)cot(2x + 1)dx = dz
{tex}\therefore I =\int {/tex}cosec2(2 x+1) cosec (2 x+1)cot (2 x+1) dx
{tex}=\int z^{2} \frac{d z}{-2}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{z^{3}}{3}+c{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\ cosec ^{3}(2 x+1)}{6}+c{/tex}
where c is the integrating constant.

269548.explanation:
{tex}\int \frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{4}+x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} 
{tex}=\int \frac{1+x^{-2}}{x^{2}+1+x^{-2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{1+x^{-2}}{x^{2}+1+x^{-2}+2-2} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{1+x^{-2}}{\left(x-x^{-1}\right)^{2}+3} d x{/tex}
Let x - x-1 = t
1 + x2 dx = dt
{tex}\therefore I =\int \frac{d t}{(t)^{2}+\sqrt{3}^{2}}{/tex}
We know, {tex}\int \frac{1}{x^{2}+a^{2}}=\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{a}+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{t}{\sqrt{3}}+c{/tex}
put t = x - x-1
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x-x^{-1}}{\sqrt{3}}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{3} x}+c{/tex}

269550.explanation:
Given integral is: I = {tex}\int \frac{\tan \left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Let, sin-1x = z
{tex} \Rightarrow\frac{d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=d z{/tex}
So,
{tex}I =\int \frac{\tan \left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
={tex}\int{/tex} tan z dz
=log |sec z|+c
=log |sec (sin -1 x)|+c
where c is the integrating constant.

269551.explanation:
{tex}I =\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}=\int \frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{\cos ^{4} x\left(\tan ^{4} x+1\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{2 \tan x \sec ^{2} x}{\left(\tan ^{2} x\right)^{2}+1} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{2 \tan x \sec ^{2} x}{\left(\sec ^{2} x-1\right)^{2}+1} d x{/tex}
Let sec2x - 1 = t
2 sec x sec x tan x dx = dt
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{d t}{(t)^{2}+1}{/tex}
We know, {tex}\int \frac{1}{x^{2}+a^{2}}=\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{a}+c{/tex}
I = tan-1t + C
put t = sec2 x - 1
= tan-1 sec2x - 1 + c
= tan-1 tan2x + c

269552.explanation:
Given integral is {tex}\int \frac{\tan (\log x)}{x}{/tex}
Let, logx = z
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x} d x=d z{/tex}
So,
{tex}\int \frac{\tan (\log x)}{x} d x{/tex}
={tex}\int{/tex} tan zdz
=log |sec z|+c

= - log |cos (log x)| + c   where c is the integrating constant.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\int \frac{\tan (\log x)}{x} d x{/tex} =  - log |cos (log x)| + c

269554.explanation:

 ={tex} \int \frac{d x}{(1)^{2}-(3 x)^{2}}{/tex}
Let t = 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dt = 3dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}d x=\frac{d t}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1^{2}-(t)^{2}}{/tex}
We know, {tex}\int \frac{1}{a^{2}-x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 a} \log \frac{a+x}{a-x}+c{/tex}

put t = 3x

269557.explanation:
f(x) = x2 + x + 1
One-one function
Let p, q be two arbitrary elements in N
Then, f(p) = f(q)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 + p + 1 = q2 + q + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 - q2 + p - q = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (p - q) (p + q + 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = q, p + q + 1 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 ({tex}\because{/tex} p, q {tex}\in{/tex} N)
When f(p) = f(q), p = q
thus, f(x) is one-one function.
Onto function
For x = 1, f(x) assumes value 3.
As, f(x) cannot assume value less than 3, for x {tex}\in{/tex} N
Thus, f(x) is not onto function. It is into function.

269559.explanation:
Consider: I = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(4)^{2}-(2 x)^{2}}{/tex}
Let 2x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2dx = dt
{tex}\Rightarrow d x=\frac{d t}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{4^{2}-(t)^{2}}{/tex}
We know, {tex}\int \frac{1}{a^{2}-x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 a} \log \frac{a+x}{a-x}+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{16} \log \frac{4+t}{4-t}+c{/tex}
put t = 2x
{tex}=\frac{1}{16} \log \frac{4+2 x}{4-2 x}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{16} \log| \frac{2+x}{2-x}|+c{/tex}.

Which is the required solution.

269579.explanation: Given: I = {tex}\int \frac{e^{x}(1+x)}{\cos ^{2}\left(x e^{x}\right)} d x{/tex} Let xe x = z {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} e x (1 + x)dx = dz So, {tex}\int \frac{e^{x}(1+x)}{\cos ^{2}\left(x e^{x}\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{d z}{\cos ^{2} z}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} sec 2 zdz = tan z + c = tan (xe x ) + c where c is the integrating constant.

269581.explanation:
one-one: Let f(m) = f(n)
Three cases arise

  1. m and n are both odd
    clearly, f(m) = f(n) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}(m+1)=\frac{1}{2}(m+1){/tex}
    = m + n
  2. m and n are both even
    in this case f(m) = f(n) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{m}{2}+\frac{n}{2} \Rightarrow m=n{/tex}
  3. m is even n is odd
    in this case f(m) = f(n)
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{n}{2}=\frac{1}{2}(n+1){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow x \neq 3{/tex}
    Hence f is not one-one.

Onto: let n {tex}\in{/tex} N (co-domai Now f(m)) = n
i.e. {tex}m=\left\{\begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{2}(m+1), \quad \text { in is } odd \\ \frac{m}{2}, \quad m \text { is even } \end{array}\right.{/tex}
So, for every n is co-domain. These exists m is domain r.t
n = f(m)
So, f is onto
Hence f is many-one onto function.
f: N {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} N: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{2}(n+1), \text { when } n \text { is odd } \\ \frac{n}{2}, \text { when } n \text { is even. } \end{array}\right.{/tex} 
One-One function

When n is odd When n is even
f(1) = 1 f(2) = 1
f(3) = 2 f(4) = 2

It is clear from the above that the function is many-one  and Onto function

269582.explanation:
{tex}I=-\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+2 x-3}{/tex}
Completing the square
x2 + 2x - 3 = x2 + 2x - 3 + 1 - 1
{tex}={/tex} (x + 1)2 - 4
{tex}\therefore I=-\int \frac{d x}{(x+1)^{2}-4}{/tex}
Let x + 1 = t
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = dt
{tex}\therefore I=-\int \frac{d t}{t^{2}-2^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=-\int \frac{d t}{2^{2}-t^{2}}{/tex}
We know, {tex}\int \frac{1}{a^{2}-x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 a} \log \frac{a+x}{a-x}+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{4} \log \frac{2+t}{2-t}+c{/tex}
put t = x + 1
{tex}=\frac{1}{4} \log \frac{2+x+1}{2-x-1}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4} \log \frac{x+3}{1-x}+c{/tex}

269585.explanation:

We know, {tex}\int \frac{1}{a^{2}-x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 a} \log \frac{a+x}{a-x}+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}} \log \frac{1+t}{1-t}+c{/tex}
Put {tex}t=\sqrt{3} \tan x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}} \log \frac{1+\sqrt{3} \tan x}{1-\sqrt{3} \tan x}+c{/tex}

269587.explanation:

269596.explanation:
Given integral is:
{tex}\int \frac{d x}{e^{x}-1}{/tex}
{tex}=-\int \frac{-1+e^{x}-e^{x}}{e^{x}-1} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\int \frac{e^{x}-1}{e^{x}-1} d x+\int \frac{e^{x}}{e^{x}-1} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\int \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\int \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}-1} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex}
{tex}=-\int \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\int \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}-1} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex}
Let, ex – 1 = z
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} exdx = dz
So,
{tex}-\int \mathrm{d} x+\int \frac{e^{x}}{e^{x}-1} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-x+\int \frac{d z}{z}{/tex}
= -x + log |z| + c
= -x + log |ex - 1| + c
where c is the integrating constant.

269597.explanation:

269600.explanation:

269609.explanation:
On dividing Nr and Dr by x2, we get

269611.explanation:

 {tex} \Rightarrow\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{x}{2} d x=d z{/tex}

269615.explanation:
Given
{tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{1+\sin x} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int d x-\int \frac{d x}{1+\sin x}{/tex}
{tex}=x-\int \frac{d x}{\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{/tex}

{tex}=x-\int \frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{x}{2} d x}{\left(\tan \frac{x}{2}+1\right)^{2}}{/tex}

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD, we have

{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{C D}=2 \vec{b}-(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{C D}=\vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex}

270409.explanation:
Given that, {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \vec{a}+\vec{b}=(1+3) \hat{i}+(4-3) \hat{j} {/tex}
{tex} =4 \hat{i}+\hat{j} {/tex}
{tex} |\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4^2+1^2}=\sqrt{16+1}=\sqrt{17} {/tex}

270413.explanation:
Consider A as origin,
The position vectors of {tex}\vec{AA}{/tex},{tex}\vec{BA}\ and \ \vec{CA} {/tex} are  {tex}\vec{0}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} respectively.
the position vector of centroid G is {tex}\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A G=\frac{\vec{a}+\vec{b}}{3}{/tex} as A is the origin.

270415.explanation:
Given ABCDEF is a regular hexagon

{tex}A D=2 \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{FC}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{E B}=2 \overrightarrow{F A}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{EB}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{FA}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{EB}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AO}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{FA}}{/tex} {tex}(\because \overrightarrow{B C}=\overrightarrow{A O}){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{EB}}=2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AO}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{FA}}){/tex}
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOF,
{tex}\overrightarrow{F A}+\overrightarrow{A O}+\overrightarrow{F O}=0{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{FA}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AO}}=-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{FO}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{EB}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{EB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{FC}}=4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}{/tex}

270440.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
Integrating on both sides,
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{y} = \int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
log |y| = log |x| + log k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = k x

270441.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\cos x\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}  + y sin x = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} + \frac{y \sin x}{\cos x} = \frac{1}{\cos x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + ytan x = sec x
Comparing with{tex}dy \over dx{/tex} + Py = Q
P = tan x, Q = sec x
Integrating factor = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{Pdx}} = \mathrm{e}^{\int \tan \mathrm{xdx}} = \mathrm{e}^{\log \sec \mathrm{x}} = \sec x{/tex}

270442.explanation:
We know that,
The degree is the power of the highest order derivative.
The highest order is 2 and its power is 2.
Hence, the degree of a differential equation is 2.

270443.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\left\{5+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right\}^{\frac{5}{3}}=x^{5}\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left\{5+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right\}^{5}=\left\{x^{5}\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)\right\}^{3}{/tex}
Degree is 3.
Therefore none of the given options are matching with answer.

270444.explanation:
We have,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = cosec x
Comparing with {tex}dy \over dx{/tex} + Py = Q of the above equation then, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P = cot x, Q = cosec x
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x} = e^{\int \cot x d x} = e^{\log \sin x}{/tex} = sin x
Multiplying on both sides by sin x
sin x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cos x = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(y \sin x){/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\int 1 d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = x + C

270448.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 y}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{2 y} = \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d y}{2 y} = \int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log |y| = 2 \log |x|+\log c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log |y| = \log x^{2}+\log c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = {tex}log \left(x^{2} c\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cx2
Tangent passing through (1,1) ,  c = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x2

270449.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a x+g}{b y+f}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}(by + f) dy = (ax + g) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{b y^{2}}{2} + f y = \frac{a x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + gx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}by^{2} + 2fy = ax^{2}{/tex} + 2 gx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} by2 + 2fy - a x2 - 2gx - c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - ax2 + by2 - 2gx + 2fy - c = 0
This is general equation for the circle if coefficients of x2 and y2 are equal.
- a = b
a = - b

270450.explanation:
We have,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 y}{x}=0\\{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{2 y}{x}\\q{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{2 y}=-\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d y}{2 y}=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log |y|{/tex}  = -log |x| + log c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{y} x{/tex} = c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx2 = c
Given that y(1) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y x2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}

270451.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y(x+1)}{x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y(x+1)}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y}=\frac{x+1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int \frac{x+1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = x + log x + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = x + c
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=e^{x+c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = xex+c

270452.explanation:
The differential equation contains only one constant.
Therefore, Order of differential equation is 1.

270453.explanation:
We have ,
y1y3 = y22
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{d y}{d x}}{\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}}=\frac{\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}}{\frac{d^{3} y}{d x^{3}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}}{\frac{d y}{d x}}=\frac{\frac{d^{3} y}{d x^{3}}}{\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}}{\frac{d y}{d x}}=\int \frac{\frac{d^{3} y}{d x^{3}}}{\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{d y}{d x}=\log \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\log C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{C} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int C d x=\int \frac{\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{\frac{d y}{d x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{Cx}+\mathrm{C}_{1}=\log \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = eCx + C1
{tex}\Rightarrow \int d y=\int e^{C x+C} 1 d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=C_{4} e^{C_{5} x}+C_{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=c_{1} e^{c_{2} x}+c_{3}{/tex}

270454.explanation:
We have,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y g'(x) = g'(x)
Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + P = Q
P = Q = g'(x)
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int g'(x) d x} = e^{g(x)}{/tex}
Multiplying by eg(x) on both sides,
{tex}e^{g(x)}\left(\frac{d y}{d x} + y g'(x)\right) = e^{g(x)} g(x) g'(x){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d}{d x} y e^{g(x)} = e^{g(x)} g(x) g'(x) {/tex}
{tex}\int \mathrm{d} y \mathrm{e}^{\mathbf{g}}(x) {/tex} = {tex}\int e^{\mathbf{g}(x)} g(x) g'(x) \mathrm{d}{/tex} x + c
Put g(x) = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g'(x) dx = dt
yeg(x) = {tex}\int e^{t}t dt{/tex} + c
{tex}y e^{g(x)} = t e^{t}-e^{t} + c{/tex}
{tex}y e^{g(x)} = g(x) e^{g(x)}-e^{g(x)} + c{/tex}
{tex}[y-1-g(x)] e^{g(x)}{/tex} = c
y - 1 - g(x) = ce - g(x)
Taking log on both sides,
log [y - 1 - g(x)] = K - g(x) ({tex}\because{/tex} log C = k)
g(x) + log [y - 1 - g(x)] = K

270455.explanation:
We have,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1+x+y^{2}+x y^{2} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(1+x)\left(1+y^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=(1+x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=\int(1+x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=\int(1+x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} y=x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}+c{/tex}
Given  y(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} x = 0, y = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\tan ^{-1} y=x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y =  {tex}\tan \left(x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right){/tex}

270457.explanation:
We have ,

{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}=y+x \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} .....(I)
Put {tex}y \over x{/tex} = v
y = vx
Differentiating on both sides,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}v+\tan v=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...from (i)
{tex}\tan v=x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{x}=\cot v d v{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}=\int \cot v d v{/tex}
log |x| + log C = log |sin v|
Cx = sin v
{tex}\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = c x

270460.explanation:
We have ,

{tex}\frac{d z}{d x}+\frac{z}{x} \log z=\frac{z}{x^{2}}(\log z)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d z}{d x}=\frac{z}{x^{2}}(\log z)^{2}-\frac{z}{x} \log z{/tex} ....(i)
Put v = (logz)-1
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-1}{(\log z)^{2}} \frac{1}{z} \frac{d z}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d z}{d x}=-z(\log z)^{2} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ....(ii)
From (i) and (ii)
{tex}-z(\log z)^{2} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{z}{x^{2}}(\log z)^{2}-\frac{z}{x} \log z{/tex}
{tex}(\log z) \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x^{2}}(\log z)+\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x^{2}}+\frac{u}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}-\frac{u}{x}=-\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
P(x) = {tex}-1 \over x{/tex}, q (x) = {tex}-1 \over x^2{/tex}
Given differential equation can be reduced.
using v = (log z)-1

270461.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{\phi\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\phi^{\prime}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} ....(i)
Put v = {tex}y \over x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}+v=\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}+v=v+\frac{\phi(v)}{\phi^{\prime}(v)}{/tex} .. from (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{\phi(v)}{\phi^{\prime}(v)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\phi^{\prime}(v)}{\phi(v)} \mathrm{d} v=\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\phi^{\prime}(v)}{\phi(v)} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\phi {/tex}(v) = log |x|+log k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\phi\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} - log |x| = log k
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left[\frac{\phi\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x}\right]=\log k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \phi\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=k x{/tex}

270463.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\left(y_{2}\right)^{5}+\frac{4\left(y_{2}\right)^{3}}{y_{3}}+y_{3}=x^{2}-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y_{3}\left(y_{2}\right)^{5}+4\left(y_{2}\right)^{3}+y_{3}^{2}=\left(x^{2}-1\right) y_{3}{/tex}
Here, highest order derivative is 3 = m
power of 3rd order derivative is 2 = n.

270465.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+1=e^{x+y} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}-1{/tex} ...(1)
Let x + y = v 
{tex}\Rightarrow 1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{d} v}{\mathrm{dx}} {/tex} ....from (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = e-v dv
{tex}\Rightarrow \int d x=\int e^{-v} d v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -e-v + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - c = -e-(x + y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - c)ex + y + 1 = 0

270467.explanation:
We have ,

{tex}x^{2}+y^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=4{/tex}
{tex}y^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=4-x^{2}{/tex}
{tex}y^{2} d y=\left(4-x^{2}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int y^{2} d y=\int\left(4-x^{2}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y^{3}}{3}=4 x-\frac{x^{3}}{3} {/tex} + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y3 + 12x - x3 + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x3 + y3 = 12x + C

270469.explanation:
Draw ON, the perpendicular to the lien AB

Let {tex}\vec{i}{/tex} be the unit vector along ON
The resultant force {tex}\vec{R}=3 \overrightarrow{O A}+5 \overrightarrow{O B}{/tex} 
The angles between {tex}\vec{i}{/tex} and the forces {tex}\vec{R}, 3 \overrightarrow{O A}, 5 \overrightarrow{O B}{/tex} are {tex}\angle \mathrm{CON}, \angle \mathrm{AON}, \angle \mathrm{BON}{/tex} respectively.
{tex}\Rightarrow R \cos \angle C O P=3 O A{/tex}{tex}\times \frac{\mathrm{OP}}{\mathrm{OA}}+5 \mathrm{OB} \times \frac{\mathrm{OP}}{\mathrm{OB}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{R}{O D}=3+5{/tex}
{tex}R=8 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CA}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}}=3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{CA}}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CB}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}}=5 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}+5 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{CB}}{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\vec{R}=8 \overrightarrow{O C}+3 \overrightarrow{C A}+5 \overrightarrow{C B}{/tex}
{tex}8 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}=3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{CA}}+5 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{CB}}{/tex}
{tex}|\overrightarrow{3 C A}|=|\overrightarrow{5 C B}|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3CA = 5CB

270470.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD, 
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BD}}{/tex}
Given that {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}{/tex}
And {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BD}}{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AE}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AE}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}{/tex}

270473.explanation:
We have,

xdx + ydy = x2y dy - y2x dx
x dx + y2x dx = x2y dy - y dy
{tex}x\left(1 + y^{2}\right) dx=y\left(x^{2} - 1\right) dy{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x dx}{x^{2} - 1}=\frac{y dy}{1 + y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac{x dx}{x^{2} - 1}=\int \frac{y dy}{1 + y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 x dx}{x^{2} - 1}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 y dy}{1 + y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left(x^{2} - 1\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1 + y^{2}\right) + \log c{/tex}
{tex}\log \left(x^{2} - 1\right)=\log \left(1 + y^{2}\right) + \log c{/tex}
x2 - 1 = {tex}\left(1 + y^{2}\right) c{/tex}

270474.explanation:
We have,

{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=x{/tex}
Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - Py = Q
{tex}\Rightarrow P=\frac{-1}{x}, Q=x{/tex}
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \frac{-1}{x} d x}=e^{-\log x}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
Multiplying {tex}1 \over x {/tex} on both sides,
{tex}\frac{1}{x} \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d}{d x} \frac{y}{x}=1{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac d{dx} \frac{y}{x}=\int x d x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{y}{x}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+c{/tex}
2y = x3 + cx
2y - x3 - cx=0

270476.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-k y=0{/tex}
{tex}dy \over dx{/tex} = ky 
{tex}dy \over dx{/tex} = kdx
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{y}=k \int d x{/tex}
log |y| = kx + c
y(0) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, y = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |y| = kx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ekx = y 
Given that {tex}e ^{k\infty}= 0{/tex}
as {tex}e ^{-\infty}= 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k < 0

270477.explanation:
we have,

{tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+1+y^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(1+y^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=-\frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=-\int \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
tan-1 y = -tan-1 x + tan-1 C
tan-1 y + tan-1 x = tan-1 C

270479.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+x y+y^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1+\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}} {/tex} ...(i)
Let y = vx
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}1+v+v^{2}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ....from(i)
{tex}1+v^{2}=x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{x}=\frac{d v}{1+v^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}=\int \frac{d v}{1+v^{2}}{/tex}
log |x| = tan-1x + c

270481.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q y^{n}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y^{-n} \frac{d y}{d x}+P y^{1-n}=Q{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y^{-n} \frac{d y}{d x}+P y^{1-n}=Q{/tex}
Let y1-n = v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=(1-n) v \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1-n} \frac{d v}{d x}+P v=Q{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}+P v(1-n)=Q(1-n){/tex}
which is linear form.
z = {tex}y^(1-n){/tex} can be reduced to linear form.

270482.explanation:
We have,

{tex}y \frac{d y}{d x}+x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x y{/tex} = cos X
Highest derivative is 2 = p and degree is 1 = q 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p > q.

270484.explanation:
We have,
{tex}(x \log x) \frac{d y}{d x}+y=2 \log x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x \log x} y=\frac{2}{x} {/tex}
Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = Py = Q
{tex}P=\frac{1}{x \log x}, Q=\frac{2}{x} {/tex}
I.F. = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x \log x} d x{/tex} = elog(log x) = log x

270485.explanation:
We have,

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + ysec x = tan x
Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q
P = sec x, Q = tan x 
{tex}\text { I. } F \text { . }=e^{\int \sec x d x}=e^{\log (\sec x+\tan x)}=\sec x+\tan x{/tex}

(Multiply by {tex}\sec \frac{x}{2}{/tex} in numerator and denominator)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\int \tan ^{-1} \frac{1+\tan \frac{x}{2}}{1-\tan \frac{x}{2}} d x{/tex}={tex}\int \tan ^{-1} \frac{\tan \frac{\pi}{4}+\tan \frac{x}{2}}{\tan \frac{\pi}{4}-\tan \frac{\pi}{4} \tan \frac{x}{2}} d x{/tex}={tex}\int \tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\int\left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right) d x=\frac{\pi x}{4}+\frac{x^{2}}{4}+c{/tex}

{tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} = 7 = {tex}|\vec a| |\vec b| sin \theta{/tex} 
{tex}2.5 \sin \theta{/tex} = 8
sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}

271100.explanation:
We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex}
Let y = vx
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x^{2} v^{2}-x^{2}}{2 v x^{2}}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}-v=x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{-v^{2}-1}{2 v}=x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{x}+\frac{2 v d v}{v^{2}+1}=0{/tex}
On integrating on both sides, we obtain
log x + log(v2 + 1) = C
log(x(v2 + 1) = C
{tex}x\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+1\right)=c{/tex}
y2 + x2 = Cx

271101.explanation:
We have, {tex}x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2}+x y+y^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1+\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex}
Let y = vx
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}1+v+v^{2}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}1+v^{2}=x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{x}=\frac{d v}{v^{2}+1}{/tex}
On integrating on both sides, we obtain
log x = tan-1v + C
{tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\log x+c{/tex}

271106.explanation:
Given DE: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}=y+x \tan \frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Now,Dividing both sides by x, we obtain{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\tan \frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Let y = v x Differentiating both sides,we get
{tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} = v + x{tex}\frac {dv}{dx}{/tex}
Now, our differential equation becomes,
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\tan v{/tex}
On separating the variables, we obtain{tex}\frac{d v}{\tan v}=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get, sin v = Cx
Substituting the value of v we get, {tex}\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=C x{/tex}

271107.explanation:

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex}
let y = vx
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x^{2} v^{2}-x^{2}}{2 v x^{2}}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}-v=x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{-v^{2}-1}{2 v}=x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{x}+\frac{2 v d v}{v^{2}+1}=0{/tex}
On integrating on both sides, we obtain
log x + log(v2 + 1) = log C
log(x(v2 + 1)) = log C
{tex}x\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+1\right)=C{/tex}
y2 + x2 = C.x

271122.explanation:
Here, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} = 0,
Take its dot product with {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} , we have
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} =  0
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec b{/tex} = 0
{tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex} = 0
Adding these three, we get
{tex}|a|^{2}{/tex} + {tex}2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec b){/tex} + {tex}|\vec b|^2{/tex} + {tex}2(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}){/tex} + {tex}|\vec c|^2{/tex} + {tex}2(\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12 + {tex}2[(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})+(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c})+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})]{/tex} + 12 + 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2[(\vec a \cdot \vec b)+(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c})+(\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})]{/tex} = -3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-3}{2}{/tex}

271153.explanation:
Direction ratios are given implies that we can write the parallel vector towards that line, lets consider first parallel vector to be {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}{/tex} and second parallel vector be {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
For the angle, we can use the formula {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{\overrightarrow{a} .\vec{b}}{|\vec{a}| \times|\vec{b}|}{/tex}
For that, we need to find the magnitude of these vectors
{tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}+(-6)^{2}}{/tex}
= 7
{tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=\sqrt{1+2^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex}
= 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{(3 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{7 \times 3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{3+4-12}{21}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{-5}{21}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\alpha=\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{5}{21}\right){/tex}
The negative sign does not affect anything in cosine as cosine is positive in the fourth quadrant.
{tex}\alpha=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{21}\right){/tex}

271157.explanation:
Let's consider the first parallel vector to be {tex}\vec{a}=a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+c \hat{k}{/tex} and second parallel vector be {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=(\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a}) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
For the angle, we can use the formula {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}| \times|\vec{b}|}{/tex}
For that, we need to find the magnitude of these vectors
{tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+(c)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=\sqrt{(\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c})^{2}+(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a})^{2}+(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b})^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{2\left(a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}-a b-b c-c a\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{(a \hat{\imath}+b \hat{\jmath}+c \hat{k}) \cdot((b-c) \hat{\imath}+(c-a) \hat{\jmath}+(a-b) \hat{k})}{\sqrt{2\left(a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}-a b-b c-c a\right)} \times \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{a b-a c+b c-b a+c a-c b}{\sqrt{2\left(a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}-a b-b c-c a\right)} \times \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{0}{\sqrt{2\left(a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}-a b-b c-c a\right)} \times \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\cos ^{-1}(0){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} or {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x^{2}} \times y{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x^{2}} \times e^{\frac{1}{x}}{/tex}

271277.explanation: Given ,y = f(x) = x x Taking log both sides, we obtain log e y = x {tex}\times{/tex} log x(-1) (Since log a b c = c log a b) Differentiating (i) with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=x \times \frac{1}{x}+\log _{e} x \times 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=y \times\left(1+\log _{e} x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}(x){/tex} = x x (1 + log e x)

271281.explanation:
Let y = f(x) = xsinx
Taking log both sides, we obtain
loge y = sin x loge x -(1) (Since loga bc = c loga b)
Differentiating (i) with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin x \times \frac{1}{x}+\log _{e} x \times \cos x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=y \times\left(\frac{\sin x}{x}+\log _{e} x \cos x\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}(x){/tex}{tex}=x^{sinx}\left(\frac{\sin x+x \log x \sin x}{x}\right){/tex}.
Which is the required solution.

271284.explanation:
Let y = f(x) {tex}=x^{\sqrt{x}}{/tex}
Taking log both sides, we obtain
loge y {tex}=\sqrt{x} \times \log _{e} x-(1){/tex}
(Since loga bc = c loga b)
Differentiating (i) with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{x} \times \frac{1}{x}+\log _{e} x \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=y \times\left(\frac{2+\log _{e} x}{2 \sqrt{x}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}(x){/tex}
{tex}=x^{\sqrt{x}}\left(\frac{2+\log _{e} x}{2 \sqrt{x}}\right){/tex}

271287.explanation:
Given that  {tex}y=e^{\sin \sqrt{x}}{/tex}
Taking log both sides, we obtain
{tex}\log _{e} y=\sin \sqrt{x}{/tex}
(Since loga bc = c loga b)
Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos \sqrt{x} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}
Or
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos \sqrt{x} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} \times y{/tex}
Therefore {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{e^{\sin \sqrt{x}} \cos \sqrt{x}}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}

271290.explanation:
Given that y = (tan x)cot x
Taking log both sides, we obtain
loge y = cot x {tex}\times{/tex} loge tan x (Since loga bc = cloga b)
Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\cot x \times \frac{1}{\tan x} \times \sec ^{2} x-\log _{e} {/tex} tanx {tex}\times{/tex} cosec2x = cosec2x (1 - loge tanx)
Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} cosec2x (1 - loge tan {tex}\times{/tex} y) = cosec2x (1 - loge tanx) (tanx)cotx

271304.explanation:
Given that y = (sinx) logex
Taking log both sides, we obtain
loge y = loge x {tex}\times{/tex} loge sinx (Since logabc = c loga b)
Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\log _{e} x \times \frac{1}{\sin x} \times \cos x{/tex}{tex}+\log _{e} \sin x \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{x \cot x \log _{e} x+\log _{e} \sin x}{x}{/tex}
Therefore {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x \cot x \log _{e} x+\log _{e} \sin x}{x} \times y{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{x \cot x \log _{e} x+\log _{e} \sin x}{x}(\sin x)^{\log _{e} x}{/tex}

271317.explanation: Given that xy = e x + y Taking log both sides, we get log e xy = x + y (Since log a b c = clog a b) Since log a bc = log a b + log a c, we get log e x + log e y = x + y Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=1+\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} Or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}\left(\frac{y-1}{y}\right)=\frac{1-x}{x}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y(1-x)}{x(y-1)}{/tex}

271318.explanation: Given that {tex}\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{y}=\sqrt{a}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{y}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} Or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\sqrt{\frac{y}{x}}{/tex}

271319.explanation: given that x y = y y Taking log both sides, we obtain y log e x = c log e y (Since log a b c = c log a b) Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{y}{x}+\log _{e} x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log _{e} y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-y \log _{e} x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{y-x \log _{e} y}{x}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y\left(y-x \log _{e} y\right)}{x\left(x-y \log _{e} x\right)}{/tex}

271321.explanation:
Given that xpyq = (x + y)p + q
Taking log both sides, we obtain
logexpyq = (P + q) loge (x + y)
(Since logabc = c loga b)
Since loga bc = loga b + loga c, we get
logexp + logeyq = (p + q) loge (x + y)
p loge x + q logey  =  (p + q) loge (x + y)
Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\frac{p}{x}+\frac{q}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{p+q}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\frac{x q-y p}{y(x+y)}\right)=\frac{x q-y p}{x(x+y)}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex}

271326.explanation:
Given that y {tex}=\log _{e}(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}){/tex}
Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}} \times 2 x\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}{/tex}{tex} \times \frac{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}{/tex}

271329.explanation:
Given that y {tex}=\log _{e} \frac{1+\sqrt{x}}{1-\sqrt{x}}{/tex}
Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-\sqrt{x}}{1+\sqrt{x}}{/tex}{tex}\times \frac{(1-\sqrt{x}) \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}-(1+\sqrt{x}) \times-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}}{(1-\sqrt{x})^{2}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{1}{(1-x) \sqrt{x}}{/tex}

271331.explanation:
Given that y {tex}=\log _{e}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x}\right){/tex}
Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+x}{/tex}{tex}\times \frac{(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x) \times\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1+x^{2}}} \times 2 x+1\right)-(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+x) \times\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1+x^{2}}} \times 2 x-1\right)}{(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x)^{2}}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{/tex}

271332.explanation: Consider {tex}y=2 e^{x^4} \log x{/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(2 {e}^{{x}^4} \log {x}\right){/tex} Differentiating w.r.t {tex}x{/tex} by using chain rule, we get {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=2\left(\log x \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x^4}\right)+e^{x^4} \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=2\left(\log x \cdot e^{x^4} \frac{d}{d x} x^4+e^{x^4} \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=2\left(\log x \cdot e^{x^4} \cdot 4 x^3+e^{x^4} \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{x^4}\left(4 x^3 \log x+\frac{1}{x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(2 e^{x^4}\left(4 x^4 \log x+1\right)\right)}{x} {/tex}

271335.explanation: Given that y = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} Using 1 - cos x = 2sin 2 {tex}\frac {x} {2}{/tex} and Using sin x = 2 sin x {tex}\frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}{/tex} , we obtain y = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}\right){/tex} or y = {tex}\tan ^{-1} \tan \frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{x}{2}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

271336.explanation: Given that {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\right){/tex} Using {tex}\cos x=\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\sin x=2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta=1{/tex} Therefore, {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)}{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\cos \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}-\sin \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}}{\cos \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}+\sin \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}}{/tex} Dividing by cos {tex}\frac {x} {2}{/tex} in numerator and denominator, we get {tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \frac{1-\tan \frac{x}{2}}{1+\tan \frac{x}{2}}{/tex} Using {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)=\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}{/tex} , we obtain {tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

271337.explanation:
Given that y = {tex}\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1-\cos x}{1+\cos x}}{/tex}
Using 1 - cos x = 2 sin2 {tex}\frac {x} {2}{/tex} and 1 + cos x = 2 cos2 {tex}\frac {x} {2}{/tex}, we obtain
{tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}}{/tex}{tex}=\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)=\frac{x}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

271338.explanation:
Given that y = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\operatorname{acos} x-b \sin x}{b \cos x+a \sin x}\right){/tex}

Using tan (A - B) {tex}=\frac{\tan A-\tan B}{1+\tan A \tan B}{/tex}, we obtain
y = tan-1 tan {tex}(\alpha-x)=\alpha-x{/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1} \frac{a}{b}-x{/tex}
Differentiating with respect to x, we 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -1

271339.explanation: Given that y = sin -1 (3x - 4x 3 ) Let x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\sin ^{-1} x{/tex} Then, {tex}y=\sin ^{-1}\left(3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^{3} \theta\right){/tex} Using {tex}\sin 3 \theta=3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^{3} \theta{/tex} , we get {tex}y=\sin ^{-1}(\sin 3 \theta)=3 \theta=3 \sin ^{-1} x{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}

271340.explanation:
Given that y = cos-1 (4x3 - 3x)
Let x = cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1} x{/tex}
Then, {tex}y=\cos ^{-1}\left(4 \cos ^{3} \theta-3 \cos \theta\right){/tex}

Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain

271341.explanation: Given that {tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{x}}{1-\sqrt{a x}}{/tex} Let {tex}\sqrt{a}=\tan A{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{x}}=\tan \mathrm{B}{/tex} , then {tex}A=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{a}{/tex} and {tex}B=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} Hence, {tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\tan A+\tan B}{1-\tan A \tan B}{/tex} Using tan (A + B) {tex}=\frac{\tan A+\tan B}{1-\tan A \tan B}{/tex} , we obtain y = tan -1 tan (A + B) = A + B {tex}=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{a}+\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0+\frac{1}{1+(\sqrt{x})^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}(1+\mathrm{x})}{/tex}

271342.explanation:
Given that {tex}y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos y=\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} or {tex}\sec y=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
Since tan2 x = sec2 x - 1, therefore
{tex}\tan ^{2} y=\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right)^{2}-1{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4 x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\tan y=-\frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex}
Let x = tan {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x{/tex}
Hence, {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex}
Using {tex}\tan 2 \theta=\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}{/tex}, we get
Using -tan x = tan (-x), we obtain
{tex}=-2 \theta{/tex}
= -2tan-1 x
Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-2}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}

271343.explanation: Given that {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+x^{2}}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} Let x 2 = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x^{2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\tan \theta}{1-\tan \theta}\right){/tex} Using {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)=\frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x}{/tex} , we obtain {tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}+\tan ^{-1}\left(x^{2}\right){/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1+x^{4}} \times 2 x=\frac{2 x}{1+x^{4}}{/tex}

271345.explanation:
Given that y = tan-1 (sec x + tan x)
Hence, {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos x}\right){/tex}
Using {tex}\cos x=\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\sin x=2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta{/tex} = 1
Hence, {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}\right){/tex}{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\left(\cos ^{\frac{x}{2}}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos ^{\frac{x}{2}}+\sin \frac{x}{2}}{\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}}\right){/tex}
Dividing by cos {tex}\frac {x} {2}{/tex} in numerator and denominator, we obtain
{tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \frac{1+\tan \frac{x}{2}}{1-\tan \frac{x}{2}}{/tex}
Using {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)=\frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x}{/tex}, we obtain
{tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
Differentiating with respect to x, we 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

271346.explanation: Given that {tex}y=\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right){/tex} Let x = {tex}\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} and using {tex}\cot ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} Hence, {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right){/tex} Using {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)=\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}{/tex} , we obtain {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}+\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}

= ex(x - 1) + C

271354.explanation:
{tex}\int\underset Ix\;\mathrm{co}\underset{II}{sec}^2\;x\;dx{/tex}

= -x cotx - {tex}\int(-cotx)~dx{/tex}

271355.explanation:
The given integral be I = {tex}\int{/tex} x sin x cos x dx
Using, sin(2x)=2sinxcosx we can write
{tex}I={\textstyle\frac12}\int\underset Ix\;s\underset{II}{\mathrm{in}}\;2x\;dx{/tex}
Now, by using IBP formula we get,
{tex}{\textstyle\frac12}\int x\;s{\mathrm{in}}\;2x\;dx{/tex} = {tex}-\frac12x~cos(2x)+\frac14sin(2x){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\int{/tex} x sin x cos x dx = {tex}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex} x cos 2x + {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} sin 2x + C

271359.explanation:
{tex}I=\int x.{\textstyle\frac12}(1\;+\;\cos\;2x)\;dx\;=\;{\textstyle\frac12}\int x\;dx\;+\;{\textstyle\frac12}\int\underset Ix\;c\underset{II}{\mathrm{os}}\;2x\;dx{/tex}
After doing integrate by parts we get
{tex}\int{x}cos(2x)~dx {/tex} = {tex}\frac{xsin(2x)}2+\frac{cos(2x)}4{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{x \sin 2 x}{4}+\frac{\cos 2 x}{8}+C{/tex}

271360.explanation:

after solving we get
I= x (log x - 1) log10 e + C

271363.explanation: {tex}I=\left\{(\mathrm{lo}\underset Igx)^2\cdot\underset{II}1\right\}dx=(\log x)^2\cdot x-\int\frac{2\log x}x\cdot x\,dx{/tex} = x (log x)2 - 2 {tex}\int{/tex} log x dx = x (log x)2 - 2 [x log x - 1)] + C

I = {tex}2[\sqrt{x} \sin \sqrt{x}+\cos \sqrt{x}]{/tex} + C

= sec x tan x - {tex}\int{/tex} sec x (sec2 x - 1) dx = sec x tan x - 1 + {tex}\int{/tex} sec x dx

271373.explanation:
Given that {tex}y=\sec ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sec y=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
Since tan2x = sec2x - 1, thus
{tex}\tan ^{2} y=\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right)^{2}-1{/tex}{tex}=\frac{4 x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\tan y=-\frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex}
Let {tex}x=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x{/tex}

271407.explanation:
For continuity left hand limit must be equal to right hand limit and value at the point.
Continuity at x = 2.
For continuity at x = 2.
L.H.L = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}(1+x){/tex} = 3
R.H.L = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{+}}(5-x){/tex} = 3
f(2) = 1 + 2 = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Therefore,f(x) is continuous at x = 2
Now for differentiability.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(2-) = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(2)}{x-2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(2-) = {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(2-h)-f(2)}{2-h-2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(2-) = {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1+2-h-3}{2-h-2}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-h}{-h}=1{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(2+) = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(2)}{x-2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(2+) = {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(2+h)-f(2)}{2+h-2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(2-) = {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{5-(2-h)-3}{2+h-2}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h}{-h}{/tex}
= -1
As, f'(2-) is not equal to f(2+)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous but not differentiable at x = 2.

When {tex}{x}=2, {t}=4{/tex}

Using integration by parts, we get
{tex} \int_2^4 7 \log t d t=7\left(\text { logt } \int d t-\int(\text { log } t)^{\prime} \int d t\right) {/tex}
{tex} =7(t \log t-t)_2^4 {/tex}
{tex} =7(4 \log 4-4-2 \log 2+2) {/tex}
{tex} =7(6 \log 2-2)=14(3 \log 2-1) {/tex}

{tex}=\int_{-1}^{0}-1 \mathrm{dx}+\int_{0}^{1} 0 \mathrm{dx}{/tex}
= -1 - 0 + 0
= -1

271549.explanation:
{tex}\int_{1}^{2}\left|x^{2}-3 x+2\right| d x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x2 - 3x + 2 = 0
(x - 2) (x - 1) = 0
So 2, and 1 itself are the limits so no breaking points for the integral,
{tex}\therefore \int_{1}^{2}\left(-x^{2}+3 x-2\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{-x^{3}}{3}+\frac{3 x^{2}}{2}-2 x\right](1+02){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{6}{/tex}

271550.explanation:
The given integral is {tex}\int_{\pi}^{2 \pi}|\sin x| d x{/tex}
sin x = 0
{tex}as~x=0, \pi, 2 \pi{/tex} ...
So {tex}\pi, 2 \pi{/tex} are the limits so no breaking points for the integral,
{tex}\therefore \int_{\pi}^{2 \pi}-\sin x d x={/tex}{tex}-\cos x(\pi \text { to } 2 \pi){/tex}
 = 2

271551.explanation:
The given integral is  {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac {1} {\sqrt{2}}} \frac{\sin ^{-1} x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{\frac {3} {2}}} d x{/tex}
Put sin-1 x = t
{tex}d t=\frac{d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
x = sin t
{tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
= t;
and sin-1 0 = 0
= t
Limit changes to,
{tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{t d t}{1-\sin ^{2} t}{/tex}{tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} t \sec ^{2} t d t{/tex}
{tex}=\mathrm{t} \tan \mathrm{t}-\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan \mathrm{t} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{t}{/tex}
= [t tan t + log cos t] (0 to {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex})
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \log 2{/tex}

271552.explanation:
Let the given integral be I = {tex}\int_{0}^{1} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
Put x = tan y
dx = sec2 dy
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 y) \sec ^{2} y d y{/tex}
{tex}=2 \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} y \sec ^{2} y d y{/tex}
{tex}=2\left[y \tan y-\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan y d y\right]{/tex}
= 2[y tan y + log cos y] (0 to {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex})
{tex}=2\left[\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \log 2\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\log 2{/tex}

271553.explanation:
We know that
{tex}\therefore \int_{0}^{a} f(x)=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x)=1{/tex} ...(let)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Here,
{tex}a=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex};
{tex}f(x)=\frac{\sqrt{\cot x}}{\sqrt{\cot x}+\sqrt{\tan x}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\mathrm{x}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{\tan x}}{\sqrt{\cot x}+\sqrt{\tan x}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 21=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 1 d x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 21=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 1=\frac{\pi}{2.2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

272124.explanation:
Putting {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} = t and {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex} dx = dt,
we get {tex}I=2\int\underset It\;\underset{II}e^tdt{/tex} 
Solving the integral using Integration by parts, we get
I = {tex}2 e^{\sqrt{x}}(\sqrt{x}-1){/tex} + C

272134.explanation:
Put x = tan t, so that dx = sec2t dt and t = tan-1 x
{tex}\therefore I=\int\frac{t(\tan t)}{\left(1+\tan^2t\right)^{3/2}}\sec^2tdt=\int\underset It\;s\underset{II}{\mathrm{in}}\;tdt{/tex}
After solving the above using integration by parts we get
I = {tex}\frac{-\tan ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}+\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{/tex} + C

272138.explanation:
Put sec-1 x = t, so that x = sec t and dx = sec t tan t dt
{tex}\therefore I=-\int\underset It\;(sec\;\underset{II}{t\;t}\mathrm{an}\;t)dt{/tex}
After solving it by using integration by parts we get
I = x sec-1 x - log |x + {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}-1}{/tex}| + C

272139.explanation:
Put x = sin t and dx = cos t dt
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int{/tex} sin-1 (sin 3t) cot t dt = {tex}3\int\underset It\;\underset{II}\cos\;t\;dt{/tex}
After solving by using integration by parts we get
I = 3 [x sin-1 x + {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex}] + C

272147.explanation:
Putting x = sin t and dx = cos t dt, we get
I = {tex}\int{/tex} {sin-1 (sin t)}2 cos t dt = {tex}\int\underset I{t^2}\underset{II}\cos\;tdt{/tex}
= t2 sin t - {tex}2\int\underset It\;\underset{II}\sin\;t{/tex} dt
= t2 sin t - 2[t (-cos t) - {tex}\int{/tex} (-cos t) dt]
= t2 sin t + 2t cos t - 2sin t + C
= x(sin-1x)2 + 2(sin-1x) {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} - 2x + C

272163.explanation:
We know that,
{tex} \int_{0}^{a} f(x)=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x){/tex} 
Here {tex}a=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex};
and {tex}f(x)=\frac{\cos \frac{1}{4} x}{\sin ^{\frac{1}{4}} x+\cos ^{\frac{1}{4}} x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\mathrm{x}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{\cos^{ \frac{1}{4}}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}}{{\sin ^{\frac{1}{4}}}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) ~+~\cos ^{\frac{1}{4}}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{{\sin^{ \frac{1}{4}}\left(x\right)}}{{\cos ^{\frac{1}{4}}}\left(x\right) ~+~\sin ^{\frac{1}{4}}\left(x\right)}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore 2I=\int_{0}^{a} f(x)+\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x){/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^{\frac{1}{4}} x+\cos ^{\frac{1}{4}} x}{\sin ^{\frac{1}{4}} x+\cos ^{\frac{1}{4}} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 1 \mathrm{d} x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 2I=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I=\frac{\pi}{2.2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

272441.explanation:
We have {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} {x} & {2} \\ {18} & {x} \end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ll} {6} & {2} \\ {18} & {6} \end{array}\right|{/tex} 
We know that determinant of A is calculated as {tex}|A|=\left|\begin{array}{ll} {a} & {b} \\ {c} & {d} \end{array}\right|=a d-b c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x) - 2(18) = 6(6) - 2(18)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 36 = 36 - 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 36 - 36 + 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 6

272746.explanation:
From the identity, we know that,
If {tex}\cos \alpha, \cos \beta {/tex} and {tex} \cos \gamma{/tex} be the direction cosines of a vector, then,
{tex}\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex}
Using, {tex}\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex}
{tex}\sin ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \beta+\sin ^{2} \gamma+\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma{/tex} = 1 + 1 + 1
{tex}\sin ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \beta+\sin ^{2} \gamma+1=3{/tex}
On simplifying, we get,
{tex}\sin ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \beta+\sin ^{2} \gamma=2{/tex}

So, in totality there are 5 constants. Hence, the order of the equation is 5.

-{tex}\frac{1}{x y}{/tex} + log y = c

495464.explanation:
Given differential equation is
y = x{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{(d y / d x)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y{tex}\left(\frac{d p}{d x}\right){/tex} = x{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}{/tex} + 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Degree = Power of highest derivative = 2

495468.explanation:
Given,
extan y dx + (1 - ex)sec2 y dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e^{x}}{1-e^{x}} \cdot d x{/tex} + {tex}\frac{\sec ^{2} y}{\tan y} \cdot d y{/tex} = 0
On integrating, we get
{tex}\int \frac{e^{x} d x}{1-e^{x}}{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{\sec ^{2} y}{\tan y} d y{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log(1 - ex) + log tan y = logC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log tan y = log C + log(1 - ex)
= logC (1- ex)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} tan y = C(1 - ex)

495470.explanation:
Given, x2 dy + y2 dx = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y^{2}}+\frac{d x}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 0
On integrating, we get
{tex}\int y^{-2} d y{/tex} + {tex}\int x^{-2} d x{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y^{-2+1}}{-2+1}+\frac{x^{-2}+1}{-2+1}{/tex} = -C1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-1}{y}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} = C1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y}{/tex} = C1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y = C1xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{1}}{/tex}(x + y) = xy
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C(x + y) = xy, where {tex}\frac{1}{C_{1}}{/tex} = C

495472.explanation: The pairs {tex}\left(2, \frac{3}{2}\right){/tex} is not feasible. Because the degree of any differential equation cannot be rational type. If so, then we use rationalization and convert it into an integer.

495478.explanation:
The given differential equation is
{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} + y = 0 ...(i)
y = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -1
From Eq. (i), (1)2 + x(1) + x = 1 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
So, y = x is not a solution of Eq. (i).

495488.explanation:
Given differential equation is
{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{d y}{y}=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}+\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} = 0
On integrating both sides, we get
log x +log y = log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = C

495492.explanation:
Given differential equation is {tex}\left(\frac{d^{4} y}{d x^{4}}\right)^{\frac {3}{5}}-5\left(\frac{d^{3} y}{d x^{3}}\right)+6\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right){/tex}{tex}-3\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} + 5 = 0
Since the highest exponent of the highest derivative is called the degree of a differential equation provided exponent of each derivative and the unknown variable appearing in the differential equation is a non-negative integer.
{tex}\therefore\left(\frac{d^{4} y}{d x^{4}}\right)^{\frac {3}{5}}{/tex} = {tex}5\left(\frac{d^{3} y}{d x^{3}}\right){/tex}{tex}-6\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right){/tex}{tex}+8\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} - 5
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{d^{4} y}{d x^{4}}\right)^{3}{/tex}
= {tex}\left\{5\left(\frac{d^{3} y}{d x^{3}}\right)-6\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)+8\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)-5\right\}^{5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required degree = 3

495498.explanation:
Given differential equation is
log{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} + x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = -x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = e-x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int d y=\int e^{-x} \cdot d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get y
y = -e-x + C
which is the required general solution.

495500.explanation:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - sin2y = 0
The highest order derivative, present in the differential equation is {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}.
Therefore, its order is one.

495503.explanation:
Given differential equation is
y = 2 cos x + 3 sin x ...(i)
Now, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = = -2sin x + 3cos x
= -(2 cos x + 3 sin x) = -y [from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\therefore \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + y = 0
So, only Statement (i) is correct.

495505.explanation:
Given that, {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = 10, |{tex}\vec b{/tex}| = 2 and
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}| \vec{b} \mid \cos \theta{/tex} = 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac 3 5{/tex}
{tex}\sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\cos ^{2} \theta}=\sqrt{1-\frac{9 }{ 25}}=\frac 4 5{/tex}
Now, {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta \vec{n} \mid{/tex}
{tex}=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}||\sin \theta||\hat{{n}} |= 10 \cdot 2 \cdot 1 \cdot| \sin \theta|{/tex}
= 10{tex}\times{/tex}2{tex}\times{/tex}1{tex}\times{/tex}|{tex}\frac45{/tex}| = {tex}20 \times \frac{4}{5}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 16

495508.explanation:
Given differential equation,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = 1
On comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, we get P = 2 and Q = 1
Now, IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex} = e2x
Then, complete solution
y IF = {tex}\int Q(\mathrm{IF}){/tex} dx + C = {tex}\int 1 \cdot e^{2 x}{/tex} dx + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y e2x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}e2x + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + C e-2x ...(i)
Now, at x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 0
From Eq. (i),
0 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + C e0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + C = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required solution is y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-2 x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{1-e^{-2 x}}{2}{/tex}

495510.explanation:
Let the position vectors of the vertices of a rectangle A, B, C and D are
{tex}\vec {O A}=-\hat{{i}}+\frac{\hat{\vec{j}}}{2}+4 \hat{{k}}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{O B}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\frac{\hat{\mathbf{j}}}{2}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{O C}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\frac{\hat{\mathbf{j}}}{2}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{O D}=-\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\frac{\mathbf{j}}{2}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{AB} {}=\vec{O B}-\vec{OA} {}=\left(\hat{\vec{i}}+\frac{\hat{\mathbf{j}}}{2}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}\right){/tex}{tex}-\left(-\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\frac{\hat{\mathbf{j}}}{2}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}\right)=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{B C}=\vec{O C}-\vec{O B}=\left(\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\frac{\hat{\mathbf{j}}}{2}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}\right){/tex}{tex}-\left(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\frac{\hat{\mathbf{j}}}{2}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}\right)=-\hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of rectangle = {tex}|\vec{A B}| \cdot|\vec{B C}|{/tex}
= {tex}(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}})|\times|(-\hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mid{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2 sq units

495514.explanation:
Since the vectors {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}=\hat{x} \hat{\mathbf{j}}=y \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} and {tex}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+x \hat{\mathbf{j}}+y \hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex} are orthogonal to each other, then
{tex}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-x \hat{\mathbf{j}}-y \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+x \hat{\mathbf{j}}+y \hat{\mathbf{k}})=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - x2 - y2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 1
which represent a circle with centre at origin and having radius is 1.

495518.explanation:
We know that, if three vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar, then [{tex}\vec{a}{/tex}{tex}\vec{b}{/tex}{tex}\vec{c}{/tex}] = 0
Let {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
and {tex}\mathbf{c}=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+m \mathbf{j}+5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
Then, {tex}[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & m & 5 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(10 + 3m) + 1(5 + 9) + 1(m - 6) = 7m + 28 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = −4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7m + 28 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = -4

495520.explanation:
Given differential equation is
(x2 + x + 1)dy + (y2 + y + 1)dx = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 + x + 1)dy = -(y2 + y + 1)dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{\left(1+y+y^{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{d x}{\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)}+\frac{d y}{\left(1+y+y^{2}\right)}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{3^{2}}{4}}{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{d y}{\left(y+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{3}{4}}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{d y}{\left(y+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 0 [on integrating]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)} \tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}\right\}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}tan-1C1 {tex}\left[\because \int \frac{d x}{a^{2}+x^{2}}=\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{a}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} + {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}tan-1C1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} + tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} = tan-1C1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan-1{tex}\left\{\frac{\left(\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+\left(\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)}{1-\left(\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)\left(\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)}\right\}{/tex} = tan-1C1 [{tex}\because{/tex} tan-1 x + tan-1 y = tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{x+y}{1-x y}\right){/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}[(2 x+1)+(2 y+1)]}{3-(2 x+1) \cdot(2 y+1)}{/tex} = C1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}(x + y + 1) = 2C(1 - x - y - 2xy)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + y + 1) = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}(1 - x - y - 2xy)
On comparing with (x + y + 1) = A(1 + Bx + Cy + Dxy)
Here, A is parameter and B, C and D are constants.
The value of B = -1

495528.explanation:
Given that, {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|{/tex}
Now, {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex}
{tex}=\vec{a} \times\vec{a}+\vec{b} \times \vec{a}-\vec{a} \times \vec{b}-\vec{b} \times \vec{b}{/tex}
= 1 + {tex}\vec a{/tex}{tex}\vec b{/tex} - {tex}\vec a{/tex}{tex}\vec b{/tex} - 1 = 0 {tex}\neq{/tex} l
i.e. ({tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex}) is perpendicular to ({tex}\vec a{/tex} - {tex}\vec b{/tex}).

495533.explanation:
Given, a = (2, 1, -1),
b = (1, -1, 0) and c = (5, - 1, 1)
Now, a + b - c = (2 + 1 - 5, 1 - 1 + 1, -1 + 0 - 1)
(-2, 1, -2) = {tex}\vec d{/tex} [say]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Unit vector {tex}(\hat{\mathbf{d}})=\frac{\vec{d}}{|\vec{d}|}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(-2,1,-2)}{\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(1)^{2}+(-2)^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(-2,1,-2)}{\sqrt{4+1+4}}=\frac{1}{3}(-2,1,-2){/tex}
But in opposite direction {tex}\vec d{/tex} = {tex}-\vec d{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3}(2,-1,2)=\frac{2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}}{3}{/tex}

495537.explanation:
The given differential equation of the curve is,
dy = y tan x dx {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int \tan x \cdot d x{/tex} [on integrating]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = log sec x + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = log C sec x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = C sec x ...(i)
Since, the curve passes through the origin (0, 1), then
1 = C sec 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equation of curve is, y = sec x

495540.explanation:
Given that, {tex}\vec a{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} ({tex}\vec b{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\vec a{/tex}) which is the vector triple product = {tex}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}) \vec{b}-(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}) \vec{a}=\lambda \vec{b}-\mu \vec{a}{/tex}
where, {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} are scalar quantity. Hence, {tex}\vec a{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} ({tex}\vec b{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\vec a{/tex}) is coplanar with both a and b.

495544.explanation:
Consider the given differential equation
ln{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} + x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ln{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = -x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = e-x
On separating the variable, we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dy = e-x dx
On integrating both side, we get {tex}\int d y=\int e^{-x}{/tex}dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + e-x = C

495545.explanation:
Given that, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex},
i.e. {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are perpendicular to each other and {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} = 0
i.e. {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are parallel to each other. So, both conditions are possible iff {tex}\vec a{/tex} = 0 and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = 0

495547.explanation:

495548.explanation:
Consider the given differential equation,
sin{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} - a = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dy = (sin-1 a) dx
On integrating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\int d y=\int\left(\sin ^{-1} a\right){/tex}dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = (sin-1 a){tex}\int d x \Rightarrow y{/tex} = (sin-1 a) x + C

495550.explanation:
Consider the given differential equation,
{tex}\frac{y d x-x d y}{y^{2}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow d\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} = 0 {tex}\left[\because d\left(\frac{u}{v}\right)=\frac{v \cdot d u-u \cdot d v}{v^{2}}\right]{/tex}
On integrating both side, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \int d\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} = C1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{y}{/tex} = C1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = Cx, where C = {tex}\frac{1}{C_{1}}{/tex}

495551.explanation:
Given,
{tex}(\lambda \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex}
{tex}=(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-11 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-7 \hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ \lambda & 1 & -1 \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \end{array}\right|=(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-11 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-7 \hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-(4 \lambda+3) \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(-2 \lambda-3) \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}{tex}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-11 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-7 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
On comparing the coefficient of, we get
{tex}(4 \lambda+3){/tex} = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2

495552.explanation:
Consider the given differential equation, {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{y}{/tex} = y2
On comparing with linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} + Px = Q. Here, P = {tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q = y2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int P d y}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{y} d y}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \ln (y)}{/tex} = y
Now, the solution of given differential equation is
xy = {tex}\int\left(y \cdot y^{2}\right) {/tex}dy + C1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\int y^{3}{/tex} dy + C1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\frac{y^{4}}{4}{/tex} + C1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4xy = y4 + C,
where C = 4C1

495554.explanation:
If {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are two vectors, where {tex}a_1, a_2{/tex} are the components of vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}b_1, b_2{/tex} are the components of vector {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}, then the scalar product is given by
{tex} \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=a_1 b_1+a_1 b_2 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}) \cdot(6 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j})=2(6)+5(-7)=12-35=-23 . {/tex}

495556.explanation:
1. We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = f(x) + x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dy = [f(x) + x]dx
    On integrating both sides, we get
    {tex}\int d y=\int[f(x)+x]{/tex}dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\int f(x){/tex} dx + {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C
    Let g(x) = {tex}\int f(x){/tex}dx + {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex}
    Thus, general solution is of the form y = g (x) + C
  2. Consider the given differential equation {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}{/tex} = f(x)
    Clearly, the highest order derivative occurring in the differential equation is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and its highest power is 2.
  3. Also, given equation is polynomial in the derivative. So the degree of a differential equation is 2.

495563.explanation:
Let {tex}\vec{a}=4 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{(4 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})}{|\mathbf{a}| \cdot|\mathbf{b}|}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4+0-4}{|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}|}=0 \Rightarrow \theta=\cos 90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex} + cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = sin90o + cos90o = 1

495564.explanation:
Let {tex}\mathbf{\vec a}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
Let any vector normal to {tex}\vec a{/tex}, then the dot product of both vectors should be zero.

  1. {tex}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex} = 1 + 1 - 1 = 1 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
  2. {tex}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex} = 1 - 1 + 1 = 1 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
  3. {tex}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex} = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0

495568.explanation:
Given, f"(x) = g”(x)
On integrating both sides, we get
f'(x) = g’(x) + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(1) = f'(1) + r {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 = 6 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = g'(x) - 2
Again, on integrating both sides, we get
f(x) = g(x) - 2x + c1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(2) = g(2) - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + c1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = 9 - 4 + c1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c1 = - 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) - g(x) = -2x - 2
At x = 4, [f(x) - g(x)] = - 8 - 2 = -10

495569.explanation:
Given that,
The magnitude of {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec {b}){/tex} = Magnitude of {tex}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}|{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{a}| \vec{b}|| \sin \theta|| \hat{{n}}|=| \vec{a}|\vec{b}||\cos \theta|{/tex}  ({tex}\because{/tex} {tex} \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=|\vec{a}| \vec{b} \| \sin \theta \mid \hat{{n}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=|\vec{a}| \vec{b}|| \cos \theta \mid{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\sin \theta| \cdot 1=|\cos \theta|{/tex} [{tex}\because {/tex} {tex}|\hat{{n}}|=1{/tex} ]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\tan \theta|{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta{/tex} = 1= {tex}\tan \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
So, the angle between {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b {/tex} is {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.

495570.explanation:
Given, y = cx + c2 -3c3/2 + 2 ...(i)
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = C ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have
y = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} x + {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}{/tex} - 3{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3 / 2}{/tex} + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}{/tex} - 2 = -3{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3 / 2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[y-x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)-\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-2\right]^{2}{/tex} = 9{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}{/tex}
Hence, order is 1 and degree is 4.

495571.explanation:
Let {tex}\alpha=x \hat{{i}}+y \hat{{j}}+{z} \hat{{k}}{/tex}
Then {tex}(\alpha \cdot \hat{{i}}) \hat{{i}}+(\alpha \cdot \hat{{j}}) \hat{{j}}+(\alpha \cdot \hat{{k}}) \hat{{k}}{/tex}
= {tex}\{(x \hat{{i}}+y \hat{{j}}+z \hat{{k}}) \cdot \hat{{i}}\} \hat{{i}}{/tex}  + {tex}\{(x \hat{{i}}+y \hat{{j}}+z \hat{{k}}) \cdot \hat{{j}}\} \hat{{j}}{/tex} + {tex}\{(x \hat{{i}}+y \hat{{j}}+z \hat{{k}}) \cdot \hat{{k}}\} \hat{{k}}{/tex}
= {tex}(x) \hat{{i}}+(y) \hat{{j}}+(z) \hat{{k}}=\alpha{/tex}

495572.explanation:
Given that, {tex}\alpha=\hat{{k}}{/tex}
and {tex}\gamma=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}+4 \hat{{k}}{/tex}
Since, {tex}\beta{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex}.
i.e., {tex} \beta=\pm(\alpha \times \gamma)=\pm\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\pm \hat{{i}}(0-3)-\hat{{j}}(0-2)+\hat{{k}}(0-0){/tex}
= {tex}\pm(-3 \hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}){/tex}

495573.explanation:
