538004.explanation:
For a function f to be non decreasing
x1 < x2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x1) {tex}\leq{/tex} f(x2) for all x1, x2
Case I: Range has only one element Number of functions = 5
Case II: Range has two elements select three elements of set B.
For every selection of three elements, there are two monotonic functions. For instance, 1, 2 are selected.

Then, the number of non-decreasing functions = 2
The number of monotonic increasing functions (Range having only two elements) = 5C2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 20
Case III: Range has three elements.
For every selection of three elements, there is only one monotonic increasing function.
Number of non-decreasing functions = 5C3 = 10.
The required number = 5 + 20 + 10 = 35

538010.explanation:
Let f(x) = exp (2 + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} cos x + sin x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = exp (2 + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} cos x + sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (-{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} sin x + cos x)
f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (-{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} sin x + cos x) exp (2 +{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} cos x + sin x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} sin x + cos x = 0 

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} sin x - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} cos x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin (x - {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} : a point of relative maxima
{tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} = exp{tex}\left(2+\sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} = exp(4)

538017.explanation:
Let y = {tex}\frac{x^{2}-x+1}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex} = f(x), say
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)(2 x-1)-\left(x^{2}-x+1\right)(2 x+1)}{\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)^{2}} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x^{2}-2}{\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)^{2}}=\frac{2}{\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)^{2}}{/tex} (x - 1)(x + 1)
{tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, -1 
x = -1, 1 are points of relative maxima and minima respectively
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(-1) = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(1) = {tex}\frac 13{/tex}

538024.explanation:
L. H. L. = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^-}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1}{/tex} |log x| = 0
R. H. L. = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^-}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \sqrt {x - 1}{/tex} = 0
f(1) = 0

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is continuous at x = 1.
In (0, 1), f is decreasing ...[Refer to the graph.]
f is non-monotonic on R+.
Differentiability of function f at x = 1:
L. h. lim = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^-} \frac {f(x) - f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{|\log x|-0}{x-1}{/tex}
= {tex}-\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{\log x}{x-1}{/tex}
Let x = 1 = t
= -{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}{/tex} log {tex}(1 + t)^\frac 1t{/tex}
= -1
R. h. lim = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{\sqrt{x-1}-0}{x-1}{/tex}: does not exist
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is not differentiable at x = 1

538032.explanation:
f(x) = 2x + {tex}3x^{\frac 23}{/tex}
f'(x) = 2 + {tex}\frac{2 \times 3}{3 x^{\frac{1}{3}}}{/tex}, x {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 ...(i)
f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + {tex}x^{-\frac 13}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f" (x) = -{tex}\frac{2}{3} x^{-\frac{4}{3}}{/tex} < 0 for x = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 is a point of maxima
Also f is not differentiable at x = 0
The rough sketch of y = f(x) is given in the figure.

Number of points of optima = 2

538037.explanation:
f(x) = x + sin x, x {tex}\in{/tex} R
f is defined on an open interval, so no end point candidates for points of relative optima.
f is differentiable on R.
f' (x) = 1 + cos x.
f' (x) = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} cos x = - 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = an odd multiple of {tex}\pi{/tex}
But in neighborhood of all odd multiples of {tex}\pi{/tex}, f' (x) > 0.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is increasing everywhere.

538043.explanation:
f (-x) = f(x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is an even function.
Let g (x) = cos x, h (x) = cos {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}x.
The function h and g are periodic with periods
2{tex}\pi{/tex} (= a, say) and {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} (= b, say).
Since {tex}\frac ab{/tex} is not rational.
Therefore f is not periodic.
f is differentiable everywhere,
cos x {tex}\leq{/tex} 1 and cos {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 1 ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x + cos {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f(x) {tex}\leq{/tex} 2
The global maximum value of f(x) = 2 if equality occurs in (i).
(Equality in (i) occurs when x = 0).
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Global maximum of f occurs at x = 0

538050.explanation:
f(x) = |x2 - 4|
= x2 - 4, x2 - 4 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0
= 4 - x2, x2 - 4 < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f(x) = x2 - 4, x {tex}\leq{/tex} -2 or x {tex}\geq{/tex} 2
= 4 - x2, -2 < x < 2
f is differentiable, except possibly at x = -2, 2
f' (x) = 2x, x < -2 or x > 2
= -2x, -2 < x < 2

Differentiability at x = - 2.
L.h. lim = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-2^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(-2)}{x-(-2)} {/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-2^{-}} \frac{x^{2}-4}{x+2}{/tex} = -4
R.h. lim = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(-2)}{x-(-2)}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} \frac{-\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{x+2}{/tex} = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is not differentiable at x = - 2
Similarly, it can be shown that the function f is not differentiable at x = 2.
f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0
x = -2, 0, 2 are the candidates for points of relative optima.

x = - 2, 2 are points of relative minima.
x = 0 is a point of relative maxima.

538057.explanation:
Let a > 0
f(x) = x2 + {tex}\frac ax{/tex}
f is differentiable.
f' (x) = 2x - {tex}\frac{a}{x^{2}}=\frac{2}{x^{2}}\left(x^{3}-\frac{a}{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2}{x^{2}}\left[\left(x-\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)(\underbrace{x^{2}+x\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}+\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}}_{>0 \text { for all } x, a})\right]{/tex}
f' (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} is a point of relative minima.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Also f' (x) < 0 for x < 0 is a decreasing function in (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, 0)
As x {tex}\rightarrow -\infty{/tex}, f(x) {tex}\rightarrow \infty{/tex}
As x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0-, f(x) {tex}\rightarrow -\infty{/tex}
As x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0+, f(x) {tex}\rightarrow \infty{/tex}
As x {tex}\rightarrow \infty{/tex}, f(x) {tex}\rightarrow \infty{/tex}
Following is the rough sketch of y = f(x)

y-axis is the asymptote to the graph of y = f (x).
f is non-monotonic.
The graph of y = f(x) has x-intercept.
The function f has no relative maxima.
(For a < 0, the graph would be the mirror image y = f(x) in the y-axis.)

538064.explanation:
f' changes sign from +ve to -ve in neighbourhood of x = 3.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 is a point of relative maxima.
f' changes sign from -ve to +ve in neighbourhood of x = -1.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = - 1 is a point of relative minima.
f" changes sign -ve to +ve and +ve to -ve, respectively, in the neighbourhood of x = - 2 and x = 1.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = - 2 and x = 1 are points of inflection.

538071.explanation:
Since, {tex}f(x)=\cos x{/tex} is minimum at {tex}x=(2 m+1) \pi{/tex}.
So, the minimum value of {tex}\cos x{/tex} is {tex}\cos (2 m \pi+\pi)=\cos \pi=-1{/tex}

538076.explanation:
P(x) = x4 + ax3 + bx2 + cx + d
The graph of y = P(x) is symmetric about the y - axis.
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} P(x) = P(-x), for all x
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x4 + ax3 + bx2 + cx + d
= x4 - ax3 + bx2 - cx + d, for all x
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} ax3 + cx = 0 for all x.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 0, c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(x) = x4 + bx2 + d
P(0) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(x) = x4 + bx2 + 1
= {tex}\left(x^{2}+\frac{b}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(1-\frac{b^{2}}{4}\right) \geq 1-\frac{b^{2}}{4}{/tex}
Equality occurs only when x2 = -{tex}\frac b2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b < 0 and k2 = -{tex}\frac b2{/tex}
and 1 - {tex}\frac {b^2}4{/tex} = -3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 16
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = -4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k2 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ac + bd + 2k2 = 0 - 4 + 4
= 0

538082.explanation:
From graph of y = f(x), x = -2, x = 0, x = 1 are points of relative maxima.

(Note that f is not continuous at x = 0, and derivative off in the neighbourhood of x = 0, changes sign from -ve to +ve and x = 0 is not a point of relative minima but a point of maxima.)

538088.explanation:
f(x) = cos x - cos2 x, 0 {tex}\leq x \leq \pi{/tex}
f is differentiable in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex})
f' (x) = -sin x + 2 sin x cos x
= sinx (2 cos x - 1)
f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow \underbrace{\sin x=0}_{\text {rejected as }}{/tex} or cos x = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}
sin x > 0 in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex}) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {\pi}3{/tex}
f is defined on a closed interval
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, {tex}\pi{/tex} are also candidates for points of relative optima.

f' changes sign +ve to -ve in the neighbourhood of x = {tex}\frac {\pi}3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {\pi}3{/tex} is the point of relative maxima
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The global maximum value of f = M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
In (0, {tex}\frac {\pi}3{/tex}), f(x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f increases in (0, {tex}\frac {\pi}3{/tex})
In ({tex}\frac {\pi}3, \pi{/tex}), f(x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f increases in ({tex}\frac {\pi}3, \pi{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, {tex}\pi{/tex} are points of relative minima.
f(0) = 0, f({tex}\pi{/tex}) = -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The global minimum value = -2 = m
m + {tex}\frac 1M{/tex} = -2 + 4 = 2

538093.explanation:
f(x) = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} - x - b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = x2 + 1
f' (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex}1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1, x2 are {tex}\pm{/tex}1, which are points of optima.
For three distinct roots, f(x1) f(x2) < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} - 1 - b)(-{tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} + 1 - b) < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (b + {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex})(b - {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}) < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b {tex}\in\left(-\frac{2}{3}, \frac{2}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6b can take integral values -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3
Remark:
The figure drawn corresponds to b = 0

538097.explanation:
x = 1 and x = 3 are extreme points of P(x) (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 and x = 3 are roots of P' (x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P' (x) = k(x - 1) (x - 3) = k(x2 - 4x + 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(x) = k({tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} - 2x2 + 3x) + c
Given, P(1) = 6 and P(3) = 2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 6 = k({tex}\frac 13{/tex} - 2 + 3) + c and 2 = k(9 - 18 + 9) + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 6 = {tex}\frac {4k}3{/tex} + c and c = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P'(x) = 3(x2 - 4x + 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P' (0) = 9

538103.explanation:
f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ll} e^{x} , \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 2-e^{x-1}, \ 1<x \leq 2 \\ x-e, \ \ \ \ \ \ 2<x \leq 3 \end{array}\right.{/tex} and g(x) = {tex}\int_\limits0^x{/tex} f(t) dt, x {tex}\in{/tex} [1, 3],
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g' (x) = f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cc} 2-e^{x-1} & 1<x \leq 2 \\ x-e & 2<x \leq 3 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
g' (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 + loge 2 and x = e
Also, g' (x) > 0 for x {tex}\in{/tex} (1, 1 + loge 2)
and g' (x) < 0 for x {tex}\in{/tex} (1 + loge 2, 2)
So, g has a relative maxima at x = 1 + loge 2
Similarly,
g' (x) < 0 for 2 < x < e
and g' (x) > 0 for e < x < 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g has a relative maxima at x = e
f' (x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ll} e^{x} & , \quad 0<x<1 \\ -e^{x-1} & , \quad 1<x<2 \\ 1 & , 2<x<3 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Clearly, f' (x) > 0 for x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, 1)
f' (x) < 0 for x {tex}\in{/tex} (1, 2)
f' (x) > 0 for x {tex}\in{/tex} (2, 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f has relative maxima at x = 1 and relative minima at x = 2

538109.explanation:
P(x) = x4 + ax3 + bx2 + cx + d
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P'(x) = 4x3 + 3ax2 + 2bx + c ... (i)
x = 0 is the only real root of P'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P'(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0
Substituting c = 0 in (i), we get
P'(x) = x (4x2 + 3ax + 2b)
x = 0 is the only real root of P'(x) = 0.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x2 + 3ax + 2b = 0 has no real root
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9a2 - 32b < 0
Given, P(-1) < P(1)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 1 - a + b - c + d < 1 + a + b + c + d
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a > 0
But, 9a2 - 32b < 0. Therefore, b > 0
P'(x) = x (4x2 + 3ax + 2b) > 0 for all x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(x) is increasing in (0, 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(1) is the maximum value of P(x).
P'(x) = x(4x2 + 3ax + 2b) < 0 for all x {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 0) ... [as 4x2 + 3ax + 2b > 0 for all x]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(x) is decreasing in (-1, 0).
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(-1) is not the minimum value of P.

538115.explanation:
f(x) = {tex}\int_\limits{-1}^x{/tex} t(et - 1)(t - 1)(t - 2)3(t - 3)5dt
Differentiate with respect to x, to get
f'(x) = x(ex - 1)(x - 1)(x - 2)3(x - 3)5
f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, 1, 2, 3

f' changes sign from -ve to +ve in neighbourhood of x = 1 and x = 3.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f has relative minima at x = 1 and x = 3

538119.explanation:
Point P(2, -1) lies on the curve.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 = {tex}\frac{2 a+b}{(-2)} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2a + b = 2 ...(i)
Also the curve has a turning point at P(2, -1)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} (at point P) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{a\left(x^{2}-5 x+4\right)-(2 x-5)(a x+b)}{(x-1)^{2}(x-4)^{2}}{/tex} = 0 ...(ii)
At (2, -1)
(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-2 a+2 a+b}{4}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 ....[From (i)]
then |6a + b| = 6

538122.explanation:
s = 48t - 16t2 ...(i)
Differentiate with respect to t,
{tex}\frac {ds}{dt}{/tex} = 48 - 32t
= -16 (2t - 3) 

{tex}\frac {ds}{dt}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 48 - 32t = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow t=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
t = 3 is a point of relative maxima.
Substituting t = {tex}\frac 32{/tex} in (i), we get
s = 48{tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)-16\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} = 36
Required height = 36 + 64 = 100 metres

538127.explanation:
Let {tex}\angle{/tex}APB = {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACM
Let l be the length of the ladder. Then
l = 8 cosec {tex}\alpha+\frac{27}{8}{/tex} sec {tex}\alpha{/tex}, 0 < {tex}\alpha<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow l=\frac{1}{8}{/tex} [-64 cosec {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 27 sec {tex}\alpha{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d l}{d \alpha}=\frac{1}{8}{/tex} [- 64 cosec {tex}\alpha{/tex} cot {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 27 sec {tex}\alpha{/tex} tan {tex}\alpha{/tex}]
= {tex}\frac{1}{8}\left[\frac{27 \sin ^{3} \alpha-64 \cos ^{3} \alpha}{\sin ^{2} \alpha \cos ^{2} \alpha}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(3 \sin \alpha-4 \cos \alpha)\left(9 \sin ^{2} \alpha+16 \cos ^{2} \alpha+12 \sin \alpha \cos \alpha\right)}{8 \sin ^{2} \alpha \cos ^{2} \alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d l}{d \alpha}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan {tex}\alpha=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} (a point of relative minima)

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The shortest length of the ladder
= {tex}\frac{1}{8}\left[27 \times \frac{5}{3}+64 \times \frac{5}{4}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{125}8{/tex}

538132.explanation:
V = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2h, V = 16{tex}\pi{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2h = 16 ...(i)
Amount of material (including lid),
A = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r (r + h)
= 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 + 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r {tex}\left(\frac{16}{r^{2}}\right){/tex} ...[From (i)]
= 2{tex}\pi{/tex} (r2 + {tex}\frac{16}{r}{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{dA}{d r}{/tex} = 2{tex}\pi{/tex} (2r - {tex}\frac{16}{r}{/tex})
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{d \mathrm{~A}}{d r}=\frac{4 \pi}{r^{2}}{/tex} (r3 - 8) = {tex}\frac{4 \pi}{r^{2}}{/tex} (r - 2)(r2 + 2r + 4)
{tex}\frac{dA}{d r}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} r = 2 (point of relative minima)

Amin = 2{tex}\pi{/tex} (4 + {tex}\frac{16}{2}{/tex}) = 24{tex}\pi{/tex}

538141.explanation:
f(x) = log{tex}\left[\frac{x^{2}+a b}{(a+b) x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is continuous in [a, b]
f is differentiable in (a, b)
f(a) = log 1 = 0 and f(b) = log 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The conditions of Rolle’s theorem are satisfied.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} There exists at least one point x = c in (a, b) such that f'(c) = 0
f'(c) = {tex}\frac{(a+b) c}{c^{2}+a b} \times \frac{2 c(a+b) c-(a+b)\left(c^{2}+a b\right)}{(a+b)^{2} c^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{\left(c^{2}+a b\right)(a+b) c}{/tex} ((a + b)(c2 - ab))
= {tex}\frac{c^{2}-a b}{c\left(c^{2}+a b\right)}{/tex}
f'(c) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = {tex}\pm \sqrt {ab}{/tex} (c = -{tex}\sqrt {ab}{/tex} rejected)

538144.explanation:
f(x) = (x - 2) log x, 1 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 2
f is continuous on [1, 2].
f is differentiable on (1, 2).
Also, f(1) = 0, f(2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} All the conditions of Rolle’s theorem are satisfied.
f'(x) = {tex}\frac{x-2}{x}{/tex} + log x
By Rolle’s theorem, there exists at least one value x = c in (1, 2) such that f' (c) = 0.
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{c-2}{c}{/tex} + log c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} There is at least one root, in (1, 2), of the equation {tex}\frac{x-2}{x}{/tex} + log x = 0.
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x log x = 2 - x

538148.explanation:
Let there be a value of k for which the equation x3 - 3x + k = 0 has two roots in (0, 1).
Let a, b be the roots {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(a) = f(b).
Let 0 < a < b < 1
All the conditions of Rolle's theorem are satisfied.
f'(x) = 3x2 - 3 = 3(x2 - 1)
By Rolle's theorem, there is at least one value of x, say x = c, in (0, 1) such that f'(c) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(c) = c2 - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1 - 1
Neither of the values of c {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b).
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} There is no root of f(x) = 0 in (0, 1), for any value of k.

538151.explanation:
Let f(x) = {tex}\frac{a x^{3}}{3}+\frac{b x^{2}}{2}-\left(\frac{a}{3}+\frac{b}{2}\right) x{/tex}
f is continuous in [0, 1] and f is differentiable in (0, 1).
Also, f(0) = 0, f(1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Rolle's theorem is applicable in [0, 1]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f' (x) = ax2 + bx - ({tex}\frac{a}{3}+\frac{b}{2}{/tex}) = 0 in (0, 1) for at least one value of x.
By Rolle's theorem, the equation
ax2 + bx = {tex}\frac{a}{3}+\frac{b}{2}{/tex} has a root in (0, 1)

538188.explanation:
Let a < x1 < b < x2 < c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x1) < f' (x2) ...[f' is increasing]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}<\frac{f(c)-f(b)}{c-b}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (c - b) (f(b) - f(a)) < (b - a) (f(c) - f(b))
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f(c) (b - a) + (c - b) f(a) > (c - a) f(b)

538190.explanation:
f(x) = {tex}\int_\limits{1}^{x} \frac{1}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} dt
Differentiate with respect to x,
f' (x) = {tex}\frac 1{1 + x^2}{/tex}
By Lagrange's theorem,
{tex}\frac{f(4)-f(2)}{4-2}{/tex} = f'(c), 2 < c < 4 ...(i)
2 < c < 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 < c2 < 16
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{5}>\frac{1}{1+c^{2}}>\frac{1}{17}{/tex} ...(ii)
(i) and (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{17}{/tex} < f(4) - f(2) < {tex}\frac 25{/tex}

538562.explanation:
Given differential equation
{tex}\Leftrightarrow x^{2}=e^{\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)^{-1} \frac{d y}{d x} }{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow x^{2}=e^{\frac{y}{x} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}}{/tex}
Taking log on both sides, we get
log x2 = {tex}\frac{y}{x} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y dy = x log x2 dx (Variables separable)
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\frac{y^{2}}{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} log t dt + c1, where x2 = t
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y2 = t log t - t + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y2 = x2 log x2 - x2 + c, where c = 2c1

538565.explanation:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{3 x y+y^{2}}{x^{2}+x y}{/tex} ...(i)
Let y = vx ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(iii)
Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{3 x(v x)+v^{2} x^{2}}{x^{2}+x(v x)}=-\frac{x^{2}\left(3 v+v^{2}\right)}{x^{2}(1+v)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-3 v-v^{2}}{1+v}{/tex} - v
{tex}\Leftrightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-4 v-2 v^{2}}{1+v}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v(2+v)}{1+v}{/tex}
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{v+1}{v(v+2)}{/tex} dv + {tex}\int \frac{2}{x}{/tex} dx = log c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \int\left(\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{v+2}\right){/tex} dv + 2{tex}\int \frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx = log c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} [log v + log (v + 2)] + 2 log x = log c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} log [v(v + 2)] + 4 log x = 2 log c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} log [x4 {tex}\cdot{/tex} v(v + 2)] = log c2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x4 {tex}\cdot{/tex} (v)(v + 2) = c2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x4 {tex}\cdot\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\left(\frac{y}{x}+2\right){/tex} = c2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2(2xy + y2) = c2

538568.explanation:
(y3 - 2yx2) dx + (2xy2 - x3) dy = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow\left(\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{3}-2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right){/tex} dx + {tex}\left(2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}-1\right){/tex} dy = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow\left(\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{3}-2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right)+\left(2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 ...(i) (Homogeneous)
Let y = vx ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(iii)
Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
(v3 - 2v) + (2v2 - 1) (v + x{tex}\frac {dv}{dx}{/tex}) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v-v^{3}}{2 v^{2}-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v-v^{3}-2 v^{3}+v}{2 v^{2}-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{2 v^{2}-1}{3 v\left(1-v^{2}\right)}{/tex} dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} (Variables separable)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{v^{2}+v^{2}-1}{3 v\left(1-v^{2}\right)} d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{v}{1-v^{2}}-\frac{1}{v}\right] d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
Integrating on both sides, we get
-{tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} log (1 - v2) - {tex}\frac{1}3{/tex} log v = log x + log c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} log {tex}\left(v \sqrt{1-v^{2}}\right){/tex} = log x + log c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \log \frac{1}{v \sqrt{1-v^{2}}}{/tex} = log (c3x3)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{y \sqrt{x^{2}-y^{2}}}{/tex} = c3x3
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} xy {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}-y^{2}}{/tex} = constant

538571.explanation:
y = um {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = mum-1 {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex}
Substituting in the given differential equation,
2x4 um (mum-1 {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex}) + u4m = 4x6
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{4 x^{6}-u^{4 m}}{2 m x^{4} u^{2 m-1}}{/tex}
For equation to be homogeneous,
4m = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [m] = {tex}\left[\frac{3}{2}\right]{/tex} = 1

538574.explanation:
x sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dy = [y sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} - x] dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{y}{x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-1}{\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} ...(i) (Homogeneous)
Let y = vx ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(iii)
Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
v + x{tex}\frac{d v}{d x} = \frac{v \sin v-1}{\sin v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1}{\sin v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -sin v dv = {tex}\frac{d x} x{/tex} (Variables separable)
Integrating on both sides, we get
cos v = log x + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\frac yx{/tex} = log x + c
y(1) = cos {tex}\frac {\pi}2{/tex} i.e., y = {tex}\frac {\pi}2{/tex}, when x = 1 (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\frac {\pi}2{/tex} = log 1 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log x

538577.explanation:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}-x y}{x^{2}}=\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} ...(i) (Homogeneous)
Let y = vx ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(iii)
Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v2 - v
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v2 - 2v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v(v-2)}{/tex} dv = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx (Variables separable)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{v-2}-\frac{1}{v}\right]{/tex} dv = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[log (v - 2) - log v] = log x + log c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\sqrt{\frac{v-2}{v}}{/tex} = log xc {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{v-2}{v}}{/tex} = xc
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{\frac{y}{x}-2}{\frac{y}{x}}}{/tex} = xc {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{y-2 x}{y}}{/tex} = cx ...(ii)
The curve passes through (1, 2).
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{2-2}{2}}{/tex} = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0
(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y-2 x}{y}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2x

538580.explanation:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} + sin2 {tex}(\frac{y}{x}){/tex} ...(i)
Let {tex}\frac yx{/tex} = v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = vx ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(iii)
Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v + sin2v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sin^2v}{/tex} dv = {tex}\frac 1x{/tex} dx (Variables separable)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec2 v dv = {tex}\frac 1x{/tex} dx + c
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int{/tex} cosec2 v dv = -{tex}\int \frac 1x{/tex} dx
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -cot v = log x + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -cot {tex}(\frac{y}{x}){/tex} = log x + c ...(ii)
Given that the curve passes through {tex}\left(1, \frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 = 0 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -1
(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot {tex}(\frac{y}{x}){/tex} = log x - 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} log e - log x = cot {tex}(\frac{y}{x}){/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} cot-1 [log {tex}(\frac ex){/tex}] = {tex}(\frac{y}{x}){/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y = x {tex}\cdot{/tex} cot-1[log({tex}\frac ex{/tex})]

538583.explanation:
{tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} + (3x2 tan-1 y - x3) (1 + y2) = 0

Let tan-1 y = z
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d x}{/tex}
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac {dz}{dx}{/tex} + 3x2 z = x3 (Linear)
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int 3x^2 dx}= e^{x^3}{/tex}

538589.explanation:
(x + 1) f' (x) - 2(x2 + x) f(x) = {tex}\frac{e^{x^{2}}}{x+1}{/tex} ...(i)
Let y = f(x)
(i) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} -2xy = {tex}\frac{e^{x^{2}}}{(x+1)^{2}}{/tex} ...(Linear)
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int -2x \ dx} = e^{-x^2}{/tex}
General solution of the given equation is
{tex}y \cdot e^{-x^{2}}=\int \frac{e^{x^{2}}}{(x+1)^{2}} \cdot e^{-x^{2}}{/tex} dx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow y e^{-x^{2}}=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} + c ...(ii)
f(0) = 5 i.e., y = 5, when x = 0 (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5e0 = -1 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 6
(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow y e^{-x^{2}}=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} + 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = f(x) = {tex}\left(\frac{6 x+5}{x+1}\right) e^{x^{2}}{/tex}

538592.explanation:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{(1-x) \sqrt{x}}=1-\sqrt{x}{/tex} (Linear)
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{(1-x) \sqrt{x}} d x}{/tex}
Let x = t2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = 2t dt
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{\left(1-t^{2}\right) t} \cdot 2 t d t}{/tex}
= {tex}e^{\frac{2}{2} \log \frac{1+t}{1-t}} {/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1+t}{1-t}=\frac{1+\sqrt{x}}{1-\sqrt{x}}{/tex}
General solution of the given equation is
y {tex}\times{/tex} I.F. = {tex}\int(1-\sqrt{x}) \times{/tex} I.F. dx + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow y\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{x}}{1-\sqrt{x}}\right)=\int(1+\sqrt{x}){/tex} dx + c
= x + {tex}\frac{2}{3} x \sqrt{x}{/tex} + c ...(i)
y(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y{tex}\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{x}}{1-\sqrt{x}}\right)=x+\frac{2}{3} x \sqrt{x}{/tex}
Substituting x = 4, we get
y(4) = -{tex}\frac{1}{3}\left(4+\frac{16}{3}\right)=-\frac{28}{9}{/tex}

538595.explanation:
y2 dx + (x - {tex}\frac 1y{/tex}) dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{1}{y^{2}} x=\frac{1}{y^{3}}{/tex}
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{y^{2}} d y}=e^{-\frac{1}{y}}{/tex}
General solution of the given equation is
x {tex}\cdot e^{-\frac{1}{y}}=\int \frac{1}{y^{3}} \cdot e^{-\frac{1}{y}}{/tex} dy + c
{tex}\Rightarrow x e^{-\frac{1}{y}}=e^{-\frac{1}{y}}\left(\frac{1}{y}+1\right){/tex} + c ...(i)
y(1) = 1 i.e., y = 1, when x = 1 (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1.e-1 = e-1(1 + 1) + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -e-1
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow x e^{-\frac{1}{y}}=e^{-\frac{1}{y}}\left(\frac{1}{y}+1\right){/tex} - e-1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = 1 + {tex}\frac{1}{y}-\frac{e^{\frac 1 y}}{e}{/tex}

538598.explanation:
Given differential equation
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x+2 y^{3}}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} = 2y2 (Linear)
I.F. = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{1}{v} d y}{/tex}
= e-log y ={tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex}
General solution of the given equation is
{tex}\frac{x}{y}=\int 2 y^{2} \times \frac{1}{y}{/tex} dy + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{x}{y}{/tex} = y2 + c ...(i)
y(1) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (8, 2) lies on the curve.

538601.explanation:
{tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} + g'(x) y(x) = g(x) g’(x)
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int g^{\prime}(x) d x}{/tex} = eg(x)
General solution of the given equation is
y(x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} eg(x) = {tex}\int{/tex}g(x) g'(x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} eg(x) dx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} eg(x) = eg(x) [g(x) - 1] + c ...(i)
Substituting x = 0 in (i), we get
0 = e0 (0 - 1) + c ...[y(0) = 0, g(0) = 0 (given)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y (x) eg(x) = eg(x) [g(x) - 1] + 1
Substituting x = 2, we get
y (2) e0 = e0(0 - 1) + 1 ...[g(2) = 0 (given)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y (2) = 0

538604.explanation:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{2}{/tex} sec x = {tex}\frac{\tan x}{2 y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} + y2 sec x = tan x
Let y2 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {dt}{dx}{/tex} + t sec x = tan x (Linear)
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \sec x d x}{/tex} = elog (sec x + tan x) = sec x + tan x
General solution of the given equation is
t (sec x + tan x) = {tex}\int{/tex} tan x (sec x + tan x) dx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t (sec x + tan x) = {tex}\int{/tex}(sec x tan x + sec2 x - 1) dx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 (sec x + tan x) = sec x + tan x - x + c ...(i)
y(0) = 1, i.e., y = 1, when x = 0 (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(sec 0 + tan 0) = sec 0 + tan 0 - 0 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 (sec x + tan x) = sec x + tan x - x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 = 1 - {tex}\frac{x}{\sec x+\tan x}{/tex}

538607.explanation:
The equation of the tangent at P(x, y) is Y - y = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}(X - x)
This tangent meets the y-axis at (0, y - x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}).
Given condition
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y - x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}= x3
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} y = -x2 (Linear)
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} = e-log x = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
General solution of the given equation is
y {tex}\cdot \frac{1}{x}=\int-x^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{x}{/tex} + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{y}{x}=-\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y = -{tex}\frac{x^{3}}{2}{/tex} + cx
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -{tex}\frac{x^{3}}{2}{/tex} + cx ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(1) = -{tex}\frac 12{/tex} + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 1 = -{tex}\frac 12{/tex} + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = {tex}\frac 32{/tex}
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -{tex}\frac{x^{3}}{2}+\frac{3}{2} x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(-3) = {tex}\frac{27}{2}-\frac{9}{2}{/tex} = 9

538610.explanation:
Let r be the instantaneous radius of the ball.
Then {tex}\frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}\right){/tex} = k(4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2) (k: constant of proportionality)
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} = k(4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} = k = constant
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} The rate at which the radius is decreasing is constant.
Half of the ball disappears in one day ...[Given]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Remaining half would disappear in one more day.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Total time required = 2 days

538613.explanation:
R represents the instantaneous position of the rabbit and the dog runs always in the tangential direction of the rabbit.
At an instant t, the rabbit covers the distance of 10 t.
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{10 t-y}{x}\right){/tex} = p
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} px = y - 10t
Differentiating with respect to x, we get
p + x {tex}\frac{d p}{d x}{/tex} = p - 10 {tex}\frac{dt}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=-\frac{10}{x}, \frac{d x}{d p}{/tex}
Also, {tex}\frac{dS}{d t}{/tex} = 10 = {tex}\frac{dS}{d x} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
= -{tex}\sqrt{1+p^{2}}\left(-\frac{10}{x} \cdot \frac{d x}{d p}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{d p}{\sqrt{1+p^{2}}}=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} log | p + {tex}\sqrt{1+p^{2}}{/tex} | = log |x| + log c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p + {tex}\sqrt{1+p^{2}}{/tex} = cx
At x = 1, p = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - p)2 = 1 + p2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 2x p - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = {tex}\frac{x^{2}-1}{2 x}{/tex}
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int_\limits{0}^{y}{/tex} dy = {tex}\int_\limits{1}^{x} \frac{x^{2}-1}{2 x}{/tex} dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log x - {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

538616.explanation:

Integrating on both sides, we get

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\pi \mathrm{H}^{\frac{3}{2}}}{s \sqrt{2 g}} \tan ^{2} \alpha{/tex} dH = -dt
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{s \sqrt{2 g}} \frac{2}{5} \mathrm{H}^{\frac{5}{2}} \tan ^{2} \alpha{/tex} = t
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{2 \pi}{s \sqrt{2 g}} \frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{R}^{2}}{5}{/tex} = t ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\sqrt{h}=\frac{\sqrt{H}}{5} \Rightarrow{/tex} H = 25 h

538629.explanation:

538671.explanation:

{tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{2(1-y)}-\frac{1}{2(1+y)}\right] {/tex}dy = {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{1+x}\right]{/tex}dx + log c

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\frac{y}{\sqrt{1-y}}{/tex} - log{tex}\sqrt {1 +y}{/tex} = log {tex}\frac x{1+x}{/tex} + log c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\frac{y}{\sqrt{1-y}}{/tex} - log{tex}\frac x{1+x}{/tex} = log c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\frac{y(1+x)}{x \sqrt{1-y^{2}}}{/tex} = log c
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y(1+x)}{x \sqrt{1-y^{2}}}{/tex} = c

538685.explanation:

Integrating on both sides, we get
2{tex}\int{/tex}dx = 2{tex}\int\frac {dv}{1-v^2}{/tex} + log c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2x = 2{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \log \left(\frac{1+v}{1-v}\right){/tex} + log c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2x = log{tex}\left(\frac{1+v}{1-v}\right)\cdot{/tex} c {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} e2x = {tex}\left(\frac{1+v}{1-v}\right) c{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e2x (1 - x + y) = 1 + x - y

538688.explanation:

{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} (x - 2y) + {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}log [4(x - 2y) + 5] = x + c1

538691.explanation:

538694.explanation:

{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+1}{x(x-1)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y+1} d y=\frac{1}{x(x-1)} d x{/tex} (Variables separable)

{tex}\int \frac{1}{y+1} d y=\int\left[\frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex}dx + log c

The curve passes through (2, -2).

(i) gives
x(y + 1) = -2(x - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x (y + 1) + 2(x - 1) = 0

538697.explanation:
(x2 + y2) dx = 2xy dy
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}}{2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} ...(i)
(Homogeneous)
Let y = vx ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(iii)

v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex} - v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1-v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex} (Variables separable)
Integrating on both sides, we get

{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (1 - v2){tex}\cdot{/tex}x = {tex}e^{c_1}{/tex}

{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} c{tex}\cdot{/tex}(x2 - y2) = x, where c = {tex}\frac{1}{e^{c_{1}}}{/tex}

538700.explanation:
{tex}\frac {dy}{dx} = \frac{x-y}{x+y}{/tex} ...(i)
(Homogeneous)
Let y = vx ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(iii)
Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
v + x{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x-v x}{x+v x} \Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1-v}{1+v}{/tex} - v
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1-2 v-v^{2}}{1+v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+v}{1-2 v-v^{2}} d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} (Variables separable)
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{1+v}{2-(1+v)^{2}} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} + log c1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log[2 - (1 + v)2] = log x + log c1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log(1 - 2v - v2) = log (xc1)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \log \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-2 v-v^{2}}}\right){/tex} =  log (xc1)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-2 v-v^{2}}}{/tex} = xc1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1-2 v-v^{2}}=x^{2} c_{1}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} c_{1}^{2}{/tex} (1 - 2v - v2) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} c_{1}^{2}\left(1-\frac{2 y}{x}-\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow c_{1}^{2}{/tex} (x2 - 2xy - y2) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 + 2xy - x2  = c, where c = -{tex}\frac{1}{c_{1}^{2}}{/tex}

x + y{tex}e^{\frac xy}{/tex} = constant

(cos v)(2v + x{tex}\frac {dv}{dx}{/tex}) = (v sin v)(x {tex}\frac {dv}{dx}{/tex})
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2v + x{tex}\frac {dv}{dx}{/tex} = (v tan v) (x {tex}\frac {dv}{dx}{/tex})
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2v = (v tan v - 1) (x {tex}\frac {dv}{dx}{/tex})
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{d x}{x}=\left(\frac{v \tan v-1}{2 v}\right) d v{/tex} (Variables separable)
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}=\int \frac{v \tan v-1}{2 v}{/tex} dv + log c1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} log x = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} log sec v - {tex}\frac 12{/tex} log v + log c1
= {tex}\frac 12{/tex} log {tex}\frac{1}{v \cos v}{/tex} + log c1
= {tex}\frac 12{/tex} log {tex}\frac{x}{y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} + log c1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 = {tex}\frac{x c}{y \cos \frac{y}{x}}{/tex}, where c = {tex}c_1^2{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} xy cos {tex}\frac yx{/tex} = c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = 1, n = 1, f(x, y) = cos ({tex}\frac yx{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} mn = 1, m + n = 2, f(1, 0) = cos 0 = 1
f(1, 1) = cos 1 > 0

 {tex}\frac{1}{{OA}}+\frac{1}{{OB}}{/tex} = 1

538758.explanation:

Integrate on both sides,
{tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}}\right) \pm{/tex} x + c

which represents a parabola.

538760.explanation:
Let h and r be the water level and radius, in cms, at any instant t.
r = {tex}\sqrt{100^{2}-(100-h)^{2}}{/tex}
Volume V = {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{h} \pi r^{2} d h=\pi \int_\limits{0}^{h}{/tex}(200h - h2) dh
= {tex}\pi\left(100 h^{2}-\frac{h^{3}}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d \mathrm{~V}}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex}(200 h - h2) {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d \mathrm{~V}}{d t}{/tex} = -12 {tex}\lambda \sqrt{h}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{\pi\left(200 h-h^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{h}}{/tex} dh = -12{tex}\lambda{/tex} dt
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\pi \int_\limits{100}^{0}\left(200 \sqrt{h}-h^{\frac{3}{2}}\right) d h=-\int_\limits{0}^{1} 12 \lambda{/tex} dt
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \pi\left(200 \times \frac{2}{3}(100)^{\frac{3}{2}}-\frac{2}{5}(100)^{\frac{5}{2}}\right)=12 \lambda{/tex} t
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{12}\left(\frac{4}{3}-\frac{2}{5}\right){/tex} 105 = t
{tex}\Rightarrow t=\frac{7}{9} \times 10^{4} \frac{\pi}{\lambda}{/tex}

538762.explanation:

Squaring on both sides, we get
({tex}y_2^3{/tex} - y1 - 16)2 = 64y1
The highest order derivative is y2 with degree 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The degree of given differential equation = 6

538764.explanation:
{tex}\left[1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\sin \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\right]^{\frac{3}{4}}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\sin \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{-3}=\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{4}\right.{/tex}
The highest order derivative is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The order of the differential equation = 2
The given differential equation cannot be expressed as a polynomial in differential coefficients, the degree is not defined.

538766.explanation:
y = (c1 + c2) cos (x + c3) - c4 sin x + c5 {tex}e^{-x + c_6}{/tex}
Let c1 + c2 = c7
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = c7 cos (x + c3) - c4 sin x + c5 {tex}\cdot e^{c_{6}} \cdot e^{-x}{/tex}
Let c5 {tex}e^{c_6}{/tex} = c8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = c7 cos (x + c3) - c4 sin x + c8 e-x
Clearly, the relation contains three arbitrary constants. So, the order of differential equation, satisfying it, is 3.

538768.explanation:
f" (x) = g" (x) - 1
Integrating with respect to x, we get
f'(x) = g' (x) - x + c ...(i)
Substituting x = 2, we get
f' (2) = g' (2) - 2 + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = 1 - 2 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 4
From (i),
f'(x) = g'(x) - x + 4
Integrating with respect to x, we get
f(x) = g (x) - {tex}\frac {x^2}2{/tex} + 4x + c1
Substituting x = 4, we get
f(4) = g(4) - {tex}\frac {16}2{/tex} + 16 + c1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 = 1 - 8 + 16 + c1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = g(x) - {tex}\frac {x^2}2{/tex} + 4x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(6) = g (6) - {tex}\frac {6^2}2{/tex} + 4(6)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(6) - g (6) = 24 - 18 = 6

538770.explanation:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\tan \frac{y}{x}{/tex} we can clearly see that it is an homogeneous equation
substituting {tex}y=v x \rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\tan v{/tex}
separating the variables and integrating we get
{tex} \int \frac{1}{\tan v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x {/tex}
{tex} \log (\sin {v})=\log {x}+\log {c} {/tex}
{tex} \sin v=x c \rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=x c \text { is the solution where } {c} \text { is constant. } {/tex}

538774.explanation:
y2 = 2c (x + {tex}\sqrt c{/tex}) ...(i)
Differentiating with respect to x, we get
2y{tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} = 2c ...(ii)
Differentiating with respect to x, we get
y2 = 2y{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}\left(x+\sqrt{y \frac{d y}{d x}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 \frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \sqrt{y \frac{d y}{d x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(y-2 x \frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}=4 y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}{/tex}
This is a differential equation of order 1 and degree 3.

538781.explanation:
(x - y) (dx + dy) = dx - dy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y - 1) dx + (x - y + 1) dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x-y-1}{x-y+1}{/tex} ...(i)
Let x - y = v ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow 1-\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=1-\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(iii)
Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
1 - {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{v-1}{v+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v}{v+1} \Leftrightarrow \frac{v+1}{2 v}{/tex} dv = dx
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2 v}\right){/tex} dv = {tex}\int{/tex}dx + c1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} v+\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log v = x + c1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) + log (x - y) = 2x + c, where c = 2c1
y (0) = -1, i.e., y = -1, when x = 0 (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 + 1 + log (0 + 1) = 2(0) + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) + log (x - y) = 2x + 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} log (x - y) = x + y + 1

538783.explanation:
(x + 2){tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} = x2 + 4x - 9
{tex}\Rightarrow d y=\frac{x^{2}+4 x-9}{x+2}{/tex}dx (Variables separable)
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int{/tex} dy = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+4 x-9}{x+2}{/tex} dx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\int \frac{(x+2)^{2}-13}{x+2}{/tex} dx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\int\left(x+2-\frac{13}{x+2}\right){/tex} dx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + 2x - 13 log |(x + 2)| + c
y(0) = 0 i.e., y = 0, when x = 0 (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 = 0 - 13 log 2 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 13 log 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + 2x - 13 log |x + 2| + 13 log 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(-4) = 8 - 8 - 13 log 2 + 13 log 2 = 0

538785.explanation:
{tex}x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+2 \sqrt{x y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\sqrt{x} \frac{d y}{d x}+\sqrt{y}\right)^{2}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{x} \frac{d y}{d x}+\sqrt{y}{/tex} = 0
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{\sqrt{y}}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} = c
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{y}+2 \sqrt{x}{/tex} = c
{tex}\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{y}=\sqrt{a}{/tex}, where {tex}\sqrt{a}=\frac{c}{2}{/tex}

538787.explanation:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} = 0
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} = sin-1 c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin-1 y + sin-1 x = sin-1 c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin-1 {tex}\left(x \sqrt{1-y^{2}}+y \sqrt{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} = sin-1 c
{tex}\Rightarrow x \sqrt{1-y^{2}}+y \sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} = c

538789.explanation:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{(x+y+1)^{2}}{/tex} ...(i)
Let x + y + 1 = v ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow 1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} - 1 ...(iii)
Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{v^2}{/tex} + 1
Integrating on both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{v^{2}}{v^{2}+1}{/tex} dv = {tex}\int{/tex}dx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{1}{v^{2}+1}\right){/tex} dv = {tex}\int{/tex}dx + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} v - tan-1 v = x + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x + y + 1 - tan-1 (x + y + 1) = x + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y + 1 - tan-1 (x + y + 1) = c ...(iv)
y(-1) = 0 i.e., y = 0, when x = -1 (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 + 1 - tan-1 (-1 + 0 + 1) = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1
(iv) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + 1 - tan-1 (x + y + 1) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = tan-1 (x + y + 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan y = x + y + 1

Therefore, the number of arbitrary constants in the general solution of a second order D.E is 2.

Substituting t = 0, x = 0, we get
log {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex} = 0 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = log {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\sqrt{2 x+5}{/tex} = t + log {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex}

For x = 200,
t = log {tex}\sqrt {81}{/tex} = loge 9 = 2 loge 3

f' (x) = f(x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}{/tex} = 1

1020750.explanation:
Let R be a relation on the set of all intergers Z, defined by aRb {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} b {tex}\forall {/tex} a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Z

  1. Reflexive: For 1 {tex}\in{/tex} Z
    1 {tex}\not R{/tex} 1 as 1 {tex}\not>{/tex} 1 so (1,1) {tex}\notin{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is not reflexive on Z.
  2. Symmetric: (3, 2) e R as 3 > 2
    But (2, 3) {tex}\notin{/tex} R as 2 {tex}\not >{/tex} 3
    Hence R is not symmetric on Z.
  3. Transitive: Let (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R a > b and b > c
    Now a > b > c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a > c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
    Hence R is a transitive relation on Z.

1021663.explanation:
We are given a {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} determinant with elements involving powers of {tex}\omega{/tex}, the cube roots of unity. Let {tex}x=\omega^n{/tex}. Since {tex}\omega^3=1, x^3=1{/tex}, so {tex}x \in{/tex} {tex}\left\{1, \omega, \omega^2\right\}{/tex}. The matrix becomes:
{tex} \left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & x & x^2 \\ x & x^2 & 1 \\ x^2 & 1 & x \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Expanding the determinant and simplifying each {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} minor, we find all terms cancel due to {tex}x^3=1{/tex}. Hence, the determinant evaluates to zero for any integer {tex}n{/tex}. The final answer is 0 .

1023680.explanation:

f'(x) = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3x^{3} & \cos x & -\sin x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ p & p^{2} & p^{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex} + {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3x^{3} & \cos x & -\sin x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ p & p^{2} & p^{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex} + {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x^{3} & \sin x & \cos x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3x^{3} & \cos x & -\sin x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ p & p^{2} & p^{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex} + 0 + 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3x^{3} & \cos x & -\sin x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ p & p^{2} & p^{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f''(x) = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 6x & -\sin x & -\cos x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ p & p^{2} & p^{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex} + {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3x^{3} & \cos x & -\sin x \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ p & p^{2} & p^{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex} + {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3x^{3} & \cos x & -\sin x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 6x & \sin x & -\cos x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ p & p^{2} & p^{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex}
Similarly, f''(x) = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 6x & \sin x & -\cos x \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ p & p^{2} & p^{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f''(0) = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -1 & 0 \\ 6 & -1 & 0 \\ p & p^{2} & p^{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0

1023958.explanation:
h(x) = f(x) - {f(x))2 + f(x)}3
h'(x) = f'(x) - 2 (f(x) f'(x) + 3{(f(x)}2 f'(x)
= {1 - 2(f(x) + 3{(f(x)}2}f'(x)
= 3{{tex}\frac 13-\frac 23{/tex}f(x) + (f(x))2} f'(x)
=  {tex}\left(3\left|(f(x))^{2}-\frac{2}{3} f(x)+\frac{1}{9}\right|+\frac{2}{3} ) \right.{/tex} f'(x)
= {tex}\left|3\left\{f(x)-\frac{1}{3}\right\}^{2}+\frac{2}{3}\right|{/tex} f'(x)
If f(x) is increasing, f'(x) > 0 and therefore h'(x) > 0 i.e., h(x) is increasing.
If f(x) is decreasing, f'(x) < 0 and h' (x) < 0 i.e., h(x) is decreasing.

Also 2a = 4 {tex}\neq{/tex} b
Now when a = 1, b = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} gcd (1, 2) = 1.
Also now 2a = 2 = b
Hence a = 2b
So, not symmetric
For transitive:

gcd (a, b) = 1, gcd(b, c) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} gcd (a, c) = 7
Hence not transitive

Which is true again when 3 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} divisible by 7, i.e. when {tex}\alpha{/tex} divided by 7, remainder is 4.

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+12 y=\cos \left(\frac{\pi x}{12}\right){/tex}
IF. = {tex}e^{12 \int d x}{/tex} = e12x
Solution is: {tex}y \cdot e^{12 x}{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{12 x} \cdot \cos \left(\frac{\pi x}{12}\right){/tex}dx + C
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot e^{12 x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{12 x}}{12^{2}+\left(\frac{\pi}{12}\right)^{2}}{/tex}{tex}\left[12 \cos \frac{\pi x}{12}+\frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left(\frac{\pi x}{12}\right)\right]+c{/tex}
Put x = 0, y = 0 we get
{tex}C=-\frac{12}{12^{2}+\left(\frac{\pi}{12}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
So y = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\underbrace{12 \cos \left(\frac{\pi x}{12}\right)+\frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left(\frac{\pi x}{12}\right)}_{f_{1}(x)}-12 e^{-12 x}\right]{/tex}{tex}\left[Let \frac {1}{\lambda} = \frac {1}{(\frac {\pi}{12})^2 + 12^2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}\left[[\underbrace{-\pi \sin \left(\frac{\pi x}{12}\right)+\frac{\pi^{2}}{12^{2}} \cos \frac{\pi x}{12}}_{f_{2}(x)}+144 e^{-12 x}\right]{/tex}
When x is large then 12e-12x tends to zero.
But f2(x) varies in {tex}\left[-\sqrt{\pi^{2}+\left(\frac{\pi}{12}\right)^{4}},\sqrt{\pi^{2}+\left(\frac{\pi}{12}\right)^{4}}\right]{/tex}
Hence {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is changing its sign.
So, y(x) is non-monotonic for all real number.
Also when x is very large then again -12e-12x is almost zero but f1(x) is periodic, so there exist some {tex}\beta{/tex} for which y = {tex}\beta{/tex} intersect y = y(x) at infinitely many points.

so, It is linear equation.

Solution of differential equation is,

y {tex}\times{/tex} sec2 x = {tex}\int{/tex}(sin x {tex}\times{/tex} sec2x)dx + c

{tex}y\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\left(\sec \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\sec \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+c{/tex}
0 = 2 + c

from (i)

y = cos x - 2cos2 x
Differentiate w.r.t. x both sides,
y' = -sin x + 4cos x sin x
y' = 0
4cos x sin x - sin x = 0
sin x (4cos x - 1) = 0
Take, sin x = 0, 4cos x - 1 = 0
x = 0, cos x = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Put x = 0 in the value of y.
y = cos 0 - 2 cos2 0 = 1 - 2 = -1
Put cos x = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} in value of y.
y = cos x - 2 cos2 x
= {tex}\frac{1}{4}-2 \times \frac{1}{16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{8}=\frac{2-1}{8}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
Therefore, maximum value is {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}.

1058915.explanation:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} p & q \\ r & s \end{array}\right]{/tex}
A2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} p^2+q r & p q+q s \\ p r+r s & q r+s^2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Comparing both side
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 + qr = 1 ...(i)
pq + qs = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q(p + s) = 0 ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s2 + qr = 1 ...(iii)
pr + rs = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r(p + s) = 0 ...(iv)
Equation (i) - equation (ii)
p2 = s2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p + s = 0
Now 3a2 + 4b2 = 3(p + s)2 + 4(ps - qr)2
= 3.0 + 4(-p2 - qr)2 = 4(p2 + qr)2 = 4

1058920.explanation:
Given, P2 = I - P,
{tex}\text P^\alpha + \text P^\beta{/tex} = {tex}\gamma \text I{/tex} - 29P, {tex}\text P^\alpha - \text P^\beta{/tex} = {tex}\delta \text I{/tex} - 13P
Now, P4 = (I - P)2 = I - 2P + P2 = 21 - 3P
P6 = (2I - 3P) (I - P) = 51 - 8P
P8 = (21 - 3P)2 = 41 - 12P + 9(1 - P) = 13I - 21P
P8 + P6 = 18I - 29P and P8 - P6 = 8I - 13P
So, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 8; {tex}\beta{/tex} = 6; {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 18, {tex}\delta{/tex} = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} + {tex}\gamma{/tex} - {tex}\delta{/tex} = 8 + 6 + 18 - 8 = 24

1058924.explanation:
Given matrix is A = {tex}\left(\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -2 \\ \alpha & \beta \end{array}\right){/tex}
The characteristic equation for A is |A - {tex}\lambda \text I{/tex}| = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 4-\lambda & -2 \\ \alpha & \beta-\lambda \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
expand w.r.t. R1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (4 - {tex}\lambda{/tex}) ({tex}\beta{/tex} - {tex}\lambda{/tex}) + {tex}2\alpha{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda^2{/tex} - ({tex}\beta{/tex} - 4){tex}\lambda{/tex}) + {tex}4\beta{/tex} + {tex}4\alpha{/tex} = 0
Here, {tex}\lambda{/tex} should replace by A.
Then A2 - ({tex}\beta{/tex} + 4)A+ ({tex}4\beta{/tex} + {tex}2\alpha{/tex}) I = 0
Compare above equation with given equation.
-9({tex}\beta{/tex} + 4) = {tex}\gamma{/tex} & {tex}4\beta{/tex} + {tex}2\alpha{/tex} = 18
and |A| = {tex}4\beta{/tex} + {tex}2\beta{/tex} = 18

1058927.explanation:
Given x3dy + (xy - 1)dx = 0
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-x y}{x^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x^{3}}-\frac{y}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{x^{3}}{/tex}
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x}=e^{-\frac{1}{x}}{/tex}
So, for solution
{tex}y.e e^{-\frac{1}{x}}{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{-\frac{1}{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{x^{3}} d x{/tex} (put {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} = t)
{tex}y \cdot e^{-\frac{1}{x}}{/tex} = {tex}-\int e^{t} \cdot{/tex}tdt = et - tet + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} + 1 + {tex}\mathrm{Ce}^{\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}}{/tex}, where C is constant
Put x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}  then, 3 - e = 2 + 1 + Ce2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}{/tex}
So y = {tex}\frac{1}{x}+1-\frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}{e}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(1) = 1

1058934.explanation:
Given relation det ((A + I)(adj(A) + I)) = 4, det (A) = -1,
Then, adj A = -A-1.
|(A + I) A-1 + I| = 4
|-I + A - A-1 + I| = 4
|A - A-1| = 4
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex} then  A-1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -\mathrm{d} & \mathrm{b} \\ \mathrm{c} & -\mathrm{a} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
|A - A-1| = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} a+d & 0 \\ 0 & d+a \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 4
(a + d)2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + d = {tex}\pm2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a + d| = 2

 A25 = A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A25 - A = 0 Then, A30 + A25 - A = I

1058942.explanation:
Given the differential equation
(x2 - 3y2)dx + 3xy dy = 0
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 y^{2}-x^{2}}{3 x y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\frac{1}{3} \frac{x}{y}{/tex}
Let y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
so, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v + {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}v-\frac{1}{3} \frac{1}{v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x} \Rightarrow-\frac{-1}{3 v}{/tex}
Integrating both side
{tex}\frac{v^{2}}{2}=\frac{-1}{3}{/tex} ln x + c since v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}, so {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{2 x^{2}}=\frac{-1}{3}{/tex}ln x + c
now y(1) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = c
so, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{2 x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{3}{/tex}lnx + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 = {tex}-\frac{2}{3} x^{2}{/tex} In x + x2
thus y2(e) = {tex}-\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{e}^{2}{/tex} + e2 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6y2(e) = 2e2

1058949.explanation:

Here B = I - 5C1 (adj A) + 5C2 (adj A)2 + ... + 5C5 (adj A)5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I - adj A)5 = {tex}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{array}\right]\right)^5{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right]^5{/tex}
Now, P2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right],{/tex} P3 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & -3 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
P4 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right],{/tex} P5 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & -5 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} B = P5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = (I - adj A)5 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right]^5{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -1 & -5 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Sum of its all elements = -7

1058950.explanation:

1058963.explanation:
Given that A is symmetric matrix {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AT = A, B is skew symmetric
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BT = -B

  1. C = A4 - B4
    CT = (A4 - B4)T = (A4)T - (B4)T = A4 - B4 = C symmetric
  2. C = AB - BA
    CT = (AB - BA)T = (AB)T - (BA)T
    = BTAT - ATBT = -BA + AB = C symmetric
  3. C = B5 - A5
    CT = (B5 - A5)T = (B5)T - (A5)T = -B5 - A5
  4. C = AB + BA
    CT = (AB + BA)T = (AB)T + (BA)T
    = -BA - AB = -C (Skew Symmetric)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} B5 - A5 is a skew-symmetric matrix is not true.

1059003.explanation:
Let A be symmetric matrix and B be skew symmetric matrix.

Consider

1059007.explanation:

Now {tex}\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow \infty}\left(y \cdot e^{x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow \infty}\left(\tan ^{-1}\left(e^{x}\right)+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}

1059017.explanation:

|A| = 1 + a2

|A - {tex}\mu \text I{/tex}| = 0

{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2, {tex}\beta{/tex} = 8

1059020.explanation:

Take, {tex}\vec{r} \times \vec{a}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a} \Rightarrow(\vec{r}-\vec{c}) \times \vec{a}=0{/tex}
So, {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{c}+\lambda \vec{a} \vec{r} \cdot \vec{b}=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{c} \cdot \vec{b}+\lambda \vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}=0{/tex}
Now, put the values of {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} & {tex}\vec{c}{/tex}.

{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{r}|=\frac{11 \sqrt{2}}{7}{/tex}

1059030.explanation:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x^{2}+11 x+13}{x^{3}+6 x^{2}+11 x+6}\right) y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x+3}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I.F = {tex}e^{\int \rho(x) d x}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\int p(\mathrm{x}) d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\left(2 x^{2}+11 x+13\right) d x}{(\mathrm{x}+1)(\mathrm{x}+2)(\mathrm{x}+3)}{/tex}
Using partial fraction
{tex}\frac{2 x^{2}+11 x+13}{(\mathrm{x}+1)(\mathrm{x}+2)(\mathrm{x}+3)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{P}{x+1}+\frac{Q}{x+2}+\frac{R}{x+3}{/tex}
P = {tex}\frac{4}{2}{/tex} = 2
Q = 1
R = -1
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex}p(x) dx = p ln (x + 1) + Q ln (x + 2) + R ln (x + 3)
= ln{tex}\left(\frac{(x+1)^{2}(x+2)}{x+3}\right){/tex}
from (i),
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int p(\mathbf{x}) \mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(x+1)^{2}(x+2)}{(x+3)}{/tex}
Solution y (IF) = {tex}\int{/tex}Q{tex}\cdot{/tex}(IF) dx
{tex}y\left(\frac{(x+1)^{2}(x+2)}{x+3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\int\left(\frac{x+3}{x+1}\right) \frac{(x+1)^{2}(x+2)}{(x+3)} d x{/tex}
{tex}y\left(\frac{(x+1)^{2}(x+2)}{x+3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}+\frac{3 x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + 2x + C
Satisfy (0, 1) then C = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
Now put x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(1) = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

1059036.explanation:
Use determinant rule for area of triangle.
{tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \alpha & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & \alpha & 1 \\ 0 & \alpha & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\pm4{/tex}
Expand w. r. t. R1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\pm8{/tex}
Therefore, given points are (8, -8), (-8, 8), (64, {tex}\beta{/tex}).
If three points are collinear then slope will be equal by using two different co-ordinates.
{tex}\frac{-8-8}{8+8}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{64+8}{\beta-8}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = -64

{tex} \Rightarrow \vec {\mathbf{r}}=\vec {\mathrm{c}}+\lambda \vec {\mathrm{b}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec {\mathrm{r}} \cdot \vec {\mathrm{a}}=0 \Rightarrow(\vec {\mathrm{c}}+\lambda \vec {\mathrm{b}}) \cdot \vec {\mathrm{a}}=\mathbf{0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec {\mathrm{c}} \cdot \vec {\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \vec {\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec {\mathrm{a}}=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}}{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}}=-\frac{(5-6+9)}{(1-2+6)}=-\frac{-8}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\vec {\mathrm{r}}=\frac{5(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})-8(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{5} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec {\mathrm{r}}=\frac{17 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{5}{/tex}
{tex}|\vec {\mathrm{r}}|=\frac{1}{25}(289+50) {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 25|\vec {\mathrm{r}}|^{2}=339{/tex}

1059065.explanation:

Now {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=\alpha(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c})-\vec{n} \cdot \vec{c} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=\alpha(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}){/tex}
and {tex}|\vec{c} \times(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})|=|(\vec{c} \cdot \vec{b}) \vec{a}-(\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}) \vec{b}|{/tex} 
{tex}=|(\vec{c} \cdot \vec{b}) \vec{a}-\alpha(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}) \vec{b}|=|(\vec{c} \cdot \vec{b})||\vec{a}-\alpha \vec{b}|{/tex}

1059066.explanation:
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a & b & c \\ b & c & a \\ c & a & b \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a+b+c & a+b+c & a+b+c \\ b & c & a \\ c & a & b \end{array}\right|{/tex}
= (a + b +c){tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ b & c & a \\ c & a & b \end{array}\right|{/tex}
Apply C1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C1 - C2 and C2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C2 - C3
= (a + b + c){tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ b-c & c-a & a \\ c-a & a-b & b \end{array}\right|{/tex}
= (a + b + c) [ab + bc + ca - a2 - b2 - c2]
= -(a + b + c) [(a - b)2 + (b - c)2 + (c - a)2]
Since a, b, c are sides of a scalene triangle, therefore all sides a, b, c will be unequal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (a - b)2 + (b - c)2 + (c - a)2 > 0 Also a + b + c > 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -(a + b + c) [(a - b)2 + (b - c)2 + (c - a)2] < 0

1059067.explanation:
Given matrix is
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
A2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -4 & 0 \\ 0 & -4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = -4I
 A3 = -4A
A4 = (-4I) (-4I) = (-4)2I
A5 = (-4)2 A, A6 = (-4)3I
Take, M = {tex}\sum\limits_{\mathrm k=1}^{10}\mathrm A^{2\mathrm k}{/tex} = A2 + A4 +... + A20
= [-4 + (-4)2 + (-4)3 + ... + (-4)10]I
It is GP. with a = -4, r = -4 and n = 10.
S10 = {tex}\frac{(-4)}{5}{/tex}(2)10 - 1)I
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M is symmetric matrix
N = {tex}\sum\limits_{\mathrm k=1}^{10}\mathrm A^{2\mathrm k-1}{/tex} = A + A3 + ... + A19
= A[1 + (-4) + (-4)2 + ... + (-4)9]
Similarly, N = {tex}\frac {A[1(-4)10 - 1)}{(-4 -1)]} = \frac{\left.\mathrm{A}\left[(2)^{20}-1\right)\right]}{5}{/tex}
So, N is skew symmetric matrix
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N2 is symmetric matrix
Therefore MN2 is non-identity symmetric matrix.

1059074.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1} \mathrm{y}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{\mathrm{x}+1}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex},
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y{/tex} = Q
IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{x^{2}-1} d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}y\left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\int\left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)^{1} d x{/tex}
= x - 2 loge|x + 1| + C
Since, curve passes through {tex}\left(2, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 2 loge {tex}3-\frac{5}{3}{/tex}; at x = 8, {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} y(8) = 19 - 6 loge 3

1059084.explanation:
Given {tex}\vec{a}=\lambda \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}, {/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-\lambda \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}-((\vec{a}-\vec{b}) {/tex}{tex}\times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})){/tex} {tex}=8 \hat{i}-40 \hat{j}-24 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{b}-\vec{a}) \times((\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})){/tex} {tex}=8 \hat{i}-40 \hat{j}-24 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow((\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}))(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}){/tex} {tex}=8 \hat{i}-40 \hat{j}-24 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 8(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})=8 \hat{i}-40 \hat{j}-24 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec {\mathrm{a}} \times \vec {\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \lambda & 2 & -3 \\ 1 & -\lambda & 2\end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=(4-3 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(2 \lambda+3) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\left(-\lambda^{2}-2\right) \hat{\mathrm{k}} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a}+\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \vec{a}-\vec{b}=3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})=\left|\begin{array}{lll}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 3 & -5\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} required answer = 4 + 100 + 36 = 140

1059092.explanation:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \\ a_3 & b_3 & c_3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
A {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} a_1+b_1 \\ a_2+b_2 \\ a_3+b_3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
a1 + b1 = 1; a2 + b2 = 1; a3 + b3 = 0
A{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} c_1+a_1 \\ c_2+a_2 \\ c_3+a_3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
c1 + a1 = -1; c2 + a2 = 0; c3 + a3 = 1
A{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} c_1 \\ c_2 \\ c_3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c1 = 1, c2 = 1, c3 = 2
Now, solve the above equations, we get
a1 = -2; a2 = -1; a3 = -1
After putting the value of a1, a2, a3 the value of b1, b2, b3 is 3, 2, 1 respectively
So, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} -2 & 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Take, A - 2I = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} -4 & 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
|A - 2I| = 0
Now, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} -4 & 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 4 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
-4x1 + 3x2 + x3 = 4 ...(i)
-x1 + x3 = 1 ...(ii)
-x1 + x2 = 1 ...(iii)
All the variables is independent to other variable, then the system has infinite solution.

1059094.explanation:
Given, {tex}\vec{c}=(2 \vec{a} \times \vec{b})-3 \vec{b}{/tex}
Multiply {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} both sides,
{tex}\vec {\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec {\mathrm{c}}=\vec {\mathrm{b}} \cdot(2 \vec {\mathrm{a}} \times \vec {\mathrm{b}})-3|\vec {\mathrm{b}}|^{2}{/tex}
Since, {tex}\vec{b} \cdot(2 \vec{a} \times \vec{b})=0{/tex}
then, {tex}\vec {\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec {\mathrm{c}}=-3|\vec {\mathrm{b}}|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\vec {\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec {\mathrm{c}}=-3 \times 16=-48 {/tex}

1059099.explanation:
Given differential equation we write as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + (8 + 4cot 2x)y = {tex}\frac{2 e^{-4} x}{\sin ^{2} 2 x}{/tex}(2sin x + cos 2x) ...(i)
Now eqn. (i) is linear differential equation
Now I.F. = {tex}e^{\int(8+4 \cot 2 x) d x}{/tex} = e8x+2 ln (sin 2x)
= e8x{tex}\cdot{/tex}sin2 2x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} solution is given by
y(e8x{tex}\cdot{/tex}sin2 2x) = {tex}\int{/tex}2e4x(2 sin 2x + cos 2x)dx + C
= e4x{tex}\cdot{/tex}sin 2x + C
we have given that {tex}y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} = {tex}e^{-\pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{e^{-4 x}}{\sin 2 x}{/tex}
Now {tex}y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{e}^{-4 \frac{\pi}{6}}}{\sin \left(2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{6}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{2 \pi}{3}}{/tex}

1059114.explanation:
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 3 & 0 \\ 3 & -4 & 5\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=15 \hat{i}-20 \hat{j}-25 \hat{k}{/tex}
Let {tex}\vec{c}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\because \vec{c} \cdot(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})+25=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 15 x-20 y-25 z+25=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x - 4y - 5z = -5 ...(i)
Also, {tex}\vec{c} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=4{/tex}
{tex}x+y+z=4{/tex} ...(ii)
and {tex}\frac{\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=1 \Rightarrow 4 x+3 y=5{/tex} ...(iii)
on solving (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{c}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
Projection of {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} on {tex}\vec{b}=\frac{25}{5 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

1059117.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} A = {tex}\frac 15{/tex}((A + 2B) + 2(2A - B))
= {tex}\frac 15{/tex}{tex}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 6 & -3 & 3 \\ -5 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & -2 & 10 \\ 4 & -2 & 12 \\ 0 & 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac 15{/tex}{tex}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & 10 \\ 10 & -5 & 15 \\ -5 & 5 & 5 \end{array}\right]\right){/tex}

{tex}\Delta{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & \alpha \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 3); {tex}\Delta_1{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 2 & 1 & \alpha \\ 4 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = -(3 + {tex}\alpha{/tex})
{tex}\Delta_2{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & \alpha \\ 3 & 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = -({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 3); {tex}\Delta_3{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
x = {tex}\frac{\Delta_1}{\Delta}=\frac{-(\alpha+3)}{-(3+\alpha)}{/tex} = 1; y = {tex}\frac{\Delta_2}{\Delta}=\frac{-(\alpha+3)}{-(\alpha + 3)}{/tex} = 1
z = {tex}\frac{\Delta_3}{\Delta}=\frac{0}{-(\alpha + 3)}{/tex} = 0
So, x = 1, y = 1, z = 0. Here, {tex}\alpha{/tex} cannot be -3.
Points ({tex}\alpha{/tex}, 1), (1, {tex}\alpha{/tex}) and (1, -1) are collinear, then
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \alpha & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & \alpha & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + {tex}\alpha{/tex} - 1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\pm1{/tex}

f(x) = ax - b

Satisfy (k + 1, 2),
Therefore, tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{1}{k}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(1+\frac{1}{k^2}\right){/tex} = In k
2tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{1}{k}\right){/tex} = In{tex}\left(\frac{1+k^2}{k^2}\right)+2{/tex} In k

1059698.explanation:
We are given that 
f(x) + f(x + k) = n {tex}\forall x \in R{/tex} ...(i)
or {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} f(x + k) + f(x + 2k) = n ...(ii)
Now from (i)
f(x + k) = n - f(x)
Now from eq (ii)
n - f(x) + f(x + 2k) = n
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=f(x+2 k) {/tex} {tex}\forall \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} f(x) is periodic with period 2k
{tex}I_1=\int_\limits0^{4 n k} f(x) d x=2 n \int_\limits0^{2 k} f(x) d x{/tex}
{tex}I_2=\int_\limits{-k}^{3 k} f(x) d x=2 \int_\limits0^{2 k} f(x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} we are given that 
f(x) + f(x + k) = n
{tex}\Rightarrow \int_\limits0^k f(x) d x+\int_\limits0^k f(x+k) d x=n k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int_\limits0^k f(x) d x+\int_\limits k^{2 k} f(x) d x=n k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int_\limits0^{2 k} f(x) d x=n k{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I_1=2 n^2 k, I_2=2 n k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} I1 + nI2 = 4n2k

{tex}x\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-v^2}}+e^v\right) \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 1
Take integral both side,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^{\frac{y}{x}}{/tex} + sin-1{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = 1nx + C
In passes through (1, 0)
1 + 0 = 0 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
It passes through ({tex}2\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\alpha{/tex})
{tex}e^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} + sin-1{tex}\frac 12{/tex} = 1n {tex}2\alpha{/tex} + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1n{tex}2\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{e}+\frac{\pi}{6}-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2\alpha{/tex} = {tex}e^{\left(\sqrt{e}+\frac{\pi}{6}-1\right)}{/tex}

1059821.explanation:

{tex}\int \limits_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{2+3 \sin x}{\sin x(1+\cos x)}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=2 \int \limits_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{\sin x+\sin x \cos x}{/tex}{tex}+3 \int\limits_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{1+\cos x}{/tex}
Let {tex}I_1=\int\limits_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{1+\cos x}=\int\limits_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1-\cos x}{\sin ^2 x} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int\limits_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2}\left(\operatorname{cosec}^2 x-\cot x \operatorname{cosec} x\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I_1=[{cosec} x-\cot x]_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2}{/tex} {tex}=(1)-\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
and let {tex}I_2=\int\limits_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{\sin x(1+\cos x)}{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{d x}{\left(\frac{2 \tan x / 2}{1+\tan ^2 x / 2}\right)\left(1+\frac{1-\tan ^2 x / 2}{1+\tan ^2 x / 2}\right)}{/tex}
Put, {tex} \tan \frac{x}{2}=t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \sec ^2 \frac{x}{2} d x=d t {/tex}
So, {tex}\mathrm{I}_2=\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\frac{1+\mathrm{t}^2}{\mathrm{t}}\right) \mathrm{dt}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\ln \mathrm{t}+\frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}\right]_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(0+\frac{1}{2}\right)-\left(\ln \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{6}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{3}+\ln \sqrt{3}\right) \frac{1}{2} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I_2=\left(\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{2} \ln \sqrt{3}\right){/tex}
Now, I1 + I2 {tex}=2\left(\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{2} \ln \sqrt{3}\right)+3\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{3}+\ln \sqrt{3}+3-\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{3}+\ln \sqrt{3}-\sqrt{3} {/tex}

1059914.explanation:
Given: {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} are unit vectors,
Let {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=(\sin \alpha) \overrightarrow{n_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c} \times \vec{d}=(\sin \beta) \overrightarrow{n_{2}}{/tex}
where {tex}\overrightarrow{n_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{n_{2}}{/tex} are unit normal vectors
then {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{c} \times \vec{d})=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (\sin \alpha) \overrightarrow{n_{1}} \cdot(\sin \beta) \overrightarrow{n_{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin \alpha \sin \beta \overrightarrow{n_{1}} \cdot \overrightarrow{n_{2}}=1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \alpha \sin \beta \cos \gamma=1{/tex}
where {tex}\gamma{/tex} is the angle between {tex}\overrightarrow{n_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{n_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{2}, \beta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\gamma=0^{\circ}{/tex}
If {tex}\gamma=0^{\circ} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \| \vec{c} \times \vec{d}{/tex}
Let {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\lambda(\vec{c} \times \vec{d}) {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=\lambda(\vec{c} \times \vec{d}) \cdot \vec{c}=0{/tex}
and {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \cdot \vec{d}=\lambda(\vec{c} \times \vec{d}) \cdot \vec{d}=0{/tex}
{tex}[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}]=[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{d}]=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar and {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{d}{/tex} are coplanar
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}, \vec{d}{/tex} are coplanar
and {tex}\alpha=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \perp \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\beta=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \vec{c} \perp \vec{d}{/tex}
But angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\pi}{ 3}\left(\because \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}

The possible cases are shown in figures and in any case {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} are non-parallel vectors.

RHL =  {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}}{/tex}(bx + 3)

Also, f(3) = 3a + 1 [{tex}\because{/tex} f(x) = ax + 1]

{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + 1 = 3b + 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a = 3b + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = b + {tex}\frac23{/tex}

1140904.explanation:
Given, a function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R defined as f(x) = x2.
For one-one: Here, at x = 1, f(1) = 1
and at x = -1, f(-1) = (-1)2 = 1
Thus, f(1) = f(-1) = 1, but 1 {tex}\neq{/tex} -1
So, f is not one-one.
For onto: Let y {tex}\in {/tex} R (codomain) be any arbitrary element.
Then, y = f(x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex} \pm \sqrt{y}{/tex}
Now, for y = -2 {tex}\in {/tex} R, x = {tex}\pm \sqrt{-2} \notin R{/tex}
So, f is not onto.
Hence, given function is neither one-one nor onto.

1140921.explanation:
Given function is f(x) = sin x + cos x
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
f'(x) = cos x - sin x
Put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x - sin x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} (0, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex})
Now, {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} divides the interval (0, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}) into two sub intervals (0, {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}) and ({tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}).

In interval (0, {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}), cos x > sin x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} cos x - sin x > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) > 0
So, f(x) is strictly increasing function in (0, {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}).
In interval ({tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}), cos x < sin x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} cos x - sin x < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) < 0
So, f(x) is strictly decreasing function in ({tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}).

1140946.explanation: Given, {tex}f(x)=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4-\frac{1}{2}(2 x)=4-x{/tex} put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 - x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4 Then, we evaluate the f at critical point x = 4 and at the end points of the interval {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} . {tex}f(4)=16-\frac{1}{2}(16){/tex} = 16 - 8 = 8 f(-2) {tex}=-8-\frac{1}{2}(4){/tex} = -8 - 2 = -10 {tex}f\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)=4\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)^2{/tex} {tex}=18-\frac{81}{8}=7.875{/tex} Thus, the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} is 8 occurring at x = 4.
