1140990.explanation: Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0, then LHL = RHL = f(0) or LHL = RHL = k Now, {tex}\mathrm{LHL}=\lim\limits _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{3(0-h)}-e^{-5(0-h)}}{0-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{-3 h}-e^{5 h}}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{-3 h}-1}{-h}\right)+\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{5 h}-1}{h}\right){/tex} {tex}=3 \lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{-3 k}-1}{-3 h}\right)+5 \lim\limits _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{5 h}-1}{5 h}\right){/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 8 Thus, k = 8

1140997.explanation:
Let the given set be A = {1, 2, 3}
and R = {(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 2), (2, 3)}
Reflexive Here, 1, 2, 3 {tex}\in{/tex} A and (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3) {tex}\in{/tex} R
i.e. for all a {tex}\in{/tex} A, (a, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R.
So, R is reflexive.
Symmetric Here, (1, 2) {tex}\in{/tex} R but (2, 1) {tex}\notin{/tex} R.
So, R is not symmetric.
Transitive Here, (1, 2) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (2, 3) {tex}\in{/tex} R but (1, 3 } {tex}\notin{/tex} R.
So, R is not transitive.

1141000.explanation:

Now, for x = 0 and -1, we have
f(0) = 0 and f(-1) = 0
Hence, f(0) = f(-1) but 0 {tex}\neq{/tex} -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is not one one {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is many one.

1141002.explanation: Given, (x 2 + y 2 ) 2 = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 4 + 2x 2 y 2 + y 4 - xy = 0 Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}4 x^3+2\left[2 x y^2+2 x^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]{/tex} {tex}+4 y^3 \frac{d y}{d x}-\left[y+x \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}\left[4 x^2 y+4 y^3-x\right]{/tex} {tex}+\left[4 x^3+4 x y^2-y\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-\left[4 x^3+4 x y^2-y\right]}{\left[4 x^2 y+4 y^3-x\right]}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-4 x\left(x^2+y^2\right)}{4 y\left(x^2+y^2\right)-x}{/tex}

1141003.explanation:
R is reflexive since every triangle is congruent to itself.
Further, {tex}\left(T_1, T_2\right) \in R{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} T1 is congruent to T2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T2 is congruent to {tex}T_1 \Rightarrow\left(T_2, T_1\right) \in R{/tex}.
Hence, R is symmetric.
Moreover, (T1, T2), (T2, T3) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T1 is congruent to T2 and T2 is congruent to T3
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} T1 is congruent to T3
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(T_1, T_3\right) \in R{/tex}.
Therefore, R is an equivalence relation.

1141005.explanation:

{tex}\Rightarrow \tan \theta=\left|\frac{\frac{m_2}{m_1}-1}{\frac{1}{m_1}+m_2}\right|{/tex}
 tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1{\sqrt3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o

1141008.explanation: Given, y = sin(m(sin -1 x)) ...(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right) \times \frac{m}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{m \cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow y^{\prime}=\frac{m \cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right) y^{\prime}=m \cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right){/tex} Differentiating again w.r.t. 'x', we get {tex}y^{\prime \prime}\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)+y^{\prime} \frac{(-2 x)}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}=-m^2 \sin \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right) \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y"(1 - x 2 ) - xy' = -m 2 y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y"(1 - x 2 ) - xy' + m 2 y = 0 or {tex}\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}+m^2 y=0{/tex}

Writing the above equation in standard form, we get

{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{(x-1)}{k}=\frac{y-4}{-3}=\frac{z+2}{-1} {/tex}
Now, the direction ratio of the first line is (k, -3, -1) and the direction ratio of second line is (1, k, 4)

1141060.explanation:
The function f(x) = x + 2 is one-one as for x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} Z.
Consider, f(x1) = f(x2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 + 2 = x2 + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2
Also, let y {tex}\in{/tex} codomain of f = Z such that
y = f(x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y - 2 {tex}\in{/tex} Z for all y {tex}\in{/tex} Z
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is onto.
Hence, f(x) = x + 2 is bijective.

1141064.explanation:
Given, R = {(x, y) : y is divisible by x]
and A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}
Reflexive: Let x {tex}\in{/tex} A be any arbitrary element.
We know that, x is divisible by x.
[{tex}\because{/tex} every real number except zero is divisible by itself]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, x) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Since, x {tex}\in{/tex} arbitrary element, therefore (x, x) {tex}\in{/tex} R, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} A. So, R is reflexive.
Symmetric: Clearly, 2, 4 {tex}\in{/tex} A and 4 is divisible by 2, but 2 is not divisible by 4.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (2, 4) {tex}\in{/tex} R but (4, 2) {tex}\notin {/tex} R
So, R is not symmetric.
Transitive: Let x, y, z {tex}\in{/tex} A such that (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R.
Now, as (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R, therefore y is divisible by x.
i.e. {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = k1 (say) ...(i)
where, k1 is a natural number
and as (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R, therefore z is divisible by y.
i.e. {tex}\frac{z}{y}{/tex} = K2 (say) ...(ii)
where, k2 is a natural number.
On multiplying Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{y}{x} \times \frac{z}{y}{/tex} {tex}=k_1 k_2 \Rightarrow \frac{z}{x}=k_1 k_2{/tex}
where, k1k2 is a natural number.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z is divisible by x.
Thus, (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R, for {x, y), (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R,
i.e. (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R, (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Hence, R is transitive.

1141170.explanation:
The given system can be written as AX = B, where
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 3 \end{array}\right], X=\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right] {/tex} and {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{l} 4 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Here, |A| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 15 - 14 = 1 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
Thus, A is non-singular.
Therefore, its inverse exists.
Therefore, the given system has unique solution given by
X = A-1 B
{tex}\therefore{/tex} adj (A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 3 & -2 \\ -7 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now,
A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}(\operatorname{adj} A)=\frac{1}{1}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 3 & -2 \\ -7 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 3 & -2 \\ -7 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} X = A-1 B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 3 & -2 \\ -7 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 4 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \times 4+(-2) \times 5 \\ -7 \times 4+5 \times 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{r} 2 \\ -3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{r} 2 \\ -3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Hence, x = 2 and y = -3.

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
(AB)' =B'A'

We have, f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lc} -2, & \text { if } x \leq-1 \\ 2 x, & \text { if }-1<x<\infty \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Since, f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is continuous everywhere except at x = -1. So, we have to check the continuity at x = -1 only.
At x = -1 , f(-1) = -2
LHL = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} {/tex}f(x) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} {/tex}(-2) = -2
RHL = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} {/tex}2x = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow0} {/tex} 2 (-1 + h)
[put x = -1 + h; when x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} -1+, then h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0]
= {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow0} {/tex}(-2 + 2h) = -2
Thus, f(-1) = LHL = RHL
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = -1.
Hence, f(x) is continuous for every value of x.

1141201.explanation:

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x - 1)(x - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

1141208.explanation:

At x = 0,
Value of function, f(0) = 2
And {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow0}{/tex} f(x) = 1
{tex}\because{/tex}  {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow0}{/tex} f(x) {tex}\neq{/tex} f(0)
or  {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow a}{/tex} f(x) {tex}\neq{/tex} value of function
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Function f(x) is not continuous at x = 0.

Also, the elements like 2 and 3 have no pre-image in N. Thus, f(x) is not surjective.

1141220.explanation:
Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{crr}x & \sin \theta & \cos \theta \\ -\sin \theta & -x & 1 \\ \cos \theta & 1 & x\end{array}\right|{/tex}
By expanding along first row, we get
{tex}\Delta=x\left|\begin{array}{rr} -x & 1 \\ 1 & x \end{array}\right|{/tex}{tex}-\sin \theta\left|\begin{array}{rr} -\sin \theta & 1 \\ \cos \theta & x \end{array}\right| {/tex}{tex}+\cos \theta\left|\begin{array}{rr} -\sin \theta & -x \\ \cos \theta & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
= x(-x2 - 1) - sin {tex}\theta{/tex}(-x sin{tex}\theta{/tex} - cos{tex}\theta{/tex}) + cos{tex}\theta{/tex}(-sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + x cos{tex}\theta{/tex})
= -x3 - x + x sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} - sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + x cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex}
= -x3 - x + x(sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex}) = -x3 - x + x[{tex}\because{/tex} sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1]
= -x3
Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex} is independent of {tex}\theta{/tex}.

= 6(x - 2)(x - 3) ...(ii)
Sign scheme for {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} i.e., for (x - 2)(x - 3) is

y has maximum value at x = 2

y has minimum value at x = 3

1141232.explanation:
We have, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -b \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & 4 \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 6 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} a+1 & 6 \\ 1 & 2-b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 6 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + 1 = 5, 2 - b = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 4, b = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a2 + b2 = 42 + 22 = 16 + 4 = 20

1141237.explanation:
Given, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 0 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 0 & 3 \\ x & -3 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We know that, if A is a skew-symmetric matrix, then
A = -AT ...(i)
From Eq. (i) We, get
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 0 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 0 & 3 \\ x & -3 & 0 \end{array}\right]=-\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 0 & -1 & x \\ 1 & 0 & -3 \\ -2 & 3 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 0 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 0 & 3 \\ x & -3 & 0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 0 & 1 & -x \\ -1 & 0 & 3 \\ 2 & -3 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
On comparing the corresponding element, we get 
-2 = -x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2

1141238.explanation: Clearly R is reflexive and transitive. For R to be symmetric we should add (3, 1) in R.

1141243.explanation:
Given function, f(x) = 2x3 - 3x2
and f'(x) = 6x2 - 6x
Now, for maximum or minimum of f(x),
Put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x2 - 6x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x(x - 1) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0 or x = 1
Now, f''(x) = 12x - 6
At x = 0, f''(0) = -6 < 0 (maximum)
At x = 1, f''(1) = 12 - 6 = 6 > 0 (minimum)
Hence, the minimum value of the function will be at 1.

1141253.explanation:
Given function, f(x) = x3 + 2x2 - 4x + 6
and f'(x) = 3x2 + 4x - 4
Now, for maximum or minimum of f(x), put f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 + 4x - 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(3x - 2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
Now, f''(x) = 6x + 4,
At x = -2,
f''(-2) = 6(-2) + 4 = -12 + 4
= -8 < 0 [maximum]
So, maximum value of the function f(x) exists at x = -2

1141254.explanation:
Let x1 and x2 be any two numbers in R,
where x1 < x2.
Given, f(x) = e2x
Then, we have x1 < x2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x1 < 2x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e2x1 < e2x2
[{tex}\because{/tex} x1 < x2 then ax1 < ax2, when a > 1]
f(x1) < f(x2).
Hence, f is strictly increasing on R.

1141259.explanation: For R to be reflexive, (b, b) and (c, c) should belong to R and for R to be transitive [a, c) should belong to R, as (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R. Hence, minimum number of ordered pairs to be added in R is 3.

1141263.explanation:
A - A' {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 5 \\ 6 & 7 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 5 \\ 6 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 5 \\ 6 & 7 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 6 \\ 5 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A - A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and (A - A')' {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]=-\left[\begin{array}{rr} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= -(A - A') [{tex}\because{/tex} A' = - A, so A is a skew-symmetric matrix]
Hence, (A - A') is skew-symmetric matrix

{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is strictly decreasing function in (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, 0).

1141268.explanation:
We have, f(x) = {tex}12x^{\frac{4}{3}}-6 x^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
f'(x) = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{3} x^{\frac{1}{ 3}}{/tex}{tex}-6 \times \frac{1}{3} x^{\frac{-2}{3}}{/tex}
= {tex}16x^{\frac{1}{ 3}}-\frac{2}{x^{\frac{2}{3}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2(8 x-1)}{x^{\frac{2}{3}}}{/tex}
For critical points, put f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2(8 x-1)}{x^{\frac{2}{3}}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8x - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
Also, f'(x) is not defined at x = 0, so critical points are x = 0 and x = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}and end points of interval are -1 and 1.
Now, f(-1) = {tex}12(-1)^{\frac{4}{3}}-6(-1)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
= 12 + 6 = 18
f(0) = 12(0) - 6(0) = 0
{tex}f\left(\frac{1}{8}\right){/tex} = {tex}12\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{\frac{4}{3}}-6\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
= 12{tex}\left(\frac{1}{16}\right)-6\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{-9}{4}{/tex}
and f(1) = {tex}12(1)^{\frac{4}{3}}-6(1)^{\frac{1}{ 3}}{/tex} = 6
Hence, absolute maximum value of function f is 18 at x = -1

1141270.explanation:
Given that,
x dy - y dx = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x dy = y dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
log y = log x + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = log Cx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = Cx
which is a straight line passing through the origin.

 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\cos y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -sin x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}= {tex}-\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos y}{/tex}

1141295.explanation: The differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = f{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) {/tex} or {tex} \frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = g{tex}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} is called a homogeneous differential equation.

1141299.explanation:
sin |x| = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \sin (x), & x \geq 0 \\ \sin (-x), & x<0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \sin x, & x \geq 0 \\ -\sin x, & x<0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}

1141303.explanation:
Let B {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(A-A^{\prime}\right){/tex}
{tex}B^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}\left(A-A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}\left[A^{\prime}-\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(A^{\prime}-A\right)=-\frac{1}{2}\left(A-A^{\prime}\right)=-B{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}\left(A-A^{\prime}\right){/tex} is skew-symmetric matrix of A.

1141304.explanation:
Given, {tex}2\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 4 \\ 5 & x \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & y \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 0 \\ 10 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 6 & 8 \\ 10 & 2 x \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & y \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 0 \\ 10 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 8+y \\ 10 & 2 x+1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 0 \\ 10 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
On comparing the corresponding elements, we get
8 + y = 0 and 2x + 1 = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -8 and {tex}x=\frac{5-1}{2}=2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  x - y = 2 - (-8) = 10

1141308.explanation:
Let y = cos-1(5x2 + 4).
Put z = 5x2 + 4.
Then, y = cos-1 z.
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\cos ^{ - 1} z\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d z}\left(\cos ^{ - 1} z\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} [by chain rule]
= {tex}\frac{ - 1}{\sqrt{1 - z^2}} \frac{d}{d x}\left(5 x^2 + 4\right) {/tex} [put z = 5x2 + 4]
= {tex}- \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \left(5 x^2 + 4\right)^2}} \times{/tex}[5{tex}\times{/tex}2x + 0] [put z = 5x2 + 4]
= {tex}- \frac{10 x}{\sqrt{1 - \left(5 x^2 + 4\right)^2}} {/tex}

1141313.explanation:
Given, y = sin-1{tex}\left(\frac{2 x}{1 + x^2}\right){/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{2 x}{1 + x^2}\right)^2}} \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{2 x}{1 + x^2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1 + x^2}{\sqrt{\left(1 + x^2\right)^2 - 4 x^2}}{/tex}{tex}\left[\frac{\left(1 + x^2\right)(2) - 2 x(2 x)}{\left(1 + x^2\right)^2}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1 + x^2}{\sqrt{1 + x^4 + 2 x^2 - 4 x^2}}{/tex}{tex}\left[\frac{2 - 2 x^2}{\left(1 + x^2\right)^2}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2\left(1 + x^2\right)\left(1 - x^2\right)}{\left(1 - x^2\right)\left(1 + x^2\right)^2} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{1 + x^2}{/tex}

1141314.explanation:
Given, {tex}x\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]+y\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 10 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{l} 2 x \\ 3 x \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} -y \\ y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 10 \\ 5 \end{array}\right] {/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 x-y \\ 3 x+y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 10 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - y = 10 ...(i)
and 3x + y = 5 ...(ii)
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get x = 3

1141319.explanation:
We have, f(x) = |sin x + cos x|
f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{2}\left|\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{2}\left|\sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right|{/tex}
Now, check the continuity At x = {tex}\pi{/tex},
f({tex}\pi{/tex}) = {tex}\sqrt{2}\left|\sin \left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right|=\sqrt{2}\left|-\sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=1{/tex}
LHL = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \pi^{-}} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(\pi-h){/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \sqrt{2}\left|\sin \left(\pi-h+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \sqrt{2}\left|\left(\pi-\left(h-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right)\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{2}\left|\sin \left(h-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2}\left|\sin \left(0-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{2}\left|-\sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 1
and RHL = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \pi^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(\pi+h){/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \sqrt{2}\left|\sin \left(\pi+h+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \sqrt{2}\left|-\sin \left(h+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{2}\left|\sin \left(0+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL= RHL = f({tex}\pi{/tex})
Hence, f(x) is continuous at x = {tex}\pi{/tex}

1141345.explanation: Given, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & y \\ 3 y & x \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} On multiplying the matrices of left side, we get {tex}\left[\begin{array}{r} x \cdot 1+2 \cdot y \\ 3 y \cdot 1+2 \cdot x \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{c} x+2 y \\ 3 y+2 x \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, equating the corresponding elements, we get x + 2y = 3 ...(i) and 3y + 2x = 5 ...(ii) On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get x = 1 and y = 1

1141350.explanation:
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-1} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-1} \frac{x^2-2 x-3}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-1} \frac{(x-3)(x+1)}{x+1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-1}(x-3){/tex}
= -1 - 3 = -4
For continuity at x  = -1, we must have {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow-1} f(x){/tex} = f(-1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4 = k

1141353.explanation:
Let y = log (cos ex)
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\log \left\{\cos \left(e^x\right)\right\}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{\cos \left(e^x\right)} \frac{d}{d x}\left\{\cos \left(e^x\right)\right\} {/tex} [using chain rule]
= {tex}\frac{1}{\cos \left(e^x\right)}\left\{ - \sin \left(e^x\right)\right\}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^x\right){/tex}[using chain rule]
= -tan (ex){tex}\cdot{/tex}ex = -ex tan (ex)

1141356.explanation:
Given function is
y = [x(x - 2)]2 = [x2 - 2x]2
On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2(x2 - 2x) {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex}(x2 - 2x)
= 2(x2 - 2x(2x - 2)
= 4x(x - 2)(x - 1)
On putting {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0, we get
x = 0, 1 and 2
which divides real line in disjoint intervals (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, 0), (0, 1), (1, 2) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex}).

Intervals Sign of f'(x) Nature of f(x)
(-{tex}\infty{/tex}, 0) (-)(-)(-) = -ve Strictly decreasing
(0, 1) (+)(-)(-) = +ve Strictly increasing
(1, 2) (+)(-)(+) = -ve Strictly decreasing
(2, {tex}\infty{/tex}) (+)(+)(+) = +ve Strictly increasing

Therefor, y in increasing in  (0, 1) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex}).

1141361.explanation:
At x = 0,

= {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{2 k x}{x(\sqrt{1+k x}+\sqrt{1-k x})}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2 k}{\sqrt{1+0}+\sqrt{1-0}}{/tex} = k
RHL  = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}{/tex}(2x2 + 3x - 2) = -2
f(0) = -2
{tex}\because{/tex} It is given that f(x) is continuous at x = 0.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL  = RHL = f(0) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = -2

1141377.explanation:

= {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^x\right){/tex} + {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x^2}\right){/tex} + {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x^3}\right){/tex}+ {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x^4}\right){/tex} + {tex} \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x^5}\right){/tex}
= ex + {tex}e^{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x} x^2{/tex} + {tex}e^{x^3} \frac{d}{d x} x^3 {/tex} + {tex}e^{x^4} \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^4\right){/tex} + {tex}e^{x^5} \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^5\right){/tex}[using chain rule]
= ex + {tex}e^{x^2}(2 x){/tex} + {tex}e^{x^3}\left(3 x^2\right) {/tex} + {tex}e^{x^4}\left(4 x^3\right){/tex} + {tex}e^{x^5}\left(5 x^4\right){/tex}
= ex + {tex}2 x e^{x^2}{/tex} + {tex}3 x^2 e^{x^3}{/tex} + {tex}4 x^3 e^{x^4}{/tex} + {tex}5 x^4 e^{x^5}{/tex}

1141401.explanation:
We have a function f : N {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} N,  defined as
f(1) = F(2) = 1 and f(x) = x - 1, for every x > 2
For one-one: Since, f(1) = f( 2) = 1 therefore 1 and 2 have same image, namely 1. So, f is not one-one. i.e. f is many one.
For onto: Note that y = 1 has two pre-images, namely 1 and 2. Now, let y {tex}\in{/tex} n, y {tex}\neq{/tex} 1, be any arbitrary element.
Then, y = f(x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y + 1 > 2 for every y {tex}\in{/tex} N, y {tex}\neq{/tex} 1.
Thus, for every y {tex}\in{/tex} N, y {tex}\neq{/tex} 1, there exists
x = y + 1 such that
f(x) = f(y + 1) = y + 1 - 1 = y
Hence, f is onto.

(i), we get c = 2 On solving eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get a = -3 and d = -5 from eq. (iv), we get b = 2 Now, ab - cd = (-3)2 - 2(-5) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ab - cd = -6 + 10 = 4

= 3(x - 2)2 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R
Thus, f'(x) {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R
Hence, f(x) is an increasing function on R.

1141410.explanation:
Given, f(x) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a x+3, & x \leq 2 \\ a^2 x-1, & x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex}continuity at x = 2,
LHL = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2}{/tex} (ax + 3) = 2a + 3
RHL = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2}{/tex}(a2x - 1) = 2a2 - 1
Since, f(x) is continuous for all values of x.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a + 3 = 2a2 - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a2 - 2a - 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 - a - 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 - 2a + a - 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a(a - 2) + 1(a - 2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a + 1)(a - 2) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = -1, 2

1141417.explanation:
Let x1 and x2 be any two numbers in R. Then,
x1 < x2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x1 < 9x2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x1 - 5 < 9x2 - 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x1) < f(x2)

1141420.explanation:
Here, f(x) = {tex}\begin{cases}3, & \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 4, & \text { if } 1<x<3 \\ 5, & \text { if } 3 \leq x \leq 10\end{cases}{/tex}
For 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 1, f(x) = 3; 1 < x < 3 ; f(x) = 4 and 3 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 10, f(x) = 5 are constant functions, so it is continuous in the given interval, so we have to check the continuity at x = 1, 3.
At x = 1, LHL = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}}(3){/tex} = 3,
RHL  = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}(4){/tex}= 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL {tex}\neq{/tex} RHL
Thus, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 1.
At x = 3, LHL = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{-}}(4){/tex} = 4,
RHL = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}}(5){/tex} = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL {tex}\neq{/tex} RHL.
Thus, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 3.
Hence, f (x) is continuous everywhere except at x = 1, 3.

1141425.explanation:
Given, f(x) = {tex}\frac 1x{/tex}
For one-one: Let x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} R, such that
f(x1) = f(x2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x_1}=\frac{1}{x_2}{/tex} [put x1 and x2 in f(x) = {tex}\frac 1x{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2
So, f is one-one.
For onto: Let y {tex}\in{/tex} R be any arbitrary element.
Then, y = f(x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac 1x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac 1y{/tex} [expressing x in terms of y]
It is clear that for every y {tex}\in{/tex} R (codomain), x {tex}\in{/tex} R (domain)
Thus, for each y {tex}\in{/tex} R (codomain), there exist
x = {tex}\frac 1y{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R (domain), such that 
f(x) = {tex}f\left(\frac{1}{y}\right)=\frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{y}\right)}=y{/tex}
[i.e. every element of codomain has pre-image in domain]
So, f is onto.

1141426.explanation:

{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a_1 & b_1 & 1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & 1 \\ a_1+a_2 & b_1+b_2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}

Applying R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R2 - R1 and R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R3 - R1,
we get
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a_1 & b_1 & 1 \\ a_2-a_1 & b_2-b_1 & 0 \\ a_2 & \dot{b}_2 & 0 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left|\begin{array}{cc} a_2-a_1 & b_2-b_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} [expanding along C3]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2(a2 - a1) - d2(b2 - b1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2b2 - a1b1 - a2b2 + a2b1 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a1b2 = a2b1

1153199.explanation:
Continuous but not differentiable at x = 0.
f(x) = |x| - x
|x| is a continuous function at x=0
x is a continuous function at x=0
{tex}\because{/tex} f(x) is continuous function at x=0

{tex}\ f(x) = \begin{cases} x -x & \quad \text{if } \text{x }\geq0\\ -x-x & \quad \text{if } \text{ x}<0 \end{cases} {/tex}

{tex}\ f(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & \quad \text{if } \text{x }\geq0\\ -2x & \quad \text{if } \text{ x}<0 \end{cases} {/tex}

Therefore, at x=0 f(x) is continuous. However, f(x) is not differentiable at x=0 because the left and right derivatives are not equal.

Specifically, the left derivative is -2 and the right derivative is O, so the function is not differentiable at x=0

1153201.explanation:
continuous and differentiable at x = 0.
Given f(x) = x|x|
function can be written as,
{tex} f(x) = \begin{cases} x(x) & \quad \text{if } x\geq0\\ x(-x) & \quad \text{if } x<0 \end{cases}{/tex}
{tex} f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & \quad \text{if } x\geq0\\ -x^2 & \quad \text{if } x<0 \end{cases}{/tex}
Now, 

{tex}Rf(0)=\begin{equation} \lim_{x\to 0^-} (-x^2)\end{equation}{/tex} = 0

{tex}Lf(0)=\begin{equation} \lim_{x\to 0^+} (x^2)\end{equation}{/tex} = 0

f(0) = 0

{tex}\because Rf(0) = Lf(0) = f(0){/tex}
so f(x) is continuous at x=0
Now
{tex}Rf'(0)=\begin{equation} \lim_{x\to o^-} \tfrac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0} \end{equation}{/tex}
{tex}=\begin{equation} \lim_{x\to o^-} \tfrac{(-x^2)-0}{x-0} \end{equation}=0{/tex}

{tex}Lf'(0)=\begin{equation} \lim_{x\to o^+} \tfrac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0} \end{equation}{/tex}

{tex}=\begin{equation} \lim_{x\to o^+} \tfrac{x^2-0}{x-0} \end{equation}=0{/tex}

{tex}\because Rf'(0)=Lf'(x){/tex}
so f(x) is continuous at x=0
hence f(x) is continuous and differentiable at x = 0.

1345792.explanation: we check the points where the pieces meet, specifically at {tex}x=-3{/tex} and {tex}x=3{/tex} . At {tex}x=-3{/tex} : - {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-3^{-}} f(x)=|-3|+3=6{/tex} - {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-3^{+}} f(x)=-2(-3)=6{/tex} - {tex}f(-3)=6{/tex} - Since both limits and the function value agree, {tex}f{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x={/tex} {tex}-3{/tex} . At {tex}x=3{/tex} : - {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 3^{-}} f(x)=-2(3)=-6{/tex} - {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x)=6(3)+2=20{/tex} - {tex}f(3)=6(3)+2=20{/tex} - The left and right limits do not agree, indicating a discontinuity at {tex}x={/tex} 3. The function {tex}f(x){/tex} has potential discontinuities at {tex}x=-3{/tex} and {tex}x=3{/tex} . At {tex}x={/tex} -3 , it is continuous; however, at {tex}x=3{/tex} , the left and right limits differ. Thus, there is 1 point of discontinuity. The answer is 1.

1345794.explanation: To determine the behavior of the function {tex}f(x)=x^3-3 x^2+12 x-18{/tex} , we first compute its derivative: {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-6 x+12 \text {. }{/tex} Next, we analyze the derivative to find critical points and its sign: Finding the roots of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} : {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=3\left(x^2-2 x+4\right){/tex} The discriminant of the quadratic {tex}x^2-2 x+4{/tex} is: {tex} D=(-2)^2-4 \cdot 1 \cdot 4=4-16=-12{/tex} Since the discriminant is negative, {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} has no real roots, which means it does not change sign. Analyzing the sign of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} : The quadratic x 2 - 2x + 4 opens upwards (as the coefficient of x 2 is positive) and is always positive because it has no real roots. Therefore, {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} > 0 for all x. Since {tex}f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} for all {tex}x \in R{/tex} , the function {tex}f(x){/tex} is strictly increasing on the entire real line. Thus, the correct answer is strictly increasing on R

1345796.explanation: The integral {tex} I=\int_\limits0^\frac{\pi}{2} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{1+\sin x \cos x} d x{/tex} equals zero. By symmetry, {tex}I+I^{\prime}=0{/tex} , where {tex}I^{\prime}{/tex} is the integral with sin and cos swapped. Thus, the answer is zero.

1345798.explanation: The correct answer is {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} \leq|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} . This follows from the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which states that the dot product of two vectors is less than or equal to the product of their magnitudes. Equality holds only when the vectors are parallel.

1345801.explanation: The conditional probability {tex}P(S \mid E){/tex} represents the probability of the sample space {tex}S{/tex} given event {tex}E{/tex} . Since {tex}S{/tex} is the entire sample space, it always occurs, leading to: {tex} P(S \mid E)=1{/tex} Thus, the correct answer is 1.

1345803.explanation: To find a unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} , we use the cross product: {tex} (\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times(\hat{i}-\hat{k})=\hat{j}(-1-1)=-2 \hat{j}{/tex} The unit vector is {tex} \frac{-2 \hat{j}}{2}=-\hat{j}{/tex} Thus, the correct answer is {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} .

1345804.explanation: To find {tex}k{/tex} such that {tex}[F(x)]^2=F(k x){/tex} , we compute {tex}[F(x)]^2{/tex} : {tex} [F(x)]^2=F(x) F(x)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 x & -2 \sin x \cos x & 0 \\ 2 \sin x \cos x & \cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 x & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=F(2 x){/tex} Thus, {tex}k=2{/tex} . The correct answer is 2.

1345808.explanation: To find the angle with the z-axis, we use the relation: {tex} \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \beta+\cos ^2 \gamma=1{/tex} where {tex}\alpha=30^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\beta=120^{\circ}{/tex} . Calculating, we get: {tex} \cos ^2 30^{\circ}=\frac{3}{4}, \cos ^2 120^{\circ}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Thus, {tex} \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \Longrightarrow \cos ^2 \gamma=0 \Longrightarrow \gamma=90^{\circ} .{/tex} So, the angle with the z-axis is 90 o .

1345936.explanation: The greatest integer function, denoted by [x], is discontinuous at every integer point. Therefore, f(x) = [x] is not differentiable at any integer point within the interval 0 < x < 3. There are three integer points within this interval: 1, 2, and 3. However, since the interval is strictly between 0 and 3, we only consider the points 1 and 2. Therefore, f(x) is not differentiable at 2 points.

1345939.explanation: The given integral is: {tex}\int_\limits0^{\mathrm{a}} \frac{1}{4+\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} To solve this integral, we can use the substitution x = 2 tan {tex}\theta{/tex} . Then, dx = 2 sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} . Substituting these into the integral, we get: {tex} \int_0^a \frac{1}{4+x^2} d x=\int_0^{\arctan (a / 2)} \frac{2 \sec ^2 \theta}{4+4 \tan ^2 \theta} d \theta{/tex} Simplifying the expression inside the integral: {tex} \int_0^{\arctan (a / 2)} \frac{1}{2} d \theta=\frac{1}{2}[\theta]_0^{\arctan (a / 2)}{/tex} Evaluating the integral at the limits of integration: {tex} \frac{1}{2}[\arctan (a / 2)-0]=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} Solving for a: {tex} \arctan (a / 2)=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} Taking the tangent of both sides: {tex} a / 2=\tan (\pi / 3)=\sqrt{3}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}a=2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} .

1345941.explanation: To find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , we can use the chain rule: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \cdot \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} First, let's find {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} : {tex} \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{a}{t^2}{/tex} Next, let's find {tex}\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} : {tex} \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{1}{a}{/tex} Substituting these values into the chain rule equation: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(-\frac{a}{t^2}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{a}\right){/tex} Simplifying: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{t^2}{/tex} Therefore, the correct answer is {tex} -\frac{1}{t^2}{/tex} .

1345943.explanation: The given differential equation is: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\log y}{/tex} To solve this, we can separate the variables: {tex} \log y d y=d x{/tex} Integrating both sides: {tex} \int \log y d y=\int d x{/tex} The integral on the left side can be solved by integration by parts: {tex} \int \log y d y=y \log y-\int y \cdot \frac{1}{y} d y {/tex} {tex} \int \log y d y=y \log y-\int d y {/tex} {tex} \int \log y d y=y \log y-y+C {/tex} Therefore, the solution to the differential equation is: {tex} y \log y-y=x+C{/tex}

1431312.explanation: The inequality 2x + 3y > 6 represents a half-plane where points satisfy the condition of being above the line 2x + 3y = 6. The line 2x + 3y = 6 is not included in the solution, since the inequality is strict (">" and not "geq"). To determine which half-plane, we can test a point not on the line. Testing the origin (0, 0) : Substitute x = 0 and y = 0 into the inequality: 2(0) + 3(0) = 0 Since 0 {tex}\ngtr{/tex} 6, the origin is not in the solution region. Thus, the solution is a half-plane that does not contain the origin nor the points on the line. The correct option is: half-plane that neither contains the origin nor the points of the line 2x + 3y = 6.

1472649.explanation: The differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y=1{/tex} is a first-order linear equation. Its general solution is {tex}y(x)=C e^x+e^x-1{/tex} . Given the initial condition {tex}y(0)=1{/tex} , we find {tex}C=0{/tex} . Thus, there is exactly one solution: {tex}y(x)=e^x-1{/tex} . The answer is 1 .

1481378.explanation: {tex} \left(1+y^2\right)(1+\log x) d x+x d y=0 {/tex} {tex} x d y=-\left(1+y^2\right)(1+\log x) d x {/tex} {tex} \frac{x}{1+\log x} d y=-\left(1+y^2\right) d x {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{1+y^2} d y=\frac{-(1+\log x)}{x} d x {/tex} vitegating both side {tex} \text { Mintegating both side } {/tex} {tex} \int \frac{1}{1+y^2} d y=-\int \frac{1+\log x}{x} d x {/tex} {tex} \tan ^{-1} y=-\int \frac{1+\log x}{x} d x {/tex} {tex} \text { nut } 1+\log x=t . {/tex} put {tex}1+\log x=t{/tex} . {tex} \frac{1}{x} d x=d t{/tex} {tex}\tan ^{-1} y=-\int \frac{\operatorname{tdt}}{}{/tex} {tex} \tan ^{-1} y=-\frac{t^2}{2}+c {/tex} {tex} 2 \tan ^{-1} y=-t^2+c {/tex} {tex} 2 \tan ^{-1} y=-(1+\log x)^2+c {/tex}

1481382.explanation: The number of equivalence classes for the relation R defined on the set A={x:x∈Z and 0≤x≤10} with the property that x=y is the same as the number of distinct elements in the set A. Since R is an equivalence relation, it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. Thus, each element forms an equivalence class with itself. Given that A contains all integers from 0 to 10, there are 11 distinct elements, and hence, there will be 11 equivalence classes. The equivalence classes would be: {0}, {1}, {2}, ... , {10}. The correct answer to the question is 11.

1481386.explanation: (i.) {tex}f(x, y)=\cos x-\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Replace {tex}x \rightarrow \lambda x y \rightarrow \lambda y{/tex} . {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y) =\cos \lambda x-\sin \left(\frac{\lambda y}{\lambda x}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\cos \lambda x-\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \neq \lambda^n f(\Delta x, y) {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Not Homogenous. (ii) {tex} f(x, y)=\frac{y}{x} {/tex} {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda y}{\lambda x}=\lambda^0 \frac{y}{x}=\lambda^0 f(x, y) {/tex} Hence it is homog. {tex} \text { (iii) } f(x, y)=\frac{x^2+y^2}{x y} {/tex} {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda^2 x^2+\lambda^2 y^2}{\lambda x \lambda y} {/tex} {tex} =\lambda^0 \frac{x^2+y^2}{x y}=\lambda^0 f(x, y) {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence homog. } {/tex} {tex} \text { (r) } \cos ^2\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) {/tex} {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\cos ^2\left(\frac{\lambda x}{\lambda y}\right)=\lambda^0 \cos ^2\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) {/tex} Hence homogeneous

{tex}\overrightarrow{{P^{\prime}}}=\alpha \hat{i}+\beta \hat{j}{/tex}
{tex}\theta+45^{\circ}+\delta=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \delta=45^{\circ}-30^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\delta=15^{\circ}{/tex}
Now, Area of {tex}\Delta {OP}^{\prime} {Q}{/tex}

1686033.explanation:
Given curves are {tex}y^{2}=8 x{/tex} and {tex}y=\sqrt{2} x{/tex}
Let us find the intersection point of both the curves:
{tex}(\sqrt{2} x)^{2} =8 x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 x^{2} =8 x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 x^{2}-4 x =0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x(x-4) =0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x =0,4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y =0,4 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
So intersection points are {tex}(0,0){/tex} and {tex}(4,4 \sqrt{2}){/tex}
Let {tex}P Q R{/tex} be the triangle formed by {tex}y=\sqrt{2} x{/tex}, {tex}x=1{/tex} and {tex}y=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow P=(1,2 \sqrt{2}), Q=(1, \sqrt{2}),{/tex} {tex} R=(2,2 \sqrt{2}){/tex}

{tex}\text { Area of OPSRQ } =\int_\limits{0}^{4}(\sqrt{8 x}-\sqrt{2} x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\sqrt{8}\left(\frac{2}{3} x^{3 / 2}\right)-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} x^{2}\right]_{0}^{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \sqrt{8}}{3}(4)^{3 / 2}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3}(8)-\frac{16}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Area of {tex}O P S R Q=\left(\frac{32 \sqrt{2}}{3}-8 \sqrt{2}\right){/tex} sq. units
Now, area of {tex}\triangle P Q R=\frac{1}{2} \times(P Q) \times(P R){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times(2 \sqrt{2}-\sqrt{2}) \times 1{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle P Q R=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}{/tex} sq. units
So, Required area = area of shaded region {tex}={/tex} Area of OPSRQ - Area of {tex}\triangle P Q R{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{32 \sqrt{2}}{3}-8 \sqrt{2}-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{64 \sqrt{2}-48 \sqrt{2}-3 \sqrt{2}}{6}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{13 \sqrt{2}}{6} \text { sq. units }{/tex}

1686034.explanation:
Given: {tex}\vec{a}=\alpha \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-\beta \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{c}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
The projection of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} on {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{10}{3}{/tex}
As we know, the projection of {tex}\vec{x}{/tex} on {tex}\vec{y}{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{\vec{x} \cdot \vec{y}}{|\vec{y}|}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{c}|} =\frac{10}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(\alpha \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k})}{\mid \hat{\ddot{u}}+2 \hat{\wedge}-2 \hat{\mid}} =\frac{10}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\alpha+6+2}{\sqrt{1+4+4}} =\frac{10}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha+8 =10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha =2{/tex}
Also, given that {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{c}=-6 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(3 \hat{i}-\beta \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \times(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=-6 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -\beta & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=-6 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow(2 \beta-8) \hat{i}-\hat{j}(-6-4)+\hat{k}(6 +\beta) \hat{k} {/tex} {tex} =-6 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2 \beta-8 =-6 \text { or } 6+\beta=7 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \beta =1 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha+\beta =2+1=3{/tex}

1686036.explanation:
Given differential equation is
{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y =\sqrt{y^{2}+16 x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x} =y+\sqrt{y^{2}+16 x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} =\frac{y+\sqrt{y^{2}+16 x^{2}}}{x}{/tex} ...(1)
{tex}\text { Let } y =t x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} =t+x \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} ...(2)
From equation (1) and (2), we get
{tex}t+x \frac{d t}{d x} =\frac{y+\sqrt{y^{2}+16 x^{2}}}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow t+x \frac{d t}{d x} =\frac{t x+\sqrt{t^{2} x^{2}+16 x^{2}}}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow t+x \frac{d t}{d x} =t+\sqrt{t^{2}+16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d t}{d x} =\sqrt{t^{2}+16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t^{2}+16}} =\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
Integrating both the sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t^{2}+16}}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{In}\left[t+\sqrt{t^{2}+16}\right]=\ln x+\ln c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow t+\sqrt{t^{2}+16}=x c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}+16}=x c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{\frac{y^{2}+16 x^{2}}{x}}=x c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y+\sqrt{y^{2}+16 x^{2}}=x^{2} c{/tex}
{tex}\text { Now, } y(1)=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3+\sqrt{9+16}=c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow c=8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y+\sqrt{y^{2}+16 x^{2}}=8 x^{2}{/tex}
So, at {tex}x=2{/tex},
{tex}y+\sqrt{y^{2}+64}=32{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{y^{2}+64}=(32-y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y^{2}+64=y^{2}+(32)^{2}-64 y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 64=960{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=15{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y(2)=15{/tex}

1686057.explanation:
unit vector {tex}\hat{u}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}{/tex} makes {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{3} \& \frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} with the vectors {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{k}, ~ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{j}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{k}{/tex} & {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{j}{/tex} respectively.
{tex}\therefore \frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{z}{\sqrt{2}} =|\hat{u}| \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}} \cdot \cos \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=0{/tex}
{tex}x+z =0{/tex} ...(i)
& {tex}\sqrt{\frac{y}{2}}+\frac{z}{\sqrt{2}} =|\vec{u}| \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}} \cos \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

1686151.explanation:
Given {tex}{A}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}i & -i \\ -i & i\end{array}\right] ; i=\sqrt{-1}{/tex} ...(i)
We have to find the solution of the system of linear equations
{tex}{A}^{8}\left[\begin{array}{l}x \\ y\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}8 \\ 64\end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(ii)
Now using Equation (i)
{tex} {A}^2 =\left[\begin{array}{cc} i & -i \\ -i & i \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} i & -i \\ -i & i \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {A}^2 =\left[\begin{array}{cc} i^2+i^2 & -i^2-i^2 \\ -i^2-i^2 & i^2+i^2 \end{array}\right] \left(\because i^2=-1\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A^2=\left[\begin{array}{cc} -2 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A^2=2\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A^4=4\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A^4=4\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A^4=8\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A^8=64\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A^8=64\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A^8=128\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(iii)
Now using the equation (ii) and (iii)
{tex}128\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}x \\ y\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c}8 \\ 64\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 128\left[\begin{array}{l}x-y \\ -x+y\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c}8 \\ 64\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l}x-y \\ -x+y\end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{128}\left[\begin{array}{l}8 \\ 64\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}x-y=\frac{1}{16}{/tex} ...(iv)
{tex}-x+y=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ...(v)
from equation (iv) and (v)
System of equation has no solution.

1686153.explanation:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 y \tan x=\sin x{/tex} ...(i)
Solution of this differential equation will be
{tex}y \text { (I.F) } =\int \sin x({I} . {F}) d x{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\text { I.F } =e^{\int 2 \tan x d x}{/tex}
{tex}\text { I.F } =e^{2 \int \tan x d x}{/tex}
{tex}\because \int \tan x d x =\ln (\sec x)+c{/tex}
{tex}\text { I.F. } =e^{2 \ln \sec x}{/tex}
{tex}\text { I.F } =e^{\ln ^{2} \sec ^{2} x}{/tex}
{tex}\text { I.F } =\sec ^{2} x{/tex} ...(iii)
Now using equation (ii) and (iii)
{tex}y\left(\sec ^{2} x\right)=\int(\sin x)\left(\sec ^{2} x\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(\sec ^{2} x\right)=\int\left(\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}\right)(\sec x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(\sec ^{2} x\right)=\int(\tan x \sec x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(\sec ^{2} x\right)=\sec x+{C}{/tex} ...(iv)
Given that {tex}y\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=0{/tex} i.e., when {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{3}, y=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(0)\left(\sec ^{2} \frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\sec \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+{C} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {C}=-\sec \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow C=-2{/tex} ...(v)
Using equation (iv) and (v)
{tex}y\left(\sec ^{2} x\right)=\sec x-2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{\sec x-2}{\sec ^{2} x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{\sec x}{\sec ^{2} x}-\frac{2}{\sec ^{2} x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{\sec x}-\frac{2}{\sec ^{2} x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\cos x-2 \cos ^{2} x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=-2\left\{\cos ^{2} x-\frac{1}{2} \cos x\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=-2{/tex} {tex}\left\{\cos ^{2} x-\frac{1}{2} \cos x+\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{8}-2\left(\cos x-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
For {tex}y_{\max }{/tex} put {tex}\cos x=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow y_{\max }=\frac{1}{8}{/tex}

1686162.explanation:
Given, {tex}\vec{{P}}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k} ; {P}(3,-1,2){/tex}
{tex}\text { and } \overrightarrow{{Q}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k} ; {Q}(1,2,-4){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{PR}} \| 4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{QS}} \|-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Direction ratios of normal to the plane containing {tex}{P}, {T}{/tex} and Q will proportional to
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -1 & 2 \\ -2 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{l}{0}=\frac{m}{4}=\frac{n}{2}{/tex}
For the point, T:
{tex}\overrightarrow{{PT}}=\frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y+1}{-1}=\frac{z-2}{2}=\lambda{/tex}
and {tex}\overrightarrow{{QT}}=\frac{x-1}{-2}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z+4}{-2}=\mu{/tex}
{tex}{T}:(4 \lambda+3,-\lambda-1,2 \lambda+2){/tex}
{tex}\simeq(-2 \mu+1, \mu+2,-2 \mu-4){/tex}
After comparing, we get
{tex}4 \lambda+3=-2 \mu+1 \Rightarrow 2 \lambda+\mu=-1{/tex}
{tex}-\lambda-1=\mu+2 \Rightarrow \lambda+\mu=-3{/tex}
{tex}2 \lambda+2=-2 \mu-4 \Rightarrow \lambda+\mu=-3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=2{/tex} and {tex}\mu=-5{/tex}
So, point {tex}{T}(11,-3,6){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{OT}}=11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{{TA}}=\overrightarrow{{OA}}-\overrightarrow{{OT}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{OA}}=\overrightarrow{{OT}}+\overrightarrow{{TA}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{OA}}=(11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\frac{(4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{4^{2}+2^{2}}} \times \sqrt{5}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{OA}}=(11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})+\frac{(4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{2 \sqrt{5}} \times \sqrt{5}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{OA}}=(11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})+\left(\frac{2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{5}}\right)(\sqrt{5}){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{OA}}=(11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})+(2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{OA}}=11 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+7 \hat{k} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{OA}}=\sqrt{171}{/tex}

1686201.explanation:
Given: position vectors of three point {tex}A, B, C{/tex} are
{tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\alpha \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{c}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=\vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{i}+(\alpha-4) \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
And {tex}\overrightarrow{A C} =\vec{c}-\vec{a}{/tex}
{tex}=2 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
If {tex}A, B, C{/tex} are collinear, then {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \| \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\alpha-4}{-6}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a=2{/tex} is the smallest positive integer for which {tex}A, B, C{/tex} are non-collinear.
Now, mid-point of {tex}B C=p\left(\frac{5}{2}, 0, \frac{9}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of median through {tex}A=A P{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{5}{2}-1\right)^{2}+(4)^{2}+\left(\frac{9}{2}-3\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{9}{4}+16+\frac{9}{4}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{82}}{2}{/tex}

1686205.explanation:
{tex}\left(1+e^{2 x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2\left(1+y^{2}\right) e^{x}=0{/tex} and {tex}y(0)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}} =-\frac{2 e^{x}}{1+e^{2 x}} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}} =-2 \int \frac{e^{x}}{1+e^{2 x}} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}(y) =-2 \tan ^{-1} e^{x}+C{/tex}
{tex}\because y(0) =0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \tan ^{-1}(0) =-2 \tan ^{-1}(1)+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {C} =2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} y+2 \tan ^{-1} e^{x}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{x=0}=\left(-\frac{2\left(1+y^{2}\right) e^{x}}{1+e^{2 x}}\right)_{x=0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{x=0}=-1{/tex}
Put {tex}x=\log _{e} \sqrt{3}{/tex} in equation (i), we get
{tex}\tan ^{-1} y+2 \tan ^{-1} e^{\log _{e} \sqrt{3}}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} y+2 \tan ^{-1} \sqrt{3}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} y+\frac{2 \pi}{3}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} y=-\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(\log _{e} \sqrt{3}\right)=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 6\left[y^{\prime}(0)+y\left(\log _{e} \sqrt{3}\right)^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=6\left[-1+\frac{1}{3}\right]=-4{/tex}

1686209.explanation:

where {tex}{A}+{A}^{{T}}{/tex} is symmetric and {tex}{A}-{A}^{{T}}{/tex} is skew symmetric matrix.
{tex}\Rightarrow {P}=\frac{1}{2}\left({~A}+{A}^{{T}}\right){/tex} and {tex}{Q}=\frac{1}{2}\left({~A}-{A}^{{T}}\right){/tex}

Now, {tex}{P}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{cc}4 & 3+a \\ a+3 & 0\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{det}({P})=\frac{-1}{4}(a+3)^{2}=36{/tex} or 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} So, Modulus of all possible values of {tex}\operatorname{det}({P}){/tex} {tex}=36{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow-1=a-1=-1{/tex}

Since, {tex}f(x){/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=1{/tex}
So, {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1+} f(x)=f(1){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a-1=2-b=2-b{/tex}
Put {tex}a=0{/tex}, so {tex}0-1=2-b{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-3=-b{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b=3{/tex}
So, the value of {tex}a+b=0+3=3{/tex}

1686293.explanation:
Given differential equation
{tex}\Rightarrow x \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) d y=y \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) d x-x d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)(x d y-y d x)=-x d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\left(\frac{x d y-y d x}{x^{2}}\right)=\frac{-x}{x^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\left(d\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right)=\int \frac{-1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ln |\sec (y / x)|=-\ln x+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|x \sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right|=c{/tex}
Now, apply {tex}y\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} in above
{tex}\therefore \ln \left|\frac{1}{2} \sec \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right|=c{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ln \left|\frac{1}{2} \times 2\right|=c \Rightarrow c=\ln 1=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y=x \sec ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex}
So, required bounded area in upper half,
{tex}{A}=\int_\limits{0}^{1 / \sqrt{2}} x \sec ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits{0}^{1 / \sqrt{2}} x \cos ^{-1}(x) d x{/tex}
Using integration by parts
{tex}=\left|\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2} \cos ^{-1} x\right)\right|_{0}^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}+\int_{0}^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} \frac{x^{2}}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}-0\right)+\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} \frac{1-\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{16}+\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)_{0}^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}-\int_{0}^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} \sqrt{1-x^{2}} d x\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{16}+\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{4}-\left.\left\{\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} x\right\}\right|_{0} ^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{16}+\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{4}-\left\{\frac{1}{4}+\frac{\pi}{8}\right\}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\text { Area }=\frac{\pi-1}{8}{/tex}

1686297.explanation:
Given {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 / \sqrt{5} & 2 / \sqrt{5} \\ -2 / \sqrt{5} & 1 / \sqrt{5}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}{AA}^{{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 / \sqrt{5} & 2 / \sqrt{5} \\ -2 / \sqrt{5} & 1 / \sqrt{5}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 / \sqrt{5} & -2 / \sqrt{5} \\ 2 / \sqrt{5} & 1 / \sqrt{5}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]={I}{/tex} ...(i)
Given {tex}{Q}={A}^{{T}} {BA}{/tex}
So, {tex}Q^{2}=\left(A^{T} B A\right)\left(A^{T} B A\right)=A^{T} B^{2} A{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {Q}^{3}={A}^{{T}} {B}^{3} {~A}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {Q}^{2021}={A}^{{T}} {B}^{2021} {~A}{/tex}
Now, let {tex}{P}={AQ}^{2021} {~A}^{{T}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {P}={A}\left({A}^{{T}} {B}^{2021} {~A}\right) {A}^{{T}}{/tex}
Since {tex}A A^{T}=I{/tex} from (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow {P}={B}^{2021}{/tex}
Now, {tex}{B}^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ i & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ i & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 2 i & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}{B}^{3}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ 2 i & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ i & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ 3 i & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, {tex}B^{2021}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 2021 i & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Inverse of {tex}{P}=\left({P}^{-1}\right)=\left({B}^{2021}\right)^{-1}=\left(\begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ -2021 i & 1\end{array}\right){/tex}

1686303.explanation:
Given, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-(\vec{c} \times \vec{a})=\vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{c} \times \vec{a}=-\vec{b}{/tex}
Here, {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}){/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}\vec{a} & \vec{b} & \vec{c}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}\vec{b} & \vec{c} & \vec{a}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{c} \times \vec{a}){/tex}
{tex}=\vec{b} \cdot(-\vec{b}){/tex}
{tex}=-|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex}
{tex} =-\left(\sqrt{(1)^2+(-1)^2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex}[ \because|\vec{b}| =\sqrt{(1)^2+(-1)^2} {/tex}{tex} =\sqrt{2} ] {/tex}
{tex}=-(\sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=-2{/tex}

1686382.explanation:
{tex}(y+1) \tan ^{2} x d x+\tan x d y+y d x=0{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+(1+y) \tan x=-y \cot x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+y(\tan x+\cot x)=-\tan x{/tex}
{tex}{IF}=e^{\int(\tan x+\cot x) d x}=e^{\int \frac{\tan ^{2} x+1}{\tan x} d x}=\tan x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y \tan x=\int-\tan ^{2} x d x+{C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \tan x=\int\left(1-\sec ^{2} x\right) d x+{C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \tan x=x-\tan x+{C}{/tex}
Now {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} x y=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}\left(\frac{x}{\tan x}\right)(x-\tan x+{C})=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 1(0-0+{C})=1 \Rightarrow {C}=1{/tex}
Then, the function {tex}y \tan x=x-\tan x+1{/tex} at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}-\tan \frac{\pi}{4}+1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

1686551.explanation:

    {tex}R=\{(A, B): A \cap B \neq \phi, A, B, \in M\}{/tex} [Given]
    If {tex}A=B=\phi \Rightarrow A \cap B=\phi{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}R{/tex} is not reflexive.
  2. As, if {tex}(A, B) 6 M \Rightarrow(B, A) \in M{/tex}
    So, {tex}R{/tex} is symmetric
  3. Let {tex}(A, B) \in M{/tex} and {tex}(B, C) \in M{/tex}

    But {tex}A \cap C{/tex} not necessarily empty
    So, {tex}(A, C) \notin M{/tex}
    So, R is not transitive.

=

1686578.explanation:
We have, {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}+a x=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=-a x{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \ln x=-a t+{C}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x={Ce}^{-a t}{/tex}
As, {tex}x(0)=2{/tex}
So, {tex}2={Ce}{ }^{0} \Rightarrow {C}=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=2 e^{-a t}{/tex}
Also, {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}+b y=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=-b y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=-b \int d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ln y=-b+{C}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y={K} e^{-{bt}}{/tex}
As, {tex}y(0)=1{/tex}
So, {tex}1={K} e^{0} \Rightarrow k=1 \Rightarrow y=e^{-b t}{/tex}
Given, {tex}3 y(1)=2 x(1){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 e^{-b}=2\left(2 e^{-a}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{a-b}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
If {tex}x(t)=y(t){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 e^{-a t}=e^{-b t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \frac{e^{-a t}}{e^{-b t}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 e^{-(a-b) t}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{-t}=1 \Rightarrow\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{t}=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow t=\log _{\frac{4}{3}} 2{/tex}

1686601.explanation:
{tex}y^{2} d x+\left(x-\frac{1}{y}\right) d y=0{/tex}
{tex}y^{2} \frac{d x}{d y}+x=\frac{1}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\left(\frac{1}{y^{2}}\right) x=\frac{1}{y^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}P=\frac{1}{y^{2}}, Q=\frac{1}{y^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\text { I.F }=e^{\int P d y}=e^{\int \frac{1}{y^{2}} d y}=e^{-\frac{1}{y}}{/tex}
Solution of DE
{tex}x({I} \cdot {F})=\int Q \cdot({IF}) d y+c{/tex}
{tex}x e^{-1 / y}=\int \frac{1}{y^{3}} e^{-1 / y} d y+C{/tex}
{tex}e^{-1 / y}=t \Rightarrow-1 / y=\ln t{/tex}
{tex}e^{-1 / y} \frac{1}{y^{2}} d y=d t{/tex}
{tex}x e^{-1 / y}=\int-\ln t d t+C{/tex}
{tex}x e^{-1 / y}=-t(\ln t-1)+C{/tex}
{tex}x e^{-1 / y}=-e^{-1 / y}\left(\frac{-1}{y}-1\right)+C{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{1}{y}+1+C e^{1 / y}{/tex}
{tex}x(1)=1=1+1+C e {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C=\frac{-1}{e}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=2+1-\left(\frac{1}{e}\right) \cdot e^{2}=3-e{/tex}

For {tex}\left(\frac{14}{3},-3, \frac{22}{3}\right){/tex} satisfies the equation.

1686623.explanation:
{tex}x^{3} d y+(x y-1) d x=0{/tex}
Also, {tex}y\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=3-e \& x \gt 0{/tex}
Now, {tex}x^{3} \frac{d y}{d x}+x y-1=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{3} \frac{d y}{d x}=1-x y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{3} \frac{d y}{d x}+x y=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) y=\frac{1}{x^{3}}{/tex}
This is a linear differential equation
I.F. {tex}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x}=e^{\frac{-1}{x}}{/tex}
So, differential equation becomes,
{tex}y e^{\frac{-1}{x}}=\int e^{\frac{-1}{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{x^{3}} d x{/tex}
Put {tex}\frac{-1}{x}=t \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x=d t{/tex}
R.H.S. = 
Integrating by parts
R.H.S. {tex}=-\left[t e^{t}-\int e^{t} d t\right]=-t e^{t}+e^{t}+c{/tex}
So, solution of differential equation is
{tex}y e^{\frac{-1}{x}}=-e^{\frac{-1}{x}}\left(\frac{-1}{x}-1\right)+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\left(\frac{1}{x}+1\right)+c e^{\frac{1}{x}}{/tex}
At {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}, y=3-e{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3-e=\frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}+1+c e^{\frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3-e=(2+1)+c e^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow c e^{2}=-e{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac{-1}{e}{/tex}
So, {tex}y=\left(\frac{1}{x}+1\right)+\left(-\frac{1}{e}\right) e^{\frac{1}{x}}{/tex}
Now, at {tex}x=1, y=\left(\frac{1}{1}+1\right)+\left(\frac{-1}{e}\right) e^{\frac{1}{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=2-1=1{/tex}

1686631.explanation:
{tex}f(x+y)=f(x) f(y) \forall x, y \in {R}{/tex}
From the functional equation {tex}f(x+y)=f(x) f(y){/tex}:
Put {tex}y=0, f(x)=f(x)=f(0){/tex}
This means either {tex}f^{\prime}(0)=1{/tex} or {tex}f(x)=0{/tex} for all {tex}x{/tex}
Since we have {tex}f^{\prime}(0)=4 a \gt 0, f(x){/tex} cannot be identiacially zero. Therefore {tex}f(0)=1{/tex}
This implies that {tex}f{/tex} is an exponential function.
{tex}f(x)=k^{x}{/tex} for some constant {tex}k{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime}(x) =k^{x} \ln k{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime}(0) =\ln k=4 a{/tex}
{tex}k =e^{4 a}{/tex}
{tex}f(x) =\left(e^{4 a}\right)^{x}=e^{4 a x}{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime}(x) =4 a e^{4 a x}{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x) =16 a^{2} e^{4 a x}{/tex}
Now, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)-3 a f^{\prime}(x)-f(x)=0{/tex}
{tex}16 a^{2} e^{4 a x}-12 a^{2} e^{4 a x}-e^{4 a x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\left(4 a^{2}-1\right) e^{4 a x}=0{/tex}
{tex}4 a^{2}=1{/tex}

1686849.explanation:
{tex}{R}=\{(f, g): f(0)=g(1){/tex} and {tex}f(1)=g(0)\}{/tex}
Reflexive: {tex}(f, f) \in {R}{/tex}
{tex}=f(0)=f(1){/tex} and {tex}f(1)=f(0) \rightarrow{/tex} must hold
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} but this is not true for all function.
So, it is not reflexive.
Symmetric: If {tex}(f, g) \in R \Rightarrow(g, f) \in R{/tex}
Now, {tex}g(0)=f(1){/tex} and {tex}g(1)=f(0) \rightarrow{/tex} true
{tex}\therefore{/tex} symmetric
Transitive: If {tex}(f, g) \in {R}{/tex} and {tex}(g, h) \in {R}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(f, h) \in {R}{/tex}
Now, {tex}(f, g) \in {R} \Rightarrow f(0)=g(1){/tex} and {tex}f(1)=g(0){/tex}
{tex}(g, h) \in {R} \Rightarrow g(0)=h(1){/tex} and {tex}g(1)=h(0){/tex}
For {tex}(f, h) \in {R}{/tex}, we need {tex}f(0)=h(1){/tex} and {tex}f(1)=h(0){/tex}
Now, {tex}f(0)=g(1)=h(0){/tex} and {tex}f(1)=g(0)=h(1){/tex}
Hence, it is not transitive.
It is symmetric but neither reflective nor transitive.

1686854.explanation:
Let {tex}\vec{a} =a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+a_{3}^{2} =1{/tex}
{tex}\text { Let } \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=2 \vec{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \vec{c}=\vec{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{d} =\hat{k}{/tex}
Given projections are equal therefore,
{tex}\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|b|} =\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}}{|c|}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{d}}{|d|}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2 a_{1}-a_{2}+2 a_{3}}{3} =\frac{a_{1}+2 a_{2}-2 a_{3}}{3}=a_{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 2 a_{1}-a_{2}+2 a_{3} =a_{1}+2 a_{2}-2 a_{3}{/tex}
{tex}a_{1}-3 a_{2}+4 a_{3} =0{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}2 a_{1}-a_{2}+2 a_{3} =3 a_{3}{/tex}
{tex}2 a_{1}-a_{2}-a_{3} =0{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}a_{1}+2 a_{2}-2 a_{3} =3 a_{3}{/tex}
{tex}a_{1}+2 a_{2}-5 a_{3} =0{/tex} ...(iii)
From Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}a_{2}+a_{3}=2 t \left\{\text { Let }, a_{1}=t\right\}{/tex}
From Eq. (i), we get
{tex}3 a_{2}-4 a_{3} =t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 3 a_{2}-4\left(2 t-a_{2}\right){/tex} = t
{tex}3 a_{2}+4 a_{2}-8 t =t{/tex}
{tex}7 a_{2} =9 t{/tex}
{tex}a_{2} =\frac{9 t}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Now, } a_{2}+a_{3} =2 t{/tex}
{tex}\frac{9 t}{7}+a_{3} =2 t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a_{3} =\frac{5 t}{7}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}a_{1}: a_{2}: a_{3}=t: \frac{9 t}{7}: \frac{5 t}{7}{/tex}
{tex}=7: 9: 5{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Unit vector along {tex}\vec{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{155}}(7 \hat{i}+9 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}){/tex}

Applying {tex}{R}_{2} \rightarrow {R}_{2}-{R}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{3} \rightarrow {R}_{3}-2 {R}_{1}{/tex}

{tex}[{A}: {B}] \sim\left[\begin{array}{ccccc}-1 & 2 & -9 & : & 7 \\ 0 & 1 & 16 & : & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 71 & : & 0\end{array}\right]{/tex}

{tex}2 x-2 y+z-11=0{/tex} is {tex}\frac{|-6-4-11|}{\sqrt{4+4+1}}=\frac{21}{3}=7{/tex}

1686889.explanation:
{tex} (\sin x \cos y)(f(2 x+2 y))-f(2 x-2 y){/tex} {tex}=(\cos x \sin y) {/tex} {tex} (f(2 x+2 y)+f(2 x-2 y)) {/tex}
{tex} f(2 x+2 y) \cdot(\sin x \cos y-\cos x \sin y) {/tex} {tex} =f(2 x-2 y) \cdot(\sin x \cos y+\cos x \sin y) {/tex}
{tex} f(2 x+2 y) \cdot \sin (x-y) {/tex} {tex}=f(2 x-2 y) \cdot \sin (x+y) {/tex}
{tex} \frac{f(2 x+2 y)}{\sin (x+y)} =\frac{f(2 x-2 y)}{\sin (x-y)} {/tex}
Put {tex} 2 x+2 y=m, 2 x-2 y=n {/tex}
{tex} \frac{f(m)}{\sin \left(\frac{m}{2}\right)}=\frac{f(n)}{\sin \left(\frac{n}{2}\right)}={K} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \frac{f(m)}{\sin \left(\frac{m}{2}\right)} ={K} {/tex}
{tex} f(m) ={K} \sin \left(\frac{m}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} f(x) ={K} \sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} f^{\prime}(x) =\frac{{K}}{2} \cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) {/tex}
Put {tex} x =0 ; \frac{1}{2}=\frac{{K}}{2} \Rightarrow {~K}=1 {/tex}
{tex} f^{\prime}(x) =\frac{1}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2} {/tex}
{tex} f^{\prime}(x) =-\frac{1}{4} \sin \frac{x}{2} {/tex}
{tex} 24 f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{5 \pi}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex}=\left(-\frac{1}{4} \sin \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}\right)\right) 24 {/tex}
{tex} =-\frac{24}{4} \sin \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =-6 \sin \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =-6 \sin \frac{\pi}{6}=-6 \times \frac{1}{2}=-3 {/tex}

1686912.explanation:
Given that {tex}f(x)=\max \{1+x+[x], 2+x, x+2[x]\}{/tex}
Case I: If {tex}x \in[0,1){/tex}, then {tex}f(x)=\max \{1+x+0,2+x{/tex}, {tex}x+2 \times 0\}{/tex}
{tex}=\max \{1+x, 2+x, x\}=2+x{/tex}
Case II: If {tex}x \in[1,2){/tex}, then {tex}f(x)=\max \{1+x+1,2+{/tex} {tex}x, x+2 \times 1\}{/tex}
{tex}=\max \{2+x, 2+x, 2+x\}=2+x{/tex}
Case III: if {tex}x=2{/tex}, then {tex}f(x)=\max \{3+x, 2+x, x+4\}{/tex}
{tex}=\max \{5,4,6\}=6{/tex}
So, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}2+x, & 0 \leq x \lt 2 \\ 6, & x=2\end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x){/tex} is discontinuous at {tex}x=2{/tex} and differentiable in {tex}(0,2){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow m=1{/tex} and {tex}n=0{/tex}
Hence, {tex}(m+n)^{2}+2=(1+0)^{2}+2=3{/tex}

1686919.explanation:
Given that lines {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{k}){/tex} ...(1)
and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j})+\mu(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} ...(2)
Now, direction of line of the shortest distance of line (1) and (2) is given by
{tex}\vec{n}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right|=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
So, eqn. of line passing through {tex}(-1,2,3){/tex} whose d.r's are ({tex}1,-1,-2{/tex}) is given by
{tex}\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y-2}{-1}=\frac{z-3}{-2}=\lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(x, y, z) \equiv(\lambda-1,-\lambda+2,-2 \lambda+3){/tex}
If this point lies on plane {tex}\vec{r},(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=10{/tex}
Then, {tex}\lambda-1-2(-\lambda+2)+3(-2 \lambda+3)=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda-1+2 \lambda-4-6 \lambda+9=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \lambda=-6 \Rightarrow \lambda=-2{/tex}
So, point on plane is {tex}(-3,4,7){/tex} and required distance is {tex}\sqrt{(-1+3)^{2}+(2-4)^{2}+(3-7)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{4+4+16}=2 \sqrt{6}{/tex}

Given that {tex}5 f(x+y)=f(x) . f(y){/tex} and {tex}f(3)=320{/tex}
Put {tex}x=3{/tex} and {tex}y=0{/tex}, we get
{tex}5 f(3)=f(3) f(0) \Rightarrow f(0)=5{/tex}
Put {tex}x=1{/tex} and {tex}y=1{/tex}, we get
{tex}5 f(2)=f(1) f(1)=f^{2}(1){/tex} ...(i)
Put {tex}x=2{/tex} and {tex}y=1{/tex}, we get
{tex}5 f(3)=f(2) f(1){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5 \times 320=\frac{f^{3}(1)}{5}{/tex} [from (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{3}(1)=25 \times 320=125 \times 64{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(1)=5 \times 4=20{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(2)=80{/tex} [From (i)]
Put {tex}x=2{/tex} and {tex}y=2{/tex}, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow 5 f(4)=f(2) f(2) \Rightarrow 5 f(4)=80 \times 80{/tex}
{tex}f(4)=1280{/tex}
Put {tex}x=3{/tex} and {tex}y=2{/tex}, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow 5 f(5)=f(3) f(2){/tex}
{tex}f(5)=\frac{320 \times 80}{5}=5120{/tex}
{tex}\sum_{n=0}^{5} f(x)=f(0)+f(1)+f(2)+f(3)+f(4)+f(5){/tex}
{tex}=5+20+80+320+1280+5120{/tex}
{tex}=5\left(1+2^{2}+2^{4}+2^{6}+2^{8}+2^{10}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5\left(\left(2^{2}\right)^{6}-1\right)}{2^{2}-1}=\frac{5\left(2^{12}-1\right)}{3}=6825{/tex}

1686945.explanation:
{tex}A^{2}=I \Rightarrow|A|^{2}=I \Rightarrow|A|= \pm 1=b{/tex}
Let {tex}{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}\alpha & \beta \\ \gamma & \delta\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}\alpha & \beta \\ \gamma & \delta\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}\alpha & \beta \\ \gamma & \delta\end{array}\right]=I{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}\alpha^{2}+\beta \gamma & \alpha \beta+\beta \delta \\ \alpha \gamma+\gamma \delta & \gamma \beta+\delta^{2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\alpha \beta+\beta \delta=0 \Rightarrow(\alpha+\delta) \beta{/tex} {tex}=0 \Rightarrow \alpha+\delta=0=a{/tex}
{tex}(\alpha+\delta) \gamma=0 \Rightarrow \beta \gamma+\delta^{2}=0{/tex}
Now {tex}3 a^{2}+4 b^{2}=3(0)^{2}+4(1)=4{/tex}

1686946.explanation:
Given that
{tex}f(x)=\left[x^{2}-x\right]+|-x+[x]|{/tex}
Hence, we have to check the continuity of {tex}f(x){/tex} at 0 and 1.
Now to check continuity of {tex}f(x){/tex} at {tex}x=0{/tex}
{tex}f(x)=[x(x-1)]+|-x+[x]|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f\left(0^{-}\right)=\operatorname{lt}_{h \rightarrow 0}[-h(-h-1)]+|+h+[-h]|{/tex}
{tex}=0+|0-1|=1{/tex} and {tex}f(0)=0{/tex}
since {tex}f\left(0^{-}\right) \neq f(0){/tex}, therefore {tex}f(x){/tex} is not continuous at {tex}x=0{/tex}
Similarly to check at {tex}x=1{/tex}
{tex}f\left(1^{-}\right)=\operatorname{lt}_{h \rightarrow 0}[(1-h)(-h)]+|-1+h+[1-h]|=-1+|-1|=0{/tex}
{tex}f\left(1^{+}\right)=\operatorname{lt}_{h \rightarrow 0}[(1+h) h]+|-1-h+[1+h]|=0+0=0{/tex}
and {tex}f(1)=0{/tex}
Since {tex}f\left(1^{-}\right)=f\left(1^{+}\right)=f(1){/tex}, therefore {tex}f(x){/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=1{/tex}

1686950.explanation:
Given {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=g^{\prime \prime}(x)+6 x{/tex} ...(i)
And {tex}f^{\prime}(1)=4 g^{\prime}(1)-3=9{/tex}
And {tex}f(2)=3 g(2)=12{/tex}
Integrate equation (i) w.r.t. {tex}x{/tex}, we get
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=g^{\prime}(x)+3 x^{2}+{C}_{1}{/tex} ...(ii)
Put {tex}x=1{/tex} in equation (ii), we get
{tex}f(1)=g^{\prime}(1)+3+{C}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 9=3+3+{C}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {C}_{1}=3{/tex}
Integrate equation (ii) w.r.t. {tex}x{/tex}, we get
{tex}f(x)=g(x)+x^{3}+3 x+{C}_{2}{/tex}
Put {tex}x=2{/tex} in above equation, we get
{tex}f(2)=g(2)+8+6+{C}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {C}_{2}=-6{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f(x)-g(x)=x^{3}+3 x-6{/tex}
Let {tex}{P}(x)=f(x)-g(x)=x^{3}+3 x-6{/tex}
Now, {tex}{P}(1)=-2{/tex} and {tex}{P}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)=\frac{27}{8}+\frac{9}{2}-6=\frac{15}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\because {P}(1) {P}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} At least one root of {tex}{P}(x)=0{/tex} lies in the interval {tex}x \in\left(1, \frac{3}{2}\right){/tex}.
So, there exists {tex}x_{0} \in\left(1, \frac{3}{2}\right){/tex} such that {tex}f\left(x_{0}\right)=g\left(x_{0}\right){/tex}
Now, {tex}\left|f^{\prime}(x)-g^{\prime}(x)\right| \lt 6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 x^{2}+3 \lt 6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}+1 \lt 2 \Rightarrow x^{2}-1 \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(x-1)(x+1) \lt 0 \Rightarrow x \in(-1,1){/tex}
Now, {tex}{P}(-1)=-1-3-6=-10{/tex}
{tex}{P}(2)=(2)^{3}+3(2)-6=8{/tex}
So, {tex}|{P}(x)|=|f(x)-g(x)| \lt 10{/tex}
Now, {tex}{P}(-2)=f(-2)-g(-2){/tex} {tex}=(-2)^{3}+3(-2)-6=-20{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow g(-2)-f(-2)=20{/tex}

{tex}{R}_{2} \rightarrow {R}_{2}-2 {R}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{3} \rightarrow {R}_{3}-{R}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow[A: B] \sim\left[\begin{array}{ccccc}1 & 2 & 3 & : & 4 \\ 0 & -9 & -4 & : & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 a-6 & : & b-8\end{array}\right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow a \neq 3{/tex} and {tex}b \in {R}{/tex}
and for infinite solutions {tex}2 a-6=0{/tex} and {tex}b-8=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a=3{/tex} and {tex}b=8{/tex}

1686956.explanation:
{tex}\because {A}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} m & n \\ p & q \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow|{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{ll} m & n \\ p & q \end{array}\right|=m q-n p{/tex}
{tex}\therefore d=m q-n p{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\text { Now, } {A}-d(\operatorname{Adj} {~A})=\left[\begin{array}{cc} m & n \\ p & q \end{array}\right]-d\left[\begin{array}{cc} q & -n \\ -p & m \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} m-d q & n+d n \\ p+p d & q-d m \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|{A}-d(\operatorname{Adj} {~A})|=\left|\begin{array}{cc} m-d q & n(1+d) \\ p(1+d) & q-d m \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(m-d q)(q-d m)-n p(1+d)^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow m q-d m^{2}-d q^{2}+d^{2} q m=n p(1+d)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow m q-d m^{2}-d q^{2}+d^{2} q m=(m q-d)(1+d)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow m q-d m^{2}-d q^{2}+d^{2} q m=m q+2 m q d{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow d(1+d)^{2}=d m^{2}+d q^{2}+2 m q d{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow d(1+d)^{2}=d\left(m^{2}+q^{2}+2 m q\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(1+d)^{2}=(m+q)^{2}{/tex}

1686957.explanation:
Let {tex}\vec{n}{/tex} be the normal vector to the plane {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}, \hat{i}+\hat{k}{/tex}
then {tex}\vec{n}_{1}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right|=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}
and {tex}\vec{n}_{2}{/tex} be the normal vector to plane {tex}\hat{i}-\hat{j}, \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} then
{tex}\vec{n}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & -1\end{array}\right|=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
So {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} can be taken as {tex}\vec{a}=\lambda\left|\vec{n}_{1} \times \vec{n}_{2}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a}=\lambda\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|=\lambda(-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
Also given that {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=6{/tex}, where {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda(4+2)=6 \Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex}
So {tex}\vec{a}=-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} then
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{6}{\sqrt{4+4} \times \sqrt{4+4+1}}=\frac{6}{2 \sqrt{2} \times 3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\cos \frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
and {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & -2 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 & 1\end{array}\right\|=|2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}|=6{/tex}
Hence, required pair is {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, 6\right){/tex}

1686960.explanation:
Given that {tex}{A}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 & 3 \\ \alpha & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right],|{A}|=2{/tex}
Now, adj (2A) {tex}=2^{2} \operatorname{adj}({~A}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2{/tex} adj {tex}(2 {~A})=8{/tex} adj {tex}({A}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{adj}(2 \operatorname{adj}(2 {~A}))=\operatorname{adj}(8 \operatorname{adj}({~A})){/tex}
{tex}=8^{2} \operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}({~A})){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|2 \operatorname{adj}(2 \operatorname{adj}(2 {~A}))|=|2 \times 64 \operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}({~A}))|{/tex}
{tex}=(128)^{3}|\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}({~A}))|{/tex}
{tex}=\left(2^{7}\right)^{3}|{~A}|^{(3-1) 2}{/tex}
{tex}=2^{21} \times|{~A}|^{4}=2^{21} \times 2^{4}=2^{25}{/tex}
So {tex}|2 \operatorname{adj}(2 \operatorname{adj}(2 {~A}))|=32^{n}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2^{25}=2^{5 n}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5 n=25 \Rightarrow n=5{/tex}
Also, {tex}|{A}|=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 & 3 \\ \alpha & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right|=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5-2(2 \alpha-1)+3(\alpha-3)=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5-4 \alpha+2+3 \alpha-9=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=-4{/tex}
Hence, {tex}3 n+\alpha=3 \times 5-4=11{/tex}

1686967.explanation:
Given differential equation is
{tex}x \log _{{e}} x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x^{2} \log _{{e}} x ; x \gt 1{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x \log _{e} x} y=x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation
Now, I.F. {tex}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x \log _{e} x} d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I.F. {tex}=e^{\log _{e}\left(\log _{e} x\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I.F. {tex}=\log _{{e}} x{/tex}
So, solution of given differential equation is
{tex}y\left(\log _{e} x\right)=\int x\left(\log _{e} x\right) d x+{C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(\log _{e} x\right)=\log _{e} x\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right) d x{/tex} + C
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(\log _{e} x\right)=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \log _{e} x-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right)+{C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(\log _{e} x\right)=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \log _{e} x-\frac{1}{4} x^{2}+{C}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} At {tex}x=2, y=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2\left(\log _{e} 2\right)=2 \log _{e} 2-1+{C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {C}=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y\left(\log _{e} x\right)=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \log _{e} x-\frac{1}{4} x^{2}+1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{\log _{e} x}\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2} \log _{e} x-\frac{1}{4} x^{2}+1\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y(e)=\frac{e^{2}}{2}-\frac{1}{4} e^{2}+1 \Rightarrow y(e)=\frac{4+e^{2}}{4}{/tex}

1686968.explanation:
Given that {tex}a \in {R}{/tex} and {tex}x|x-1|+|x+2|+a=0{/tex}
Let {tex}y=x|x-1|+|x+2|{/tex} and {tex}y=-a{/tex}
So {tex}y=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}-x(x-1)-(x+2), & x \lt -2 \\ -x(x-1)+(x+2), & -2 \leq x \lt 1 \\ x(x-1)+(x+2), & x \geq 1\end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -x^{2}-2, & x \lt -2 \\ -x^{2}+2 x+2, & -2 \leq x \lt 1 \\ x^{2}+2, & x \geq 1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}f\left(-2^{-}\right)=-6, f\left(-2^{+}\right)=-6, f(-2)=-6 \Rightarrow{/tex} continuous at -2 and {tex}f\left(1^{-}\right)=3, f\left(1^{+}\right)=3, f(1)=3 \Rightarrow{/tex} continuous at 1
Also {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}-2 x, & x \lt -2 \\ -2 x+2, & -2 \lt x \lt 1 \\ 2 x, & x \lt 1\end{array} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \gt 0 \forall x \in {R}\right.{/tex}
So {tex}f(x){/tex} is continous and strictly increasing {tex}\forall x \in {R}{/tex} and {tex}y=-a{/tex} is a st. line || to {tex}x{/tex}-axis
Hence, {tex}x|x-1|+|x+2|+a=0{/tex} has exactly one real solution {tex}\forall a \in {R}{/tex}

We get {tex}x=-2{/tex} and {tex}y=3{/tex}
