1686987.explanation:
{tex} \left\{(x, y): x^2+(y-2)^2 \leq 4, x^2 \geq 2 y\right\} {/tex}
Here, {tex}\left.x^2+y-2\right)^2 \leq 4{/tex} represents the interior part of the circle and {tex}x^2 \geq 2 y{/tex} represents the exterior part of the parabola.
Which can be drawn as-
On solving {tex}x^2+(y-2)^2=4{/tex}
and {tex}x^2=2 y{/tex}, we get,
{tex}2 y+y^2+4-4 y=4{/tex}
{tex}y^2-2 y=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=0,2{/tex} and {tex}x=0, \pm 2{/tex}

Hence, required area
{tex}=-2{/tex} [Area of square OABC - Area of sector of circle{tex} \left.-\int\limits_0^2 y \text { parabola }\right] {/tex}

1686989.explanation:
Since {tex}{P}(h, k){/tex} lies on {tex}x=4 y^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore h=4 k^{2}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}{PQ}=\sqrt{(h-0)^{2}+(k-33)^{2}}=\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}-66 k+(33)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}{PQ}^{2}=16 k^{4}+k^{2}-66 k+1089{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d\left({PQ}^{2}\right)}{d k}=64 k^{3}+2 k-66=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(k-1)\left(64 k^{2}+64 k+66\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow k-1=0 \Rightarrow k=1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2}\left({PQ}^{2}\right)}{d k^{2}}=192 k^{2}+2{/tex}
So, {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} P Q^{2}}{d k^{2}}\right)_{k=1}=192+2=194 \gt 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Distance is minimum when {tex}k=1{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore h=4 k^{2}=4{/tex}
So, {tex}{P}(4,1){/tex}
Given parabola is {tex}y^{2}=4(x+y){/tex}
{tex}y^{2}-4 y=4 x \Rightarrow(y-2)^{4}=4(x+1){/tex}
Directrix is {tex}x+1=-1 \Rightarrow x=-2{/tex}
Distance of {tex}{P}(4,1){/tex} from the direction {tex}x=-2{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4+2}{\sqrt{1^{2}}}=6{/tex}

1686995.explanation:
Let {tex}{I}=\int\limits_{\frac{1}{2}}^{2} \frac{\tan ^{-1} x}{x} d x{/tex} ...(1)
Put {tex}\frac{1}{x}=\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{\alpha} \Rightarrow d x=\frac{-1}{\alpha^{2}} d \alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {I}=\int\limits_{2}^{\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right)} \frac{\left(\frac{-1}{\alpha^{2}}\right) d \alpha}{\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {I}=\int\limits_{2}^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{-\cot ^{-1} \alpha}{\alpha} d \alpha \left\{\because \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{x}=\cot ^{-1} x\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {I}=\int\limits_{\frac{1}{2}}^{2} \frac{\cot ^{-1} \alpha}{\alpha} d \alpha{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {I}=\int\limits_{\frac{1}{2}}^2 \frac{\cot ^{-1} x}{x} d x {/tex} ...(2)
Adding eq. (1) and (2), we get
{tex}2 {I}=\int\limits_{\frac{1}{2}}^{2} \frac{\tan ^{-1} x+\cot ^{-1} x}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 {I}=\int\limits_{\frac{1}{2}}^{2} \frac{\frac{\pi}{2}}{x} d x \left\{\because \tan ^{-1} x+\cot ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 {I}=\frac{\pi}{2}[\ln x]_{\frac{1}{2}}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 {I}=\frac{\pi}{2}\left(\ln 2-\ln \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {I}=\frac{\pi}{4}(\ln 2+\ln 2) \left\{\because \ln \frac{1}{x}=-\ln x\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {I}=\frac{\pi}{2} \ln 2{/tex}

1687002.explanation:
Given that {tex}\vec{a}=a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k}{/tex}
Since, {tex}a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}{/tex} are fixed number so we can assume as {tex}\left|a_{1}\right| \leq\left|a_{2}\right| \leq\left|a_{3}\right|{/tex}
Now, {tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+a_{3}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^{2}=a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+a_{3}^{2} \geq a_{3}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{a}| \geq\left|a_{3}\right|{/tex} which is maximum of {tex}\left|a_{1}\right|,\left|a_{2}\right|,\left|a_{3}\right|{/tex} so {tex}|\vec{a}| \geq \max \left\{\left|a_{1}\right|,\left|a_{2}\right|,\left|a_{3}\right|\right\} \Rightarrow {A}{/tex} is true
Again, {tex}|\vec{a}|^{2}=a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+a_{3}^{2} \leq a_{3}^{2}+a_{3}^{2}+a_{3}^{2}=3 a_{3}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{a}| \leq \sqrt{3}\left|a_{3}\right|{/tex} and {tex}\left|a_{3}\right|{/tex} is maximum of {tex}\left|a_{1}\right|,\left|a_{2}\right|{/tex} & {tex}\left|a_{13}\right|{/tex}
so {tex}|\vec{a}| \leq \sqrt{3} \max \left\{\left|{a}_{1}\right|,\left|{a}_{2}\right|,\left|{a}_{3}\right|\right\}{/tex}
or {tex}|\vec{a}| \leq 3 \max \left\{\left|a_{1}\right|,\left|a_{2}\right|,\left|a_{3}\right|\right\} \Rightarrow {B}{/tex} is true.

1687012.explanation:
Given: A is symmetric matrix and B, C are skew symmetric matrices
{tex}\Rightarrow {A}^{{T}}={A}, {B}^{{T}}=-{B}, {C}^{{T}}=-{C}{/tex}
Statement 1: {tex}A^{13} B^{26}-B^{26} A^{13}{/tex} is symmetric
Now, {tex}\left(A^{13} B^{26}-B^{26} A^{13}\right)^{T}=\left(A^{13} B^{26}\right)^{T}-\left(B^{26} A^{13}\right)^{T}{/tex}
{tex}=\left({B}^{26}\right)^{{T}}\left({A}^{13}\right)^{{T}}-\left({A}^{13}\right)^{{T}}\left({B}^{26}\right)^{{T}}{/tex} {tex} \left\{\because({MN})^{{T}}={N}^{{T}} {M}^{{T}}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(B^{T}\right)^{26}\left(A^{T}\right)^{13}-\left(A^{T}\right)^{13}\left(B^{T}\right)^{26}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(-B^{T}\right)^{26}(A)^{13}-(A)^{13}(-B)^{26}{/tex}
{tex}={B}^{26} {~A}^{13}-{A}^{13} {~B}^{26}{/tex}
{tex}=-\left(A^{13} B^{26}-B^{26} A^{13}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore A^{13} B^{26}-B^{26} A^{13}{/tex} is not a symmetric matrix.
Statement 2: {tex}A^{26} C^{13}-C^{13} A^{26}{/tex} is symmetric
Now, {tex}\left(A^{26} C^{13}-C^{13} A^{26}\right)^{T}=\left(A^{26} C^{13}\right)^{T}-\left(C^{13} A^{26}\right)^{T}{/tex}
{tex}=\left({C}^{13}\right)^{{T}}\left({A}^{26}\right)^{{T}}-\left({A}^{26}\right)^{{T}}\left({C}^{13}\right)^{{T}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(C^{T}\right)^{13}\left(A^{T}\right)^{26}-\left(A^{T}\right)^{26}\left(C^{T}\right)^{13}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(-{C}^{{T}}\right)^{13}({~A})^{26}-({A})^{26}(-{C})^{13}{/tex}
{tex}=-{C}^{13} {~A}^{26}+{A}^{26} {C}^{13}{/tex}
{tex}={A}^{26} {C}^{13}-{C}^{13} {~A}^{26}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {A}^{26} {~B}^{13}-{C}^{13} {~A}^{26}{/tex} is a symmetric matrix.

1687013.explanation:
Given lines are {tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-3}{/tex}
{tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+8}{-7}=\frac{z-4}{5}{/tex}
then {tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} can be written as
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex}
and {tex}{L}_{2}{/tex} can be written as
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}){/tex}
As we know distance between two skew lines
{tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{p}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{b}+\mu \vec{q}{/tex} is given by
{tex}{D}=\left|\frac{(\vec{b}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{p} \times \vec{q})}{|\vec{p} \times \vec{q}|}\right|{/tex}
So, {tex}\vec{b}-\vec{a}=(\hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})-(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{p} \times \vec{q}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 2 & -7 & 5\end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=-16 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}-16 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}=-16(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
And {tex}|\vec{p} \times \vec{q}|=16 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
Now, {tex}(\vec{b}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{p} \times \vec{q})=160+32=192{/tex}
So, S.D. {tex}=\left|\frac{192}{16 \sqrt{3}}\right|=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex}

1687023.explanation:
The given lines are:
{tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x-5}{1}=\frac{y-2}{2}=\frac{z-4}{-3}{/tex}
{tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x+3}{1}=\frac{y+5}{4}=\frac{z-1}{-5}{/tex}
For {tex}{L}_{1}: \vec{a}_{1}=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k} ; \vec{r}_{1}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}
For {tex}{L}_{2}: \vec{a}_{2}=-3 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k} ; \vec{r}_{2}=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex}
Hence, the vector product of {tex}\vec{r}_{1} \& \vec{r}_{2}{/tex} is:
{tex}\vec{r}_{1} \times \vec{r}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 1 & 4 & -5\end{array}\right|{/tex}
Expanding along {tex}{R}_{1}{/tex}, we get
{tex}\vec{r}_{1} \times \vec{r}_{2}=\hat{i}(-10+12)-\hat{j}(-5+3)+\hat{k}(4-2){/tex}
{tex}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Hence, the shortest distance between the given lines is
{tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\vec{r}_{1} \times \vec{r}_{2}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{a}_{1}-\vec{a}_{2}\right)}{\left|\vec{r}_{1} \times \vec{r}_{2}\right|}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\left|\frac{(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(8 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{2^{2}+2^{2}+2^{2}}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\left|\frac{16+14+6}{\sqrt{12}}\right|=\frac{36}{2 \sqrt{3}}=6 \sqrt{3}{/tex}

1687025.explanation:
Given that {tex}x^{2}-p x+\frac{5 p}{4}=0{/tex} have rational roots
{tex}\therefore {D}{/tex} is a perfect square
{tex}\Rightarrow p^{2}-4(1) \frac{5}{4} p=p^{2}-5 p=p(p-5){/tex}
For perfect square {tex}p=0, p=5{/tex} and {tex}p=9{/tex}
Maximum integral value of {tex}p{/tex} is 9.
{tex}q=9{/tex}
{tex}\left.\therefore(x, y) ; 0 \leq y \leq(x-9)^{2}, 0 \leq x \leq 9\right\}{/tex}

Area {tex}=\int_{0}^{9}(x-9)^{2} d x=\left[\frac{(x-9)^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{9}{/tex}
{tex}=\left|0+\frac{9 \times 9 \times 9}{3}\right|=243{/tex}

1687029.explanation:
Given, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}(1+|\cos x|)^{\frac{\lambda}{|\cos x|}} & ; 0 \lt x \lt \frac{\pi}{2} \\ \mu & ; x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ e^{\frac{\cot 6 x}{\cot 4 x}} & ; \frac{\pi}{2} \lt x \lt \pi\end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\because f(x){/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{+}} f(x)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
Now, {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{-}}{2}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{+}}{2}}\left(1+|\cos x|^{\left(\frac{\lambda}{|\cos x|}\right)}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Above limit has {tex}1^{\infty}{/tex} indeterminate form.
{tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{-}}{2}} f(x)=e^{{ll}\lim x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{-}}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{-}}{2}} f(x)=e^{\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{-}}{2}} \lambda e^{\lambda}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{-}}{2}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{+}}{2}} e^{\frac{\cot 6 x}{\cot 4 x}}{/tex}
Let {tex}{A}=\frac{\cot 6 x}{\cot 4 x}{/tex}
So, {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{+}}{2}} {~A}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{+}}{2}} \frac{\tan 4 x}{\tan 6 x}\left(\frac{0}{0}\right.{/tex} form{tex}){/tex}
{tex}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{+}}{2}} \frac{\left(\sec ^{2} 4 x\right) 4}{\left(\sec ^{2} 6 x\right) 6}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
So, {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{+}}{2}} f(x)=e^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore e^{\lambda}=e^{\frac{2}{3}}=\mu{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\mu=e^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}9 \lambda+6 \log _{e} \mu+\mu^{6}-e^{6 \lambda}{/tex}
{tex}=9\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)+6 \log _{e} e^{\frac{2}{3}}+\left(e^{\frac{2}{3}}\right)^{6}-e^{6\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=6+6 \times \frac{2}{3}=6+4=10{/tex}

1687035.explanation:
Given: Equation of hyperbola is
{tex}16 x^{2}-y^{2}+64 x+4 y+44=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 16\left(x^{2}+4 x\right)-\left(y^{2}-4 y\right)+44=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 16\left[(x+2)^{2}-4\right]-\left[(y-2)^{2}\right]+4+44=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 16(x+2)^{2}-(y-2)^{2}=16{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(x+2)^{2}}{1}-\frac{(y-2)^{2}}{4^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Transverse axis T: {tex}y=2{/tex}
And conjugate axis C : {tex}x=-2{/tex}
Now, given equation of parabola is {tex}x^{2}=y+4{/tex}

Now, required area {tex}A=\int_{-2}^{\sqrt{6}}\left[2-\left(x^{2}-4\right)\right] d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {A}=\left[6 x-\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{-2}^{\sqrt{6}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A=\left[6 \sqrt{6}-\frac{6 \sqrt{6}}{3}\right]-\left[-12+\frac{8}{3}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A=\frac{12 \sqrt{6}}{3}+\frac{28}{3}=4 \sqrt{6}+\frac{28}{3}{/tex}

1687040.explanation:
Given that
{tex}x\left(\log _{{e}} x\right) f^{\prime}(x)+\left(\log _{{e}} x\right) f(x)+f(x) \geq 1, x \in[2,4]{/tex}
or {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+(\log x+1) y \geq 1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(y \cdot x \log x) \geq 1=\frac{d}{d x}(x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}(y \cdot x \log x-x) \geq 0, x \in[2,4]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}y \cdot x \log x-x={g}(x){/tex} (say) is increasing in [2,4]
or {tex}{g}(x)=x \log x f(x)-x{/tex} is increasing in {tex}[2,4]{/tex}
{tex}g(2)=2 \log 2 f(2)-2=\log 2-2{/tex}
and {tex}g(4)=4 \log 4 f(4)-4=\log 4-4{/tex}
Since, {tex}g(x){/tex} is increasing
Therefore, {tex}g(2) \leq g(x) \leq g(4){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log _{e} 2-2 \leq x \log _{{e}} x f(x)-x \leq \log 4-4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log _{e} 2-2 \leq x \log _{{e}} x f(x)-x \leq 2\left(\log _{e} 2-2\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x+\log _{e} 2-2}{x \log _{e} x} \leq f(x) \leq \frac{2\left(\log _{e} 2-2\right)+x}{x \log _{e} x}{/tex}
Now, for {tex}x \in[2,4]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2(\log 2-2)}{x \log _{e} x}+\frac{1}{\log x} \lt \frac{2(\log 2-2)}{2 \log _{e} 2}+\frac{1}{\log _{e} 2}{/tex}
{tex}=1-\frac{1}{\log _{e} 2} \lt 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x) \leq 1 \forall x \in[2,4]{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{\log 2-2}{x \log _{e} x}+\frac{1}{\log _{e} x} \geq \frac{\log _{e} 2-2}{4 \log _{e} 4}+\frac{1}{\log _{e} 4}=\frac{1}{8}+{/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{2 \log _{e} 2} \geq \frac{1}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x) \geq \frac{1}{8} \forall x \in[2,4]{/tex}
Hence, both the statements are true.

1687047.explanation:
Given : {tex}{A}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}\frac{1}{\sqrt{10}} & \frac{3}{\sqrt{10}} \\ \frac{-3}{\sqrt{10}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}\end{array}\right], {B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -i \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
And {tex}M=A^{T} B A{/tex}
Now, {tex}M^{2}=\left(A^{T} B A\right)\left(A^{T} B A\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow M^{2}=A^{T} B I B A{/tex} {tex}\left\{\because {AA}^{{T}}={I}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow M^{2}=A^{T} B^{2} A{/tex}
Similarly {tex}M^{3}=\left(A^{T} B^{2} A\right)\left(A^{T} B A\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow M^{3}=A^{T} B^{3} A{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {M}^{2023}={A}^{{T}} {B}^{2023} {~A}{/tex}
Let {tex}{P}={AM}^{2023} {~A}^{{T}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {P}={AA}^{{T}} {B}^{2023} {AA}^{{T}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {P}={B}^{2023} \left\{\because {AA}^{{T}}={A}^{{T}} {A}={I}\right\}{/tex}
Now, {tex}{B}^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -i \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -i \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {B}^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -2 i \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}{B}^{3}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -2 i \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -i \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -3 i \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {B}^{2023}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -2023 i \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}{P}^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & 2023 i \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}

1687057.explanation:
Given that {tex}g(x)=f(x)+f(1-x){/tex}
and {tex}f^{n}(x) \gt 0, \forall x \in(0,1){/tex}
So {tex}g^{\prime}(x)=f^{\prime}(x)-f^{\prime}(1-x){/tex}
and {tex}g^{\prime \prime}(x)=f^{\prime \prime}(x)+f^{\prime \prime}(1-x) \gt 0 \forall x \in(0,1){/tex}
Also for maxima or minima, putting {tex}g^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=f^{\prime}(1-x) \Rightarrow x=1-x{/tex}
or {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
and {tex}g^{\prime \prime}(x) \gt 0 \forall x \in(0,1){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow g{/tex} is concave up and also {tex}\alpha=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\tan ^{-1}(2 \alpha)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\alpha+1}{\alpha}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}(1)+\tan ^{-1}(3){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{1-3}\right)=\tan ^{-1}(-2){/tex}
(Note: There is some error in the question, that's why answer is not matching)

1687080.explanation:
Given, {tex}f(x)=\frac{x^{2}+2 x+1}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)+2 x}{x^{2}+1} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=1+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
Now, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0+\frac{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)-2 x(2 x)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{2 x^{2}+2-4 x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=-\frac{(x+1)(x-1)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}f(1)=\frac{1+2+1}{1+1}=2{/tex}

{tex}f(-1)=\frac{1-2+1}{1+1}=0{/tex}

So, {tex}f(x){/tex} is one-one in {tex}[1, \infty){/tex} but not in {tex}(-\infty, \infty){/tex}

1687083.explanation:
{tex}x^{2} f(x)-x=4 \int_{0}^{x} t f(t) d t{/tex}
Differentiating with respect to {tex}x{/tex}, we get
{tex}x^{2} f(x)+2 x f(x)-1=4 x f(x) \times 1-0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} f(x)-2 x f(x)-1=0{/tex}
or {tex}f^{\prime}(x)-\frac{2}{x} f(x)=\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{2}{x} y=\frac{1}{x^{2}} \left(\right.{/tex}as {tex}y=f(x){/tex} and {tex}\left.\frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}(x)\right){/tex}
which is a linear differential equation where
{tex}{P}=\frac{-2}{x}, {Q}=\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I.F. {tex}=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \frac{-2}{x} d x}=e^{-2 \log x}=\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
So, the required solution is
{tex}y \times{/tex} I.F. {tex}=\int {Q} \times{/tex} I.F {tex}d x+{C}{/tex}
or {tex}y \times \frac{1}{x^{2}}=\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x+{C}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{y}{x^{2}}=\int \frac{1}{x^{4}} d x+{C}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{y}{x^{2}}=-\frac{1}{3 x^{3}}+{C}{/tex}
or {tex}f(x)=-\frac{1}{3 x}+{C} x^{2}{/tex}
but {tex}f(1)=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{3}=-\frac{1}{3}+{C} \times 1^{2} \Rightarrow {C}=1{/tex}
so {tex}f(x)=-\frac{1}{3 x}+x^{2}{/tex}
and {tex}f(3)=-\frac{1}{9}+9=+\frac{80}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 18 f(3)=\frac{18 \times 80}{9}=160{/tex}

1687086.explanation:
Given, {tex}f(x)=\max \left\{x^{2}, 1+[x]\right\}, x \in[0,2]{/tex}, where [.] {tex}\equiv{/tex} GIF

So, {tex}f(x)= \begin{cases}1+[x], & x \in[0, \sqrt{2}] \\ x^{2} & x \in[\sqrt{2}, 2]\end{cases}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{2} f(x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\sqrt{2}}(1+[x]) d x+\int_{\sqrt{2}}^{2} x^{2} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{1}(1+0) d x+\int_{1}^{\sqrt{2}}(1+1) d x+\left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{\sqrt{2}}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{2} f(x) d x=[x]_{0}^{1}+[2 x]_{1}^{\sqrt{2}}+\left(\frac{8}{3}-\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{2} f(x) d x=(1-0)+2(\sqrt{2}-1)+\frac{1}{3}(8-2 \sqrt{2}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5+4 \sqrt{2}}{3}{/tex}

1687099.explanation:
Given differential equation is,
{tex}y(x+1) d x-x^{2} d y=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y(x+1) d x=x^{2} d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{x+1}{x^{2}}\right) d x=\frac{d y}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x=\frac{d y}{y}{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x=\int \frac{1}{y} d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ln x-\frac{1}{x}=\ln y+c{/tex}
Also, {tex}y(1)=e{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ln (1)-\frac{1}{1}=\ln (e)+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow c=-2{/tex}
So, {tex}\ln (y)=\ln (x)-\frac{1}{x}+2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=2-\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=e^{\left(2-\frac{1}{x}\right)} \Rightarrow y=x e^{\left(2-\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} y{/tex}
{tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} x e^{\left(2-\frac{1}{x}\right)}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} e^{2}\left(\frac{x}{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=0{/tex}, as {tex}x \rightarrow 0^{+}, e^{\frac{1}{x}} \rightarrow \infty{/tex}

1687101.explanation:
Since, Augmented matrix of given system of equation can be written as
{tex}[A: B]=\left[\begin{array}{ccccc}2 & -1 & 3 & : & 5 \\ 3 & 2 & -1 & : & 7 \\ 4 & 5 & \alpha & : & \beta\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Applying {tex}{R}_{1} \rightarrow {R}_{1} / 2{/tex}
{tex}[A: B] \sim\left[\begin{array}{ccccc}1 & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{3}{2} & : & \frac{5}{2} \\ 3 & 2 & -1 & : & 7 \\ 4 & 5 & \alpha & : & \beta\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Applying {tex}{R}_{2} \rightarrow {R}_{2}-3 {R}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{3} \rightarrow {R}_{2}-4 {R}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow[A: B] \sim\left[\begin{array}{ccccc}1 & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{3}{2} & : & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{7}{2} & \frac{-11}{2} & : & \frac{-1}{2} \\ 0 & 7 & \alpha-6 & : & \beta-10\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Applying {tex}{R}_{3} \rightarrow {R}_{3}-2 {R}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow[A: B] \sim\left[\begin{array}{ccccc}1 & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{3}{2} & : & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{7}{2} & \frac{-11}{2} & : & \frac{-1}{2} \\ 0 & 0 & \alpha+5 & : & \beta-9\end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, the given system of equation have
(i) unique solution, if
Rank of {tex}{A}=\operatorname{Rank}{/tex} of {tex}[{A}: {B}]=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha \neq-5{/tex} and {tex}\beta \in {R}{/tex}
(ii) Infinite solution, if
Rank of {tex}{A}=\operatorname{Rank}{/tex} of {tex}[{A}: {B}] \lt 3{/tex} i.e., 2
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=-5{/tex} and {tex}\beta=9{/tex}
(iii) Inconsistent, if
Rank of A < Rank of [A : B]
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=-5{/tex} and {tex}\beta \neq 9{/tex}

1687110.explanation:
Given, {tex}(\alpha \beta \gamma)\left[\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 10 & 8 \\ 9 & 3 & 8 \\ 8 & 4 & 8\end{array}\right]=(0,0,0){/tex}
{tex}2 \alpha+9 \beta+8 \gamma=0{/tex} ...(1)
{tex}10 \alpha+3 \beta+4 \gamma=0{/tex} ...(2)
{tex}8 \alpha+8 \beta+8 \gamma=0{/tex} ...(3)
Given equation of plane
{tex}2 x+4 y+3 z=5{/tex} ...(4)
We use cross multiplication method (1) and (2)
{tex}2 \alpha+9 \beta+8 \gamma=0{/tex}
{tex}10 \alpha+3 \beta+4 \gamma=0{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\alpha}{36-24}=\frac{-\beta}{8-80}=\frac{+\gamma}{6-90}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\alpha}{12}=\frac{-\beta}{-72}=\frac{\gamma}{-84} {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\alpha}{1}=\frac{\beta}{6}=\frac{\gamma}{-7}=k{/tex}, (say)
{tex}\alpha=k\ \ \beta=6 k\ \ \gamma=-7 k{/tex} which lies an the plane
{tex}2 k+4 \times 6 k-7 \times 3 k=5{/tex}
{tex}2 k+24 k-21 k=5 \therefore 5 k=5 \therefore k=1{/tex}
Hence {tex}\alpha=1, \beta=6, \gamma=-7{/tex}
Then the value of {tex}6 \alpha+9 \beta+7 \gamma=6+9 \times 6-7 \times 7{/tex}
{tex}=6+54-49=60-49=11{/tex}

1687167.explanation:
Given shortest distances between lines L and {tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} is {tex}2 \sqrt{6}{/tex}
{tex}{L}: \frac{x-5}{-2}=\frac{y-\lambda}{0}=\frac{z+\lambda}{1}, \lambda \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-4}{-1}{/tex},
Let {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=\langle-2,0,1\rangle \vec{b}_{1}=\langle 5, \lambda,-\lambda\rangle{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}_{2}=\langle 1,1,-1\rangle \vec{b}_{2}=\langle-1,1,4\rangle{/tex}
Normal vector of both lines is
{tex}\vec{a}_{1} \times \vec{a}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{i}(0-1)-\hat{j}(2-1)+\hat{k}(-2-0){/tex}
{tex}=-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}=\langle-1,-1,-2\rangle{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}_{1}-\vec{b}_{2}= \lt 6, \lambda-1,-\lambda-4 \gt {/tex}
Shortest distance {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\vec{b}_{1}-\vec{b}_{2}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{a}_{1} \times \vec{a}_{2}\right)}{\left|\vec{a}_{1} \times \vec{a}_{2}\right|}\right|{/tex}
{tex}2 \sqrt{6}=\left|\frac{\langle 6, \lambda-1,-\lambda-4\rangle \times\langle-1,-1,-2\rangle}{\sqrt{1+1+4}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 12=|-6-\lambda+1+2 \lambda+8|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\lambda+3|=12{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=9,-15{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=9,(\lambda \geq 0){/tex}
{tex}\because(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} lies on line L then
{tex}\frac{\alpha-5}{-2}=\frac{\beta-9}{0}=\frac{\gamma+9}{1}=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=5-2 k, \beta=9, \gamma=-9+k{/tex}
{tex}\alpha+2 \gamma=5-2 k-18+2 k=-13 \neq 24{/tex}
Therefore {tex}\alpha+2 \gamma=24{/tex} is not possible

1687209.explanation:
Given that A is a matrix of order {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} and {tex}|{A}|=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|3 {~A}|=3^{3}|{~A}|{/tex}
{tex}\left(\because|k {~A}|=k^{n}|{~A}|\right){/tex}
{tex}=3^{3} \times 2{/tex}
Now adj {tex}\left(|3 {~A}| {A}^{2}\right)=\operatorname{adj}\left(3^{3} \times 2 {~A}^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(3^{3} \times 2\right)^{3-1}(\operatorname{adj} A)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=3^{6} \times 2^{2}(\operatorname{adj} {~A})^{2}{/tex}
and {tex}\left|3 \operatorname{adj}\left(|3 {~A}| {A}^{2}\right)\right|=\left|3 \times 3^{6} \times 2^{2}(\operatorname{adj} {~A})^{2}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\left(3^{7} \times 2^{2}\right)^{3}|\operatorname{adj} {~A}|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(3^{7} \times 2^{2}\right)^{3}\left(|{~A}|^{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=3^{21} \times 2^{6} \times\left(2^{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=3^{11} \times 6^{10}{/tex}

1687314.explanation:
Given, {tex}f(x)=\int_{0}^{2} e^{|x-t|} d t{/tex}
Case-1: When {tex}x \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}f(x)=\int_{0}^{2} e^{-(x-t)} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\int_{0}^{2} e^{-x} \cdot e^{t} d t \Rightarrow f(x)=e^{-x}\left[e^{t}\right]_{0}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=e^{-x}\left(e^{2}-1\right){/tex}
Case-2: When {tex}x \in[0,2]{/tex}
{tex}f(x)=\int_{0}^{2} e^{|x-t|} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\int_{0}^{x} e^{x-t} d t+\int_{x}^{2} e^{-(x-t)} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=e^{x}\left[-e^{-t}\right]_{0}^{x}+e^{-x}\left[e^{t}\right]_{x}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=e^{x}\left[-e^{-x}+1\right]+e^{-x}\left[e^{2}-e^{x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=e^{x}+e^{2-x}-2{/tex}
Case-3: When {tex}x \gt 2{/tex}
{tex}f(x)=\int_{0}^{2} e^{x-t} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=e^{x}\left[-e^{-t}\right]_{0}^{2} \Rightarrow f(x)=e^{x}\left[1-e^{-2}\right]{/tex}
So, {tex}[f(x)]_{\min }=e^{2}-1 ; x \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}=2(e-1) ; x \in[0,2]=e^{2}-1 ; x \gt 2{/tex}
So, minimum value of {tex}f(x)=2(e-1){/tex}

1728511.explanation: The area of a triangle with vertices (x1,y1), (x2,y2), and (x3,y3) is given by the formula: Area=12∣∣x1(y2−y3)+x2(y3−y1)+x3(y1−y2)∣∣ Substituting the given points (4,−1), (1,k), and (−2,−1): Area=12|4(k+1)+1(0)+(−2)(−1−k)|=12|6k+6|=3|k+1| Setting the area equal to 9: 3|k+1|=9⟹|k+1|=3⟹k+1=±3⟹k=2 or k=−4 Among the given options, only k=2 is present.
